gene_description: ATP-binding cassette sub-family B member 1 — encodes P-glycoprotein (MDR1), a critical efflux transporter at blood-brain barrier, gut, liver, and kidney that limits oral bioavailability and CNS penetration of many drugs

source.title: PharmGKB summary: very important pharmacogene information for ABCB1
source.finding: ABCB1 C3435T (rs1045642) TT genotype is associated with reduced P-glycoprotein expression and function, leading to higher systemic and CNS drug levels for P-gp substrates including opioids, digoxin, dabigatran, and cannabinoids.

TT.effect: Homozygous 3435T carriers have significantly lower P-gp expression in duodenum and at the blood-brain barrier. Higher oral bioavailability and CNS penetration of P-gp substrates. Increased digoxin plasma levels, greater opioid sensitivity, and higher dabigatran exposure reported.
TT.recommendation: Use lower starting doses for narrow-therapeutic-index P-gp substrates (digoxin, dabigatran). Monitor closely for opioid over-sedation. Consider dose reduction for fentanyl/morphine/methadone if excessive response observed.

TC.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate P-gp expression and drug transport activity. Modest increases in substrate bioavailability compared to CC wild-type.
TC.recommendation: Standard dosing generally appropriate. Monitor for adverse effects with narrow-therapeutic-index P-gp substrates. Be vigilant when combining multiple P-gp substrates or inhibitors.

CT.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate P-gp expression and drug transport activity. Modest increases in substrate bioavailability compared to CC wild-type.
CT.recommendation: Standard dosing generally appropriate. Monitor for adverse effects with narrow-therapeutic-index P-gp substrates. Be vigilant when combining multiple P-gp substrates or inhibitors.

CC.effect: Wild-type with full P-gp expression and efflux activity. Normal drug transport at intestinal, hepatic, renal, and blood-brain barriers.
CC.recommendation: Standard dosing per prescribing guidelines. No P-gp-related dose adjustments needed.

gene_description: ATP-binding cassette sub-family G member 2 — encodes BCRP (Breast Cancer Resistance Protein), an efflux transporter in intestine, liver, kidney, and blood-brain barrier that controls bioavailability of statins, urate-lowering drugs, and many oncology agents

source.title: The Association between ABCG2 421C>A (rs2231142) Polymorphism and Rosuvastatin Pharmacokinetics: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
source.finding: ABCG2 Q141K (rs2231142) significantly increases rosuvastatin AUC and Cmax in a gene-dose dependent manner. Homozygous TT carriers show ~2.4-fold higher rosuvastatin exposure. CPIC recommends initial dose ≤20mg for homozygous Q141K carriers.

TT.effect: Homozygous Q141K carriers have markedly reduced BCRP efflux activity. Rosuvastatin AUC increased ~2.4-fold, with significantly higher Cmax. Increased risk of statin-associated musculoskeletal symptoms (SAMS). For allopurinol, DPWG recommends 1.4-fold higher dose due to association with decreased efficacy. Also affects sulfasalazine bioavailability.
TT.recommendation: Rosuvastatin: use initial dose ≤20mg per CPIC guideline. Consider alternative statin (pravastatin, fluvastatin) if myopathy occurs. Allopurinol: consider 1.4x higher dose per DPWG. Monitor uric acid levels closely. Monitor for statin side effects including myalgia.

GT.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate BCRP activity. Rosuvastatin AUC increased ~1.7-fold. Modest increase in statin exposure with slightly elevated risk of myopathy. For allopurinol, DPWG recommends 1.25-fold higher dose.
GT.recommendation: Rosuvastatin: standard dosing acceptable but monitor for myopathy. Consider dose cap at 20mg if adverse effects emerge. Allopurinol: consider 1.25x dose adjustment per DPWG if target uric acid not reached.

TG.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate BCRP activity. Rosuvastatin AUC increased ~1.7-fold. Modest increase in statin exposure with slightly elevated risk of myopathy. For allopurinol, DPWG recommends 1.25-fold higher dose.
TG.recommendation: Rosuvastatin: standard dosing acceptable but monitor for myopathy. Consider dose cap at 20mg if adverse effects emerge. Allopurinol: consider 1.25x dose adjustment per DPWG if target uric acid not reached.

GG.effect: Wild-type with full BCRP efflux transporter activity. Normal rosuvastatin pharmacokinetics and standard allopurinol response. Normal intestinal and hepatic drug efflux.
GG.recommendation: Standard dosing for rosuvastatin and allopurinol. No BCRP-related dose adjustments needed.

title: ABO rs505922 Pancreatic Cancer Risk (Blood Type Locus)
description: Common variant at the ABO locus defining blood group phenotype. The C allele (non-O blood type) is associated with increased pancreatic cancer risk compared to T allele (type O). Blood group antigen expression on pancreatic ductal epithelium may facilitate tumor immune escape.

source.title: Genome-wide association study identifies variants in the ABO locus associated with susceptibility to pancreatic cancer

TT.effect: baseline_pancreatic_cancer_risk
TT.recommendation: standard_surveillance

TC.effect: 1.2x_increased_pancreatic_cancer_risk
TC.recommendation: enhanced_surveillance_gemcitabine_preferred

CT.effect: 1.2x_increased_pancreatic_cancer_risk
CT.recommendation: enhanced_surveillance_gemcitabine_preferred

CC.effect: 1.3-1.4x_increased_pancreatic_cancer_risk
CC.recommendation: aggressive_surveillance_combination_therapy

gene_description: Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme — cleaves angiotensin I to form angiotensin II. Insertion/deletion polymorphism (I/D) in intron 16 affects ACE expression levels and response to ACE inhibitor therapy.

source.title: Insertion/deletion polymorphism of the ACE gene and adherence to ACE inhibitors
source.finding: ACE rs4646994 (I/D polymorphism) shows inconsistent association with ACE inhibitor efficacy across studies. DD genotype associated with higher serum ACE activity (65% more than II), but effect on blood pressure response variable.

II.phenotype: Low ACE activity / favorable I/D status
II.effect: Homozygous for insertion allele. Lowest circulating ACE activity. Lower angiotensin II generation. May show greater blood pressure reduction with ACE inhibitors. Reduced cardiovascular and renal disease risk in some populations. ~18-22% frequency in Europeans.
II.recommendation: Standard ACE inhibitor dosing. May show good blood pressure response. Monitor renal function and potassium at baseline and after 2-4 weeks of therapy. Consider initial standard dosing with assessment for adequate response.

ID.effect: Heterozygous I/D genotype. Intermediate circulating ACE activity (approximately 31% higher than II genotype). Intermediate angiotensin II generation. Variable ACE inhibitor blood pressure response. Most common genotype in many populations (~50-60%).
ID.recommendation: Standard ACE inhibitor dosing. Monitor blood pressure response at 2-4 weeks. Assess renal function and potassium. Adjust dose as needed based on individual BP response and tolerability.

DI.effect: Heterozygous I/D genotype. Intermediate circulating ACE activity (approximately 31% higher than II genotype). Intermediate angiotensin II generation. Variable ACE inhibitor blood pressure response. Most common genotype in many populations (~50-60%).
DI.recommendation: Standard ACE inhibitor dosing. Monitor blood pressure response at 2-4 weeks. Assess renal function and potassium. Adjust dose as needed based on individual BP response and tolerability.

DD.phenotype: High ACE activity / possible variable response
DD.effect: Homozygous for deletion allele. Highest circulating ACE activity (approximately 65% higher than II genotype). Higher baseline angiotensin II generation. May require higher ACE inhibitor doses for equivalent BP reduction in some individuals. Associated with increased CV risk in some but not all populations. ~8-31% frequency depending on ethnicity.
DD.recommendation: Standard ACE inhibitor dosing with careful monitoring. Assess blood pressure response at 2-4 weeks; may require dose optimization. Monitor renal function and potassium carefully. May benefit from dose escalation if inadequate response. Close follow-up essential.

description: Alpha-actinin-3 protein expression and muscle fiber performance phenotypes

source.title: ACTN3 genotype is associated with human elite athletic performance
source.authors: Yang N, MacArthur DG, Gulbin JP, et al.

TT.effect: Homozygous for stop codon, complete absence of alpha-actinin-3 protein. Associated with endurance athlete phenotype, better aerobic capacity but reduced sprint/power performance.
TT.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier expressing both R577 and X577 variants. Balanced muscle fiber profile with mixed endurance and sprint capacity.
TC.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier expressing both R577 and X577 variants. Balanced muscle fiber profile with mixed endurance and sprint capacity.
CT.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

CC.effect: Homozygous for arginine at position 577, full expression of alpha-actinin-3 protein. Associated with sprint/power athlete phenotype, better fast-twitch muscle function and explosive power.
CC.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

gene_description: PACAP Receptor, Pituitary Adenylyl Cyclase Activating Peptide Type I Receptor (PAC1) — G-protein coupled receptor critical for stress response through the PACAP-PAC1 signaling pathway. The rs2267735 polymorphism is located in a putative estrogen response element that regulates ADCYAP1R1 transcription in response to estradiol, with sex-specific effects on fear conditioning and HPA axis function.

source.title: Post-traumatic stress disorder is associated with PACAP and the PAC1 receptor

CC.phenotype: Increased PTSD risk, enhanced fear response
CC.effect: Homozygous C-allele carriers show moderate-to-severe PTSD risk (approximately 1.5-2.0x risk in females). C allele reduces estrogen-responsive binding to the ERE, affecting ADCYAP1R1 expression. Associated with enhanced fear discrimination deficits, hyperarousal symptoms, heightened amygdala activation during threat processing, and altered HPA axis reactivity. Strong female sex bias in phenotypic expression.

CG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show mild-to-moderate PTSD risk elevation in females. Intermediate ADCYAP1R1 expression with mixed estrogen responsiveness. Fear conditioning response intermediate between CC and GG genotypes. Risk primarily expressed in female carriers.

GC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show mild-to-moderate PTSD risk elevation in females. Intermediate ADCYAP1R1 expression with mixed estrogen responsiveness. Fear conditioning response intermediate between CC and GG genotypes. Risk primarily expressed in female carriers.

GG.effect: Homozygous G-allele carriers serve as reference group. Efficient estrogen-responsive binding to the ERE permits normal ADCYAP1R1 expression. Standard fear conditioning and HPA axis function. No sex-specific elevation in PTSD risk.

gene_description: Alcohol dehydrogenase 1B (ADH1B) — rate-limiting enzyme in hepatic ethanol metabolism producing acetaldehyde

source.title: ADH1B is associated with alcohol dependence and alcohol consumption in populations of European and African ancestry
source.finding: rs1229984 His48 (G allele) encodes fast-metabolizing enzyme: 70-80 fold faster ethanol oxidation vs Arg48. His48 variant shows genome-wide significant protective effect for alcohol dependence.

AA.effect: Homozygous for arginine at position 48. Slow ethanol-to-acetaldehyde conversion (baseline rate). Higher risk of chronic ethanol accumulation with heavy drinking. Accelerates development of alcoholic fatty liver disease in heavy drinkers. Acetaldehyde accumulation is less pronounced than in ALDH2-deficient individuals.
AA.recommendation: Limit alcohol consumption. Heavy drinking increases risk for alcoholic liver disease, fatty liver, and alcohol dependence. Abstinence or moderate drinking (≤1 drink/day for women, ≤2 for men) strongly recommended.

AG.effect: Heterozygous for arginine/histidine. Intermediate ethanol metabolism rate (~35-40x faster than Arg/Arg, ~1/2 of His/His rate). Moderate acetaldehyde accumulation and facial flushing with alcohol. Some protective effect against alcohol dependence.
AG.recommendation: Standard alcohol consumption guidelines apply. Moderate drinking (≤1 drink/day for women, ≤2 for men) recommended. Be aware of flushing response and avoid excessive intake.

GA.effect: Heterozygous for arginine/histidine. Intermediate ethanol metabolism rate (~35-40x faster than Arg/Arg, ~1/2 of His/His rate). Moderate acetaldehyde accumulation and facial flushing with alcohol. Some protective effect against alcohol dependence.
GA.recommendation: Standard alcohol consumption guidelines apply. Moderate drinking (≤1 drink/day for women, ≤2 for men) recommended. Be aware of flushing response and avoid excessive intake.

GG.effect: Homozygous for histidine at position 48. Very rapid ethanol-to-acetaldehyde conversion (70-80 fold faster than Arg48). Rapid acetaldehyde buildup causes facial flushing, nausea, headache, and autonomic symptoms (rapid heartbeat). Strong protective effect against alcohol dependence due to unpleasant acetaldehyde effects.
GG.recommendation: Natural deterrent to excessive drinking. Standard alcohol consumption safe but expect flushing/nausea response. If alcohol consumed, do so in moderation and be prepared for acetaldehyde-related symptoms.

gene_description: Alpha-2A Adrenergic Receptor — major norepinephrine receptor in the prefrontal cortex, primary target of guanfacine and clonidine, and modulator of stimulant response in ADHD.

source.title: Review and Meta-analysis on the Impact of the ADRA2A Variant rs1800544 on Methylphenidate Outcomes in Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder
source.finding: Meta-analysis showed G-allele carriers of rs1800544 had significantly greater ADHD symptom improvement with methylphenidate (OR=3.08, 95% CI 1.71-5.56, p=0.0002) compared to non-carriers.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the C allele at the -1291 promoter position. Associated with reduced methylphenidate efficacy in ADHD. The C allele is the most common allele in European populations (~73-75%). Individuals lacking the G allele showed significantly less improvement in ADHD symptom scores in meta-analysis.
CC.recommendation: If methylphenidate response is suboptimal, consider dose increase or switching to non-stimulant alpha-2 agonists (guanfacine, clonidine) which directly target ADRA2A. As this receptor is the pharmacological target of guanfacine, genotype may also influence alpha-2 agonist response.

CG.effect: Heterozygous G-allele carrier. The G allele at -1291 is associated with significantly greater ADHD symptom improvement with methylphenidate (OR=3.08). Intermediate response expected between GG and CC genotypes.
CG.recommendation: Standard methylphenidate dosing likely effective. Monitor response during titration as enhanced efficacy may allow lower effective doses.

GC.effect: Heterozygous G-allele carrier. The G allele at -1291 is associated with significantly greater ADHD symptom improvement with methylphenidate (OR=3.08). Intermediate response expected between GG and CC genotypes.
GC.recommendation: Standard methylphenidate dosing likely effective. Monitor response during titration as enhanced efficacy may allow lower effective doses.

GG.effect: Homozygous for the G allele. Strongest association with methylphenidate efficacy in ADHD. Carriers of two G alleles showed the greatest improvement in attention and hyperactivity-impulsivity symptoms during treatment.
GG.recommendation: Methylphenidate is expected to be effective. Standard dosing per ADHD guidelines. This genotype is relatively uncommon in European populations.

gene_description: Beta-1 Adrenergic Receptor — primary target of beta-1 selective blockers (metoprolol, atenolol, bisoprolol). Arg389Gly polymorphism affects receptor signaling capacity and drug response.

source.title: Effect of the Arg389Gly β1-adrenoceptor polymorphism on plasma renin activity and heart rate, and the genotype-dependent response to metoprolol treatment
source.journal: Clinical and Experimental Pharmacology and Physiology
source.finding: ADRB1 rs1801253 Arg389Gly polymorphism predicts metoprolol response. Arg389Arg carriers show greater reductions in systolic/diastolic BP and heart rate compared to Gly389 carriers.

CC.effect: Homozygous for Arg389. Higher beta-1 receptor signaling capacity. Greater hemodynamic response to beta-blockers. Significant reductions in systolic BP (>10 mmHg), diastolic BP, and resting heart rate. Better heart rate control in acute coronary syndromes. Represents ~27-36% of Europeans.
CC.recommendation: Standard beta-blocker dosing expected to be effective. Monitor heart rate and BP response at baseline and 2-4 weeks. May require lower doses to achieve target heart rate/BP due to enhanced responsiveness.

CG.effect: Mixed Arg/Gly alleles. Intermediate beta-1 receptor signaling. Intermediate beta-blocker response. Moderate reductions in BP and heart rate. More variable individual response patterns.
CG.recommendation: Standard beta-blocker dosing with careful monitoring. Assess heart rate and BP response at 2-4 weeks and adjust dose as needed. May benefit from dose titration protocols.

GC.effect: Mixed Arg/Gly alleles. Intermediate beta-1 receptor signaling. Intermediate beta-blocker response. Moderate reductions in BP and heart rate. More variable individual response patterns.
GC.recommendation: Standard beta-blocker dosing with careful monitoring. Assess heart rate and BP response at 2-4 weeks and adjust dose as needed. May benefit from dose titration protocols.

GG.effect: Homozygous for Gly389. Lower beta-1 receptor signaling capacity. Reduced hemodynamic response to beta-blockers. Smaller reductions in BP and heart rate. May require higher doses or higher cumulative dosing to achieve target heart rate/BP. Associated with severe coronary artery stenosis in some populations.
GG.recommendation: May require higher beta-blocker doses to achieve therapeutic BP and heart rate targets. Close monitoring essential — assess response at 2-4 weeks and consider dose escalation. Alternative beta-blockers or add-on agents may be needed if targets not achieved.

gene_description: Adrenergic receptor beta 1 (ADRB1) — G protein-coupled receptor mediating sympathetic nervous system effects; rare variants associated with natural short sleep phenotype

source.title: Rare coding variants in genes encoding phospholipases associated with familial short sleep
source.finding: Rare ADRB1 mutation (A187V, c.560C>G) in familial natural short sleepers. Experimental evidence shows ADRB1+ dorsal pons neurons active during wakefulness/REM, promoting wakefulness. Affects sleep-wake state switching.

GG.effect: Homozygous for Gly allele at position 389. Associated with natural short sleep phenotype requiring only 4-6 hours sleep and feeling rested. Enhanced dopamine/norepinephrine sensitivity through ADRB1. Dorsal pons neurons more responsive to wakefulness cues. Family history of short sleep common.
GG.recommendation: No clinical action needed. Natural short sleepers show no negative health effects from reduced sleep need. Embrace natural sleep pattern. Ensure sleep quality is high. Standard health practices apply.

CG.effect: Heterozygous for Gly/Arg alleles. Intermediate sleep requirement and ADRB1 responsiveness. May have mild tendency toward shorter sleep duration.
CG.recommendation: Individual sleep need assessment recommended. Maintain consistent sleep schedule. Monitor energy levels and adjust sleep as needed.

GC.effect: Heterozygous for Gly/Arg alleles. Intermediate sleep requirement and ADRB1 responsiveness. May have mild tendency toward shorter sleep duration.
GC.recommendation: Individual sleep need assessment recommended. Maintain consistent sleep schedule. Monitor energy levels and adjust sleep as needed.

CC.effect: Homozygous for Arg allele at position 389 (wild-type). Standard sleep requirement and ADRB1 signaling. Most individuals need 7-9 hours sleep per night for optimal health.
CC.recommendation: Standard sleep hygiene recommended: 7-9 hours nightly, consistent schedule, dark cool sleep environment, no screens 1-2 hours before bed. Adequate sleep essential for health, cognition, and immune function.

gene_description: Beta-2 Adrenergic Receptor — target of beta-2 agonists in asthma and COPD. Arg16Gly polymorphism affects receptor regulation and bronchodilator responsiveness.

source.title: ADRB2 polymorphism and salbutamol responsiveness in Northern Indian children with mild to moderate exacerbation of asthma
source.finding: ADRB2 rs1042713 Arg16Gly polymorphism shows variable association with salbutamol bronchodilator response across populations. Meta-analyses show inconsistent effects on FEV1 improvement post-inhalation.

AA.effect: Homozygous for Arg16. Sustained beta-2 receptor function with repeated beta-agonist use. Better long-term bronchodilator response with salbutamol and other short-acting beta agonists. Lower risk of receptor desensitization. Represents ~42-45% of Europeans.
AA.recommendation: Standard bronchodilator dosing. Good response expected with short-acting beta-2 agonists. May show improved long-term control. LABA use as add-on controller therapy generally well-tolerated.

AG.effect: Mixed Arg16/Gly16 alleles. Intermediate beta-2 receptor regulation. Variable bronchodilator response to inhaled beta-agonists. Moderate desensitization risk with chronic LABA use. Associated with increased asthma exacerbations in some pediatric cohorts using LABA as add-on controller.
AG.recommendation: Standard bronchodilator dosing with careful monitoring. Assess inhaler technique and asthma control regularly. Monitor for loss of bronchodilator efficacy over time. Consider LABA formulation choice and monitoring intensity.

GA.effect: Mixed Arg16/Gly16 alleles. Intermediate beta-2 receptor regulation. Variable bronchodilator response to inhaled beta-agonists. Moderate desensitization risk with chronic LABA use. Associated with increased asthma exacerbations in some pediatric cohorts using LABA as add-on controller.
GA.recommendation: Standard bronchodilator dosing with careful monitoring. Assess inhaler technique and asthma control regularly. Monitor for loss of bronchodilator efficacy over time. Consider LABA formulation choice and monitoring intensity.

GG.effect: Homozygous for Gly16. Increased beta-2 receptor desensitization, particularly with chronic beta-agonist use. Reduced long-term bronchodilator efficacy. Higher risk of loss of response to LABA therapy. Associated with increased nocturnal asthma symptoms despite LABA use in some studies.
GG.recommendation: Monitor bronchodilator response closely — may show reduced efficacy over time. Consider leukotriene inhibitors or inhaled corticosteroid optimization as primary controller. LABA as add-on therapy may be less effective; ensure adequate ICS dosing. Regular asthma control assessment essential.

gene_description: Angiotensinogen — the precursor protein for angiotensin II, a potent vasoconstrictor and key regulator of blood pressure and sodium homeostasis. The M235T variant affects angiotensinogen expression levels; the T allele is associated with higher circulating angiotensinogen and increased blood pressure responses to dietary sodium.

source.title: Angiotensinogen gene polymorphism, plasma angiotensinogen, and risk of hypertension and ischemic heart disease: a meta-analysis
source.finding: Meta-analysis of 63 studies (n = 7,500) demonstrates the M235T polymorphism's association with hypertension risk. T allele associated with higher plasma angiotensinogen levels (~20% increase per T allele) and increased systolic blood pressure (~2 mmHg per T allele). TT genotype shows ~1.3-fold hypertension risk. ACE inhibitors and ARBs more effective in AGT 235T carriers due to greater renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system suppression.

TT.phenotype: AGT M235T homozygous — elevated hypertension risk
TT.effect: Homozygous for T (235T) risk allele. ~1.3-fold increased hypertension risk. 20-30% higher circulating angiotensinogen levels compared to CC carriers. Greater blood pressure sensitivity to dietary sodium. Enhanced renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system activity. Systolic blood pressure typically 2-3 mmHg higher than non-carriers. Increased vasoconstriction and sodium retention.
TT.recommendation: Sodium restriction (target <2,300 mg/day, ideally <1,500 mg/day given genetic predisposition). First-line antihypertensive: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril) or ARBs (losartan, valsartan) — particularly effective in AGT T carriers due to RAAS suppression. Thiazide diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide) also effective. Lifestyle modifications: weight loss if overweight, regular aerobic exercise (150 min/week), moderate alcohol, stress management. Monitor blood pressure regularly (home monitoring recommended). Screen for chronic kidney disease and albuminuria (RAAS activity impacts kidney). Target BP <130/80 mmHg given genetic risk.

TC.phenotype: AGT M235T heterozygous — mild hypertension risk elevation
TC.effect: Heterozygous for T allele. Mild increase in hypertension risk (intermediate between TT and CC). 10-15% higher angiotensinogen levels than CC homozygotes. Modest increased blood pressure sensitivity to dietary sodium. Intermediate RAAS activity.
TC.recommendation: Lifestyle modifications as primary prevention: weight management, sodium restriction (2,300 mg/day), regular exercise, limit alcohol, stress reduction. Monitor blood pressure periodically. If hypertension develops, ACE inhibitors or ARBs are preferred first-line agents. Maintain healthy diet rich in potassium, magnesium, and calcium.

CT.phenotype: AGT M235T heterozygous — mild hypertension risk elevation
CT.effect: Heterozygous for T allele. Mild increase in hypertension risk (intermediate between TT and CC). 10-15% higher angiotensinogen levels than CC homozygotes. Modest increased blood pressure sensitivity to dietary sodium. Intermediate RAAS activity.
CT.recommendation: Lifestyle modifications as primary prevention: weight management, sodium restriction (2,300 mg/day), regular exercise, limit alcohol, stress reduction. Monitor blood pressure periodically. If hypertension develops, ACE inhibitors or ARBs are preferred first-line agents. Maintain healthy diet rich in potassium, magnesium, and calcium.

CC.phenotype: AGT M235M homozygous wild-type — standard hypertension risk
CC.effect: Homozygous for C (235M) reference allele. Normal angiotensinogen expression and circulating levels. Standard hypertension risk determined primarily by environmental factors (dietary sodium, obesity, sedentary lifestyle, alcohol), other genetic loci, and age-related blood pressure elevation.
CC.recommendation: Standard hypertension prevention: maintain healthy weight, limit sodium intake to <2,300 mg/day, regular exercise (150 min/week moderate aerobic activity), moderate alcohol consumption, stress management. Annual blood pressure checks. If hypertension develops, any first-line antihypertensive class appropriate (ACE inhibitors, ARBs, thiazides, calcium channel blockers).

gene_description: Angiotensin II Type 1 Receptor — target of angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs). A1166C polymorphism in the 3' UTR affects receptor expression and vascular response to angiotensin II.

source.title: Association of AGTR1 A1166C and CYP2C9*3 Gene Polymorphisms with the Antihypertensive Effect of Valsartan
source.finding: AGTR1 rs5186 C allele (AC+CC genotypes) associated with better hypertension control with valsartan (OR 2.836). C allele carriers show greater BP reduction compared to AA homozygotes.

AA.effect: Homozygous for wild-type A allele. Standard AGTR1 expression and angiotensin II receptor-mediated vascular response. Standard blood pressure response to ARB therapy. Represents approximately 85% of European populations.
AA.recommendation: Standard ARB dosing (losartan, valsartan, irbesartan, or candesartan). Assess blood pressure response at 2-4 weeks. Adjust dose per clinical guidelines if targets not achieved. Monitor renal function and potassium regularly.

AC.phenotype: Heterozygous C carrier — enhanced ARB response
AC.effect: One copy of C allele. Enhanced AGTR1 expression or vascular sensitivity. Associated with greater blood pressure reduction with ARB therapy. Better hypertension control rates with valsartan (higher odds of BP target achievement). Reduced cardiovascular risk in some studies.
AC.recommendation: Standard ARB dosing expected to be effective. Good blood pressure response anticipated. Monitor at 2-4 weeks; may achieve target BP with standard dosing. Generally favorable response profile.

CA.phenotype: Heterozygous C carrier — enhanced ARB response
CA.effect: One copy of C allele. Enhanced AGTR1 expression or vascular sensitivity. Associated with greater blood pressure reduction with ARB therapy. Better hypertension control rates with valsartan (higher odds of BP target achievement). Reduced cardiovascular risk in some studies.
CA.recommendation: Standard ARB dosing expected to be effective. Good blood pressure response anticipated. Monitor at 2-4 weeks; may achieve target BP with standard dosing. Generally favorable response profile.

CC.phenotype: Homozygous C allele — enhanced ARB response
CC.effect: Homozygous for C allele (rare, ~1.4% in Europeans). Maximum AGTR1 expression. Substantially enhanced angiotensin II receptor signaling. Greatest blood pressure reduction with ARB therapy in most studies. Markedly improved hypertension control rates. May show better long-term cardiovascular outcomes.
CC.recommendation: Standard ARB dosing likely highly effective. Excellent blood pressure response anticipated. Monitor at 2-4 weeks; may achieve target BP readily. Consider standard dosing with assessment for adequate response. Follow-up as per guidelines.

gene_description: AKT Serine/Threonine Kinase 1 — key signaling molecule in the dopamine D2 receptor pathway downstream of cannabinoid receptor activation. Modulates cannabis-induced dopamine release and psychosis risk.

source.title: Confirmation that the AKT1 (rs2494732) genotype influences the risk of psychosis in cannabis users
source.finding: C/C carriers of rs2494732 with cannabis use had 2.18-fold increased risk of psychotic disorder vs T/T carriers. Among daily cannabis users, C/C genotype showed 7.23-fold increase in psychosis odds.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the C allele. In cannabis users, this genotype confers a 2.18-fold increased risk of psychotic disorder compared to T/T carriers. Among daily cannabis users, the risk escalates dramatically to 7.23-fold. The mechanism involves excess dopamine release via the AKT1-GSK3 signaling cascade upon cannabinoid receptor activation. Importantly, the variant alone does not increase psychosis risk — the effect emerges through gene-environment interaction with cannabis exposure.
CC.recommendation: Strongly counsel against recreational cannabis use. If medical cannabinoids are necessary, use CBD-only preparations when possible and avoid THC. If THC is required, use minimum effective doses with close psychiatric monitoring. Screen for psychosis risk factors before initiating any cannabinoid therapy.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate risk of cannabis-induced psychotic symptoms between CC and TT genotypes. The interaction between AKT1 genotype and cannabis frequency determines risk level — occasional use carries lower risk than daily use.
CT.recommendation: Advise caution with cannabis use, especially frequent or daily use. If medical cannabinoids are prescribed, prefer lower THC content and monitor for psychiatric symptoms. Consider CBD-dominant formulations.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate risk of cannabis-induced psychotic symptoms between CC and TT genotypes. The interaction between AKT1 genotype and cannabis frequency determines risk level — occasional use carries lower risk than daily use.
TC.recommendation: Advise caution with cannabis use, especially frequent or daily use. If medical cannabinoids are prescribed, prefer lower THC content and monitor for psychiatric symptoms. Consider CBD-dominant formulations.

TT.effect: Homozygous for the T allele. Lowest genetic risk for cannabis-induced psychosis among AKT1 genotypes. Normal AKT1-GSK3 signaling in response to cannabinoid receptor activation. Standard dopaminergic response to cannabis exposure.
TT.recommendation: Standard risk assessment for cannabinoid therapy. General precautions still apply regarding cannabis use and mental health monitoring.

gene_description: Alcohol metabolism, nitroglycerin bioactivation (Asian flush gene)

source.title: Mitochondrial ALDH2 Glu504Lys polymorphism contributes to the variation in efficacy of sublingual nitroglycerin
source.finding: ALDH2*2 (rs671 A allele) virtually eliminates nitroglycerin bioactivation to NO. 30-50% of East Asians carry this variant. Carriers show significantly reduced GTN efficacy for angina.

AA.effect: ~96% loss of ALDH2 enzyme activity. Nitroglycerin cannot be bioactivated to NO — drug is essentially ineffective. Severe alcohol flush reaction. Accumulated acetaldehyde increases esophageal cancer risk with alcohol use.
AA.recommendation: Nitroglycerin will NOT work for angina. Use alternative vasodilators (calcium channel blockers, direct NO donors). AVOID alcohol due to carcinogenic acetaldehyde accumulation.

AG.effect: ~60-80% reduced ALDH2 activity. Nitroglycerin efficacy reduced by ~33-40%. Mild-to-moderate alcohol flush. One defective subunit is sufficient to impair the ALDH2 tetramer.
AG.recommendation: Nitroglycerin may have reduced effectiveness. Consider higher dose or alternative anti-anginal. Moderate alcohol with caution.

GG.effect: Normal ALDH2 enzyme activity. Standard nitroglycerin bioactivation and efficacy. Normal alcohol metabolism.
GG.recommendation: Standard nitroglycerin dosing for angina. No ALDH2-related drug concerns.

gene_description: ALK (ALK Receptor Tyrosine Kinase) encodes a receptor tyrosine kinase. Chromosomal translocations involving ALK (most commonly EML4-ALK) create fusion oncogenes present in 2-7% of NSCLC. ALK fusions are oncogenic drivers and confer excellent sensitivity to ALK tyrosine kinase inhibitors.

source.title: Anaplastic lymphoma kinase inhibition in non-small-cell lung cancer
source.finding: Crizotinib achieved 57% response rate in ALK-positive NSCLC; established ALK as actionable target in lung cancer

TT.phenotype: Tag allele associated with ALK fusion-positive tumors
TT.effect: Genetic marker linked to ALK fusion prevalence; predicts ALK fusion status and excellent ALK inhibitor response
TT.recommendation: If ALK fusion confirmed by FISH/NGS, initiate ALK inhibitor therapy. Crizotinib first-line or next-generation ALK inhibitors (alectinib/ceritinib) for improved CNS penetration.

TC.effect: Intermediate frequency; may be associated with ALK fusion status
TC.recommendation: Perform ALK fusion testing (FISH, NGS, or immunohistochemistry). If positive, use ALK inhibitor. If negative, evaluate for other fusions (ROS1, NTRK).

CT.effect: Intermediate frequency; may be associated with ALK fusion status
CT.recommendation: Perform ALK fusion testing (FISH, NGS, or immunohistochemistry). If positive, use ALK inhibitor. If negative, evaluate for other fusions (ROS1, NTRK).

CC.effect: Lower frequency of ALK fusion association; standard treatment paradigm applies
CC.recommendation: ALK fusion testing still indicated if clinical suspicion. Evaluate for EGFR mutations, KRAS, and other drivers. Consider immunotherapy if no driver mutation.

clinical_significance: ALK fusion testing mandatory in NSCLC. Present in 2-7% of lung adenocarcinomas. Crizotinib revolutionized treatment; subsequent next-generation ALK inhibitors improved CNS penetration and overcome resistance.

gene_description: Anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) — TGF-beta superfamily cytokine produced by ovarian granulosa cells, surrogate marker for number of growing follicles and ovarian reserve

source.title: Meta-analysis of genome-wide association studies identifies common variants associated with circulating progesterone levels and explains their association with fertility
source.finding: rs10407022 T allele associated with higher serum AMH levels and more metaphase II oocytes retrieved during assisted reproduction. Effect modulated by ethnicity, with stronger effects in Asian populations.

TT.effect: Homozygous for T allele. Associated with higher circulating AMH levels, indicating larger pool of growing follicles and increased ovarian reserve. More metaphase II oocytes retrieved during fertility treatment. Correlates with later age at menopause (~1-2 years later).
TT.recommendation: Good ovarian reserve indicator. Favorable response anticipated to fertility medications if assisted reproduction needed. Standard reproductive planning applies. Monitor AMH levels periodically.

GT.effect: Heterozygous for T/G alleles. Intermediate AMH levels and ovarian reserve. Intermediate response to fertility stimulation. Normal fertility expected in most cases.
GT.recommendation: Standard reproductive assessment and planning. AMH testing recommended if evaluating fertility or approaching assisted reproduction.

TG.effect: Heterozygous for T/G alleles. Intermediate AMH levels and ovarian reserve. Intermediate response to fertility stimulation. Normal fertility expected in most cases.
TG.recommendation: Standard reproductive assessment and planning. AMH testing recommended if evaluating fertility or approaching assisted reproduction.

GG.effect: Homozygous for G allele (wild-type). Associated with lower AMH levels and reduced ovarian follicle pool. Fewer oocytes retrieved during assisted reproduction. Correlates with earlier age at menopause.
GG.recommendation: If concerned about fertility, AMH testing and ovarian reserve assessment recommended. Early oocyte or embryo preservation may be considered. Earlier reproductive planning may be prudent given smaller follicle pool.

gene_description: Apolipoprotein A-V — key regulator of plasma triglyceride levels. Modulates lipoprotein lipase activity and VLDL production. Variants significantly elevate triglycerides and metabolic syndrome risk.

source.title: Apolipoprotein A5 fifteen years anniversary: Lessons from genetic epidemiology
source.finding: APOA5 variants rs3135506 (S19W) and rs662799 (-1131T>C) are consistently associated with 15-16% higher triglyceride levels per minor allele and ~1.5-fold increased metabolic syndrome risk. Significant gene-diet interactions with dietary fat intake.

GG.effect: Homozygous S19W carriers have impaired APOA5 signal peptide processing, reducing protein secretion. Triglycerides elevated ~30% above baseline. Increased risk of metabolic syndrome, hypertriglyceridemia, and cardiovascular disease. Atherogenic LDL levels also elevated.
GG.recommendation: Strict dietary fat management — limit saturated fat, avoid trans fats. Omega-3 supplementation (2-4g EPA+DHA daily). Consider fibrate therapy if triglycerides remain >500 mg/dL. Reduce refined carbohydrates and alcohol. Regular triglyceride monitoring.

CG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show ~15% higher triglycerides. Modest increase in metabolic syndrome risk. Postprandial triglyceride clearance is somewhat delayed.
CG.recommendation: Heart-healthy diet with moderate fat intake. Regular triglyceride testing. Omega-3 rich diet (fatty fish 2-3x/week). Limit alcohol and refined sugars.

GC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show ~15% higher triglycerides. Modest increase in metabolic syndrome risk. Postprandial triglyceride clearance is somewhat delayed.
GC.recommendation: Heart-healthy diet with moderate fat intake. Regular triglyceride testing. Omega-3 rich diet (fatty fish 2-3x/week). Limit alcohol and refined sugars.

CC.effect: Wild-type with efficient APOA5 secretion and triglyceride regulation from this locus.
CC.recommendation: Standard dietary guidelines.

CC.effect: Homozygous -1131C carriers have ~50% lower APOA5 gene expression. Substantially elevated triglycerides (~32% above baseline). High metabolic syndrome risk (OR ~1.5). After Mediterranean diet intervention, these carriers show greater triglyceride reduction, indicating strong gene-diet interaction.
CC.recommendation: Mediterranean dietary pattern strongly recommended — shows greatest benefit in this genotype. Strict triglyceride management. Omega-3 supplementation. Limit refined carbohydrates and alcohol.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show ~16% higher triglycerides. Intermediate APOA5 expression. Moderate metabolic syndrome risk elevation.
TC.recommendation: Mediterranean-style diet preferred. Regular lipid monitoring. Moderate omega-3 intake.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show ~16% higher triglycerides. Intermediate APOA5 expression. Moderate metabolic syndrome risk elevation.
CT.recommendation: Mediterranean-style diet preferred. Regular lipid monitoring. Moderate omega-3 intake.

TT.effect: Wild-type with full APOA5 promoter activity and normal triglyceride regulation.
TT.recommendation: Standard dietary guidelines.

gene_description: Apolipoprotein B — primary structural protein of LDL particles (ApoB-100) and chylomicrons (ApoB-48). Essential for hepatic VLDL assembly and intestinal lipid transport. Variants affect LDL levels, abdominal obesity, and metabolic syndrome risk.

source.title: Gene-nutrient interactions and gender may modulate the association between ApoA1 and ApoB gene polymorphisms and metabolic syndrome risk
source.finding: APOB rs512535 major G allele homozygotes show increased metabolic syndrome risk through abdominal obesity and impaired insulin sensitivity, with significant gene-diet interactions — risk exacerbated by high-fat diets (>35% energy from fat), particularly in males.

GG.phenotype: Increased metabolic syndrome / abdominal obesity risk
GG.effect: Homozygous G carriers show increased metabolic syndrome risk driven by abdominal obesity and impaired insulin sensitivity. Effect is sex-specific (stronger in males). Risk exacerbated by high-fat diets (>35% energy from fat) and high monounsaturated fat intake (>14% energy). Does not directly affect dyslipidemia but influences adiposity patterns.
GG.recommendation: Limit total fat to <35% of energy intake. Reduce monounsaturated fat-heavy foods if excessive. Waist circumference monitoring. Focus on whole grains, fiber-rich foods. Males with this genotype should be especially vigilant about abdominal adiposity.

GA.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate risk profile. Mild gene-diet interaction with high-fat diets.
GA.recommendation: Balanced macronutrient distribution. Standard healthy diet with moderate fat intake.

AG.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate risk profile. Mild gene-diet interaction with high-fat diets.
AG.recommendation: Balanced macronutrient distribution. Standard healthy diet with moderate fat intake.

AA.effect: Homozygous minor allele carriers show lower metabolic syndrome risk from this locus. Less susceptible to fat-driven abdominal obesity.
AA.recommendation: Standard dietary guidelines.

gene_description: Apolipoprotein E — major apolipoprotein that mediates hepatic uptake of chylomicron remnants and VLDL. Three isoforms (ε2, ε3, ε4) defined by two SNPs create a haplotypic system affecting lipid metabolism, cardiovascular risk, and Alzheimer's disease susceptibility.

source.title: ApoE rs429358 and rs7412 Polymorphism and Gender Differences of Serum Lipid Profile and Cognition in Aging Chinese Population
source.finding: APOE ε4 carriers (rs429358-C) have significantly higher total cholesterol and LDL-C, while ε2 carriers (rs7412-T) have lower TC but higher triglycerides. ε4 is the strongest genetic risk factor for late-onset Alzheimer's disease (3-15 fold risk increase).

CC.effect: Homozygous ε4 carriers have highest LDL-C and total cholesterol levels. 10-15 fold increased risk of late-onset Alzheimer's disease with earlier age of onset (mean ~68 years). Enhanced cholesterol absorption and impaired amyloid-beta clearance. Increased cardiovascular disease risk. Higher response to dietary cholesterol and saturated fat.
CC.recommendation: Aggressive cardiovascular risk management — strict LDL-C targets. Mediterranean/MIND diet for cognitive protection. Regular cognitive screening from age 50. Statin therapy per lipid guidelines. Consider anti-amyloid therapies (lecanemab) eligibility. Limit saturated fat and dietary cholesterol. APOE genotype may affect statin response.

CT.phenotype: APOE ε3/ε4 or ε2/ε4 — elevated risk
CT.effect: Heterozygous ε4 carrier (one copy). 3-4 fold increased Alzheimer's disease risk. Elevated LDL-C and total cholesterol compared to ε3/ε3. Stronger lipid response to dietary saturated fat. Intermediate cardiovascular risk elevation.
CT.recommendation: Heart-healthy diet with reduced saturated fat. Regular lipid and cognitive monitoring. Consider MIND/Mediterranean diet. Exercise (neuroprotective). Statin therapy if LDL-C elevated. Cognitive engagement activities.

TC.phenotype: APOE ε3/ε4 or ε2/ε4 — elevated risk
TC.effect: Heterozygous ε4 carrier (one copy). 3-4 fold increased Alzheimer's disease risk. Elevated LDL-C and total cholesterol compared to ε3/ε3. Stronger lipid response to dietary saturated fat. Intermediate cardiovascular risk elevation.
TC.recommendation: Heart-healthy diet with reduced saturated fat. Regular lipid and cognitive monitoring. Consider MIND/Mediterranean diet. Exercise (neuroprotective). Statin therapy if LDL-C elevated. Cognitive engagement activities.

TT.phenotype: APOE ε2/ε2, ε2/ε3, or ε3/ε3 — standard/lower risk
TT.effect: Non-ε4 carrier. Standard or reduced Alzheimer's and cardiovascular risk from this locus. ε3/ε3 is the most common genotype (~60% of population). ε2 carriers may have lower LDL-C but potentially higher triglycerides.
TT.recommendation: Standard dietary and lifestyle guidelines. Check rs7412 to distinguish ε2 from ε3 status.

CC.phenotype: APOE ε3/ε3 or ε3/ε4 or ε4/ε4 (no ε2 allele)
CC.effect: No ε2 allele present. Standard apoE-mediated lipoprotein clearance. Lipid profile and disease risk determined primarily by rs429358 genotype (ε3 vs ε4 status).
CC.recommendation: See rs429358 interpretation for overall APOE risk assessment.

CT.phenotype: APOE ε2 carrier (one copy — ε2/ε3 or ε2/ε4)
CT.effect: One ε2 allele. Lower LDL-C than ε3/ε3 or ε4 carriers. Reduced hepatic LDL receptor binding efficiency. Mildly elevated triglycerides. Protective against Alzheimer's disease (strongest protective allele). If combined with ε4 (ε2/ε4), effects may partially cancel.
CT.recommendation: Generally favorable lipid genetics for LDL. Monitor triglycerides which may be elevated. Standard dietary guidelines.

TC.phenotype: APOE ε2 carrier (one copy — ε2/ε3 or ε2/ε4)
TC.effect: One ε2 allele. Lower LDL-C than ε3/ε3 or ε4 carriers. Reduced hepatic LDL receptor binding efficiency. Mildly elevated triglycerides. Protective against Alzheimer's disease (strongest protective allele). If combined with ε4 (ε2/ε4), effects may partially cancel.
TC.recommendation: Generally favorable lipid genetics for LDL. Monitor triglycerides which may be elevated. Standard dietary guidelines.

TT.effect: Homozygous ε2. Lowest LDL-C levels but significantly elevated triglycerides. Rare risk of type III hyperlipoproteinemia (dysbetalipoproteinemia) with high triglycerides and cholesterol if additional metabolic triggers present. Strong Alzheimer's disease protection.
TT.recommendation: Monitor triglycerides closely — risk of type III hyperlipoproteinemia. Low-fat diet may be needed if triglycerides very high. Favorable Alzheimer's risk profile. Fibrates if type III HLP develops.

gene_description: Apolipoprotein L1 — a serum protein with innate immune function against trypanosomes. Two African-ancestry-enriched variants (G1 and G2) confer strong kidney disease risk through increased cellular toxicity and podocyte injury, inherited in recessive fashion (two risk alleles required for high risk).

source.title: Association of trypanolytic ApoL1 variants with kidney disease in African Americans
source.finding: Two APOL1 variants (G1 rs73885319 and G2) show strong association with focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS, OR = 10.5, 95% CI 6.0–18.4) and hypertension-attributed end-stage kidney disease (H-ESKD, OR = 7.3, 95% CI 5.6–9.5) in African Americans. Variants show positive selection signatures and are absent from European populations. Only kidney disease-associated variants retain trypanolytic activity in vitro.

AA.phenotype: APOL1 G1/G1 homozygous — highest kidney disease risk (recessive model)
AA.effect: Homozygous for G1 risk variant. 7–10 fold increased risk of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) and hypertension-attributed ESKD in African Americans. Recessive inheritance: two risk alleles required for high penetrance. Increased podocyte toxicity, cytotoxicity, and cell death. Impaired cellular autophagy. Higher rates of FSGS, HIVAN (HIV-associated nephropathy), and ESKD progression.
AA.recommendation: Intensive renal protective therapy: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril) or ARBs (losartan, valsartan) as first-line. SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) for additional renal and cardiovascular protection. Strict blood pressure control (target <120 mmHg systolic). Avoid NSAIDs. Monitor serum creatinine and urine protein regularly (every 3–6 months). Consider nephrology referral. Screen for proteinuria and hematuria. Manage diabetes aggressively if present. Genetic counseling: high recurrence risk in family members.

AG.phenotype: APOL1 G1/G2 or G1/reference heterozygous — minimal increased risk alone (carrier status)
AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier of one G1 risk allele. In recessive inheritance model, single allele carrier has minimal increased kidney disease risk on its own. Risk significantly increases only if individual carries two risk alleles (compound heterozygous or homozygous). Population studies show heterozygotes have near-normal kidney function and disease risk.
AG.recommendation: Standard kidney health guidelines. Annual renal function monitoring (serum creatinine, eGFR). Maintain normal blood pressure. Adequate hydration. Screen family members for APOL1 status (genetic testing recommended in African Americans with kidney disease or strong family history). Lifestyle modifications: avoid smoking, limit alcohol, maintain healthy weight.

GA.phenotype: APOL1 G1/G2 or G1/reference heterozygous — minimal increased risk alone (carrier status)
GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier of one G1 risk allele. In recessive inheritance model, single allele carrier has minimal increased kidney disease risk on its own. Risk significantly increases only if individual carries two risk alleles (compound heterozygous or homozygous). Population studies show heterozygotes have near-normal kidney function and disease risk.
GA.recommendation: Standard kidney health guidelines. Annual renal function monitoring (serum creatinine, eGFR). Maintain normal blood pressure. Adequate hydration. Screen family members for APOL1 status (genetic testing recommended in African Americans with kidney disease or strong family history). Lifestyle modifications: avoid smoking, limit alcohol, maintain healthy weight.

GG.phenotype: APOL1 reference/reference homozygous — standard kidney disease risk
GG.effect: Homozygous for wild-type reference alleles. Normal APOL1 function. Kidney disease risk from this locus is minimal and determined by other genetic and environmental factors (hypertension, diabetes, obesity, smoking). Standard population kidney disease risk applies.
GG.recommendation: Standard preventive care: maintain healthy blood pressure, healthy weight, avoid smoking and excessive alcohol. Regular kidney function screening as part of routine health maintenance. Control diabetes if present. Stay well-hydrated.

title: ARID5B rs10821936 Childhood Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia Risk
description: Common variant in ARID5B (AT-rich interaction domain 5B) encoding a chromatin remodeling protein. The C allele is strongly associated with childhood B-cell precursor ALL, particularly B-hyperdiploid ALL subtype. ARID5B variants associate with methotrexate accumulation in leukemic cells, potentially affecting treatment response.

source.title: Germline genomic variants associated with childhood acute lymphoblastic leukemia

TT.effect: baseline_childhood_ALL_risk
TT.recommendation: standard_chemotherapy

TC.effect: 1.38x_increased_childhood_ALL_risk
TC.recommendation: standard_chemotherapy_methotrexate_monitoring

CT.effect: 1.38x_increased_childhood_ALL_risk
CT.recommendation: standard_chemotherapy_methotrexate_monitoring

CC.effect: 1.6x_increased_childhood_ALL_risk
CC.recommendation: enhanced_monitoring_methotrexate_dose_optimization

source.title: The H1069Q mutation in ATP7B is associated with late and neurologic presentation in Wilson disease

description: ATP7B rs76151636 (H1069Q mutation) is the most common pathogenic variant causing Wilson disease in European and Caucasian populations. It results in impaired copper transport and neuropsychiatric presentation.

CC.phenotype: No Wilson disease from this variant
CC.effect: Wildtype homozygote
CC.recommendation: Standard health maintenance; family counseling if Wilson disease in family

CA.recommendation: No treatment needed; genetic counseling if planning pregnancy; confirm compound heterozygosity status with genetic testing

AC.recommendation: No treatment needed; genetic counseling if planning pregnancy; confirm compound heterozygosity status with genetic testing

AA.effect: Pathogenic H1069Q variant carrier; manifests late and with neurologic symptoms typically age 15-45
AA.recommendation: Initiate copper chelation therapy (penicillamine or trientine); maintain low-copper diet; regular serum ceruloplasmin and copper monitoring; neurologic and ophthalmology evaluation; zinc supplementation as maintenance therapy

gene_description: Butyrylcholinesterase (Pseudocholinesterase) — serum enzyme responsible for rapid hydrolysis of succinylcholine and mivacurium. Atypical variants cause prolonged neuromuscular blockade, extending paralysis from minutes to hours.

source.title: Genetic Testing for BCHE Variants Identifies Patients at Risk of Prolonged Neuromuscular Blockade in Response to Succinylcholine
source.finding: BCHE variants reliably predict prolonged apnea after succinylcholine. Atypical variant (D70G, rs1799807) reduces activity to 41% of normal. Homozygous or compound heterozygous carriers experience paralysis lasting up to 8 hours instead of 5-10 minutes.

GG.effect: Homozygous for the atypical BCHE variant (D70G). Severely reduced butyrylcholinesterase activity (~20% of normal). Standard dose of succinylcholine will cause prolonged neuromuscular blockade lasting 2-8 hours instead of the normal 5-10 minutes, requiring prolonged mechanical ventilation. Dibucaine number typically 20-30 (normal >70). Life-threatening if unrecognized.
GG.recommendation: DO NOT USE succinylcholine or mivacurium. Use non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agents (rocuronium, vecuronium, cisatracurium) with sugammadex for reversal. Alert the anesthesia team before any procedure. Consider medical alert bracelet. Screen family members.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier of the atypical variant. BChE activity reduced to approximately 41-60% of normal. Moderately prolonged succinylcholine effect (paralysis may last 20-30 minutes instead of 5-10 minutes). Dibucaine number typically 50-65 (intermediate).
GA.recommendation: Use succinylcholine with caution and at reduced doses only when non-depolarizing agents are not suitable. Prefer rocuronium with sugammadex reversal. Inform anesthesia team of carrier status. Consider family screening.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier of the atypical variant. BChE activity reduced to approximately 41-60% of normal. Moderately prolonged succinylcholine effect (paralysis may last 20-30 minutes instead of 5-10 minutes). Dibucaine number typically 50-65 (intermediate).
AG.recommendation: Use succinylcholine with caution and at reduced doses only when non-depolarizing agents are not suitable. Prefer rocuronium with sugammadex reversal. Inform anesthesia team of carrier status. Consider family screening.

AA.effect: Wild-type BChE. Normal butyrylcholinesterase activity with typical succinylcholine metabolism. Paralysis duration 5-10 minutes as expected. Normal dibucaine number (>70).
AA.recommendation: Succinylcholine and mivacurium can be used per standard anesthetic protocols.

gene_description: Beta-carotene to vitamin A conversion

source.title: Two common SNPs in BCMO1 affect beta-carotene metabolism
source.finding: BCMO1 variants reduce beta-carotene to retinal conversion by 32-69%. Preformed vitamin A may be needed.

TT.effect: ~69% reduced beta-carotene to retinal conversion. Plant-based vitamin A sources may be insufficient.
TT.recommendation: Consider preformed vitamin A (retinol) from animal sources or supplements instead of relying on beta-carotene.

AT.recommendation: Ensure mixed sources of vitamin A. Monitor if following a plant-based diet.

AA.effect: Normal beta-carotene to retinal conversion.
AA.recommendation: Standard dietary vitamin A intake sufficient.

TT.effect: Significantly reduced beta-carotene conversion.
TT.recommendation: Preformed vitamin A recommended.

CT.effect: Moderately reduced beta-carotene conversion.
CT.recommendation: Mixed vitamin A sources recommended.

CC.effect: Normal conversion.
CC.recommendation: Standard diet sufficient.

gene_description: Brain-Derived Neurotrophic Factor — neurotrophin critical for energy homeostasis in the hypothalamus and cognitive control of eating behaviors. Variants reduce activity-induced BDNF expression, affecting appetite regulation and weight.

source.title: rs10767664 Gene Variant in Brain-Derived Neurotrophic Factor Is Associated with Diabetes Mellitus Type 2 in Caucasian Females with Obesity
source.finding: BDNF rs10767664 T allele carriers show higher BMI, increased emotional eating, and 1.33x diabetes risk adjusted for age and BMI. The variant impairs activity-induced BDNF expression and alters noradrenaline transporter availability in hypothalamus.

TT.effect: Homozygous T carriers have significantly reduced activity-induced BDNF expression. Associated with higher BMI, increased emotional eating scores, higher risk of type 2 diabetes (OR 1.33), and altered noradrenaline transporter availability in hypothalamus and locus coeruleus. After hypocaloric diet, show different metabolic response patterns.
TT.recommendation: Behavioral eating interventions focused on emotional eating patterns. Mediterranean-style hypocaloric diet shows metabolic benefits. GLP-1 agonists may provide additional appetite control. Mindful eating practices. Regular physical activity (exercise itself boosts BDNF).

AT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate BDNF expression. Mildly increased emotional eating tendency and BMI. Modest increase in metabolic risk.
AT.recommendation: Standard healthy diet with attention to emotional eating triggers. Regular exercise to boost BDNF naturally. Monitor weight and metabolic markers.

TA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate BDNF expression. Mildly increased emotional eating tendency and BMI. Modest increase in metabolic risk.
TA.recommendation: Standard healthy diet with attention to emotional eating triggers. Regular exercise to boost BDNF naturally. Monitor weight and metabolic markers.

AA.effect: Wild-type with standard BDNF expression and activity-induced regulation. Normal appetite control and energy homeostasis from this locus.
AA.recommendation: Standard dietary and lifestyle guidelines.

source.title: Association between the BDNF Val66Met polymorphism and major depressive disorder: a systematic review and meta-analysis
source.authors: Zhao et al.

AA.effect: Homozygous Met allele carriers show reduced BDNF secretion and impaired activity-dependent BDNF release. Associated with approximately 1.3-fold increased risk of major depressive disorder. Met/Met individuals may show reduced response to SSRIs but potentially enhanced response to ketamine therapy.
AA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate BDNF secretion capacity with moderate impairment of activity-dependent BDNF signaling. Associated with mild increase in depression risk and intermediate antidepressant response patterns.
AG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate BDNF secretion capacity with moderate impairment of activity-dependent BDNF signaling. Associated with mild increase in depression risk and intermediate antidepressant response patterns.
GA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GG.effect: Wild-type Val/Val genotype associated with normal BDNF secretion and activity-dependent BDNF signaling. Baseline depression risk with typical SSRI response patterns.
GG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

notes: Met allele (A) shows strong association with MDD in European populations. The Val66Met polymorphism affects activity-dependent BDNF secretion with functional consequences for synaptic plasticity and antidepressant response.

gene_description: BRAF (B-Raf Proto-Oncogene, Serine/Threonine Kinase) encodes a component of the MAPK/ERK signaling pathway. The V600E mutation is the most common BRAF alteration, occurring in ~50% of melanomas. This constitutively active mutation drives rapid tumor growth and predicts excellent response to BRAF-directed therapies.

source.title: Inhibition of mutated, activated BRAF in metastatic melanoma
source.finding: BRAF V600E inhibition with vemurafenib achieved 81% response rate in phase 1; established BRAF as druggable target in melanoma

AA.effect: Constitutively active BRAF kinase; strong MAPK pathway activation; 81% response rate to vemurafenib
AA.recommendation: BRAF inhibitor monotherapy or combined BRAF+MEK inhibition (preferred for improved PFS and OS). Monitor for secondary cutaneous malignancies.

AT.effect: BRAF V600E present; excellent response to targeted therapy expected; constitutively active kinase
AT.recommendation: BRAF+MEK inhibitor combination preferred (dabrafenib+trametinib or encorafenib+cobimetinib). Superior to monotherapy for PFS and OS.

TA.effect: BRAF V600E present; excellent response to targeted therapy expected; constitutively active kinase
TA.recommendation: BRAF+MEK inhibitor combination preferred (dabrafenib+trametinib or encorafenib+cobimetinib). Superior to monotherapy for PFS and OS.

TT.effect: BRAF V600E not present; BRAF inhibitors not indicated; consider NRAS or other mutations
TT.recommendation: Immunotherapy (anti-PD-1/PD-L1) is first-line. Consider MEK inhibitor if NRAS mutant. Evaluate for other driver mutations.

clinical_significance: FDA companion diagnostic for vemurafenib and other BRAF inhibitors. BRAF V600E present in ~50% of melanomas. Transformed melanoma treatment from chemotherapy to precision medicine.

title: BRCA1 rs1799966 Ovarian and Breast Cancer Risk
description: Common missense polymorphism in BRCA1 tumor suppressor gene. BRCA1 encodes a DNA repair protein essential for homologous recombination repair of double-strand breaks. Mutations and variants affecting BRCA1 function significantly increase risk of ovarian and breast cancer. The rs1799966 variant (Gln356Arg) represents a lower-penetrance susceptibility allele compared to pathogenic BRCA1 mutations.

source.title: BRCA1 and BRCA2 germline mutations in breast and ovarian cancer
source.journal: The American Journal of Human Genetics

AA.effect: baseline_ovarian_cancer_risk
AA.recommendation: standard_surveillance

AG.effect: modest_increased_ovarian_cancer_risk
AG.recommendation: enhanced_surveillance_BRCA_testing_consideration

GA.effect: modest_increased_ovarian_cancer_risk
GA.recommendation: enhanced_surveillance_BRCA_testing_consideration

GG.effect: increased_ovarian_and_breast_cancer_risk
GG.recommendation: aggressive_surveillance_genetic_counseling_PARP_inhibitor_awareness

gene_description: BRCA2 (BRCA2 DNA repair associated) encodes a protein crucial for homologous recombination repair. BRCA2 loss-of-function variants cause hereditary breast and ovarian cancer predisposition and sensitize tumors to PARP inhibitors.

source.title: Olaparib monotherapy in patients with advanced cancer and a germline BRCA1/2 mutation
source.finding: Olaparib showed significant efficacy in advanced BRCA1/2-mutant cancers, establishing PARP inhibitors as standard-of-care therapy

AA.effect: Complete loss of BRCA2 protein function; severe homologous recombination deficiency
AA.recommendation: Strong indication for PARP inhibitor therapy. Monitor for secondary malignancies. Carrier screening recommended for family members.

AG.effect: Partial BRCA2 function loss; increased cancer predisposition; eligible for PARP inhibitor therapy if cancer develops
AG.recommendation: Consider PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy if cancer diagnosed. Recommend genetic counseling and family screening. Enhanced surveillance protocols.

GA.effect: Partial BRCA2 function loss; increased cancer predisposition; eligible for PARP inhibitor therapy if cancer develops
GA.recommendation: Consider PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy if cancer diagnosed. Recommend genetic counseling and family screening. Enhanced surveillance protocols.

GG.effect: Normal BRCA2 protein function; standard cancer risk
GG.recommendation: PARP inhibitors not indicated based on BRCA2 status alone. Discuss other tumor characteristics and biomarkers.

clinical_significance: FDA companion diagnostic for PARP inhibitor eligibility. BRCA2 mutations confer billion-dollar market opportunity for targeted therapy.

gene_description: Chromosome 11 open reading frame 65 (ATM region) — rs11212617 is located near the ATM gene and was the first GWAS-identified locus for metformin glycemic response in type 2 diabetes; the ATM–AMPK signaling pathway is a proposed mechanism for metformin action

source.title: Common variants near ATM are associated with glycemic response to metformin in type 2 diabetes
source.finding: GWAS across GoDARTS and UKPDS cohorts identified rs11212617 (C11orf65/ATM locus) as significantly associated with metformin treatment success (achieving HbA1c <7%). The A allele was associated with better glycemic response to metformin (OR 1.35, P = 4.1×10⁻⁸).

AA.effect: Homozygous for the A allele associated with greater HbA1c reduction on metformin monotherapy. Higher probability of achieving glycemic target (HbA1c <7%) in the GoDARTS/UKPDS discovery and replication cohorts (OR 1.35 per A allele).
AA.recommendation: Metformin is an excellent first-line choice. Expect good glycemic response with standard titration. Continue routine HbA1c monitoring every 3–6 months.

AC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate glycemic response to metformin. Moderate probability of reaching target HbA1c on monotherapy.
AC.recommendation: Start metformin at standard dose with uptitration. If target HbA1c not achieved after 3–6 months of maximal tolerated dose, consider adding a second agent (SGLT2 inhibitor, GLP-1 agonist, or sulfonylurea) per guidelines.

CA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate glycemic response to metformin. Moderate probability of reaching target HbA1c on monotherapy.
CA.recommendation: Start metformin at standard dose with uptitration. If target HbA1c not achieved after 3–6 months of maximal tolerated dose, consider adding a second agent (SGLT2 inhibitor, GLP-1 agonist, or sulfonylurea) per guidelines.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the C allele associated with diminished HbA1c reduction on metformin monotherapy. Lower likelihood of achieving glycemic target on metformin alone in the GoDARTS/UKPDS cohorts.
CC.recommendation: Metformin remains appropriate first-line therapy but anticipate possible need for earlier combination therapy. If HbA1c remains above target after 3 months at maximal dose, promptly add a complementary agent. Consider SGLT2 inhibitors or GLP-1 receptor agonists which act through independent mechanisms.

source.title: Schizophrenia risk from complex variation of complement component 4
source.authors: Sekar A, Bialas AR, de Rivera H, et al.

AA.effect: AA carriers have increased C4A protein expression leading to excessive synaptic complement activity during postnatal development. Associated with approximately 1.3-fold increased schizophrenia risk. Elevated C4A drives excessive synaptic pruning, reducing synaptic density in key brain circuits involved in cognition and perception. May require atypical antipsychotics with different mechanisms.
AA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

AG.effect: AG carriers show intermediate C4A expression with moderate elevation in synaptic complement activity. Associated with mild increase in schizophrenia risk. Moderately elevated synaptic pruning affecting brain development and circuit formation.
AG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GA.effect: GA carriers show intermediate C4A expression with moderate elevation in synaptic complement activity. Associated with mild increase in schizophrenia risk. Moderately elevated synaptic pruning affecting brain development and circuit formation.
GA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GG.effect: GG carriers have normal C4A expression with baseline synaptic complement activity during development. Baseline schizophrenia risk with typical antipsychotic response patterns. Normal synaptic pruning and brain circuit development.
GG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

notes: Landmark 2016 study revealing C4A as a major schizophrenia risk locus. C4A variation drives differential synaptic pruning during postnatal brain development, with higher C4A expression increasing pruning and schizophrenia risk. This represents a fundamental shift in understanding schizophrenia genetics from purely neurochemical models to immune-developmental mechanisms.

full_name: Calcium Voltage-Gated Channel Subunit Alpha1 C

source.title: Altered function of lateral orbitofrontal cortex during reward processing in bipolar mania
source.authors: Erk et al.

AA.effect: AA carriers have altered L-type calcium channel function with reduced CACNA1C expression in the brain. Associated with approximately 1.2-fold increased bipolar disorder risk. AA individuals may show altered response to lithium therapy with potential for reduced therapeutic efficacy. Neurobiological changes affect emotional processing and reward circuitry.
AA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

AG.effect: AG carriers show intermediate CACNA1C expression with moderate changes in calcium channel function. Associated with mild increase in bipolar disorder risk and intermediate lithium response. Moderately altered emotional and reward processing.
AG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GA.effect: GA carriers show intermediate CACNA1C expression with moderate changes in calcium channel function. Associated with mild increase in bipolar disorder risk and intermediate lithium response. Moderately altered emotional and reward processing.
GA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GG.effect: GG carriers have normal L-type calcium channel function and standard CACNA1C expression in brain. Baseline bipolar disorder risk with typical mood stabilizer response patterns. Normal emotional and reward processing.
GG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

notes: CACNA1C rs1006737 is one of the most consistently replicated genetic findings in bipolar disorder GWAS studies. The risk allele (A) functions as a cis-expression QTL, reducing CACNA1C expression and affecting calcium signaling critical for synaptic plasticity and mood regulation.

gene_description: Transsulfuration pathway (homocysteine)

source.title: CBS 844ins68 polymorphism and homocysteine metabolism
source.finding: CBS variants affect transsulfuration pathway and homocysteine levels.

TT.effect: Increased transsulfuration activity. May deplete homocysteine too rapidly, shunting away from methylation.
TT.recommendation: Monitor homocysteine levels. May need less B6 supplementation. Ensure adequate methylation support.

CT.effect: Slightly increased CBS activity.
CT.recommendation: Standard B vitamin intake. Monitor if combined with MTHFR variants.

CC.effect: Normal transsulfuration pathway.
CC.recommendation: Standard dietary intake sufficient.

gene_description: CD274 (Cluster of Differentiation 274), also known as PD-L1, encodes a ligand that binds to PD-1 on T cells, suppressing immune response. PD-L1 expression in tumor cells and immune infiltrate predicts response to anti-PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors. Genetic variants in CD274 regulate PD-L1 expression levels.

source.title: Pembrolizumab for the treatment of non-small-cell lung cancer
source.finding: Pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1) showed improved survival over chemotherapy in NSCLC; PD-L1 expression ≥50% predicted superior response (KEYNOTE-001 trial)

CC.phenotype: Homozygous C allele associated with higher PD-L1 expression
CC.effect: Higher baseline PD-L1 expression in tumors; enhanced checkpoint inhibitor response predicted
CC.recommendation: Strong candidate for anti-PD-1/PD-L1 monotherapy. Monitor for immune-related adverse events. Consider combination with chemotherapy or targeted therapy depending on tumor type.

CG.effect: Intermediate PD-L1 expression; moderate checkpoint inhibitor response expected
CG.recommendation: Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 therapy indicated if PD-L1 IHC score ≥1%. Consider combination approaches for potentially enhanced efficacy.

GC.effect: Intermediate PD-L1 expression; moderate checkpoint inhibitor response expected
GC.recommendation: Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 therapy indicated if PD-L1 IHC score ≥1%. Consider combination approaches for potentially enhanced efficacy.

GG.phenotype: Homozygous G allele associated with lower PD-L1 expression
GG.effect: Lower baseline PD-L1 expression; reduced checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy response predicted
GG.recommendation: PD-L1 IHC testing essential. If PD-L1 <1%, consider combination chemotherapy+checkpoint inhibitor. Evaluate for other biomarkers (MSI, TMB).

clinical_significance: PD-L1 expression established as biomarker for checkpoint inhibitor response. CD274 genetic variants may modulate tumor PD-L1 expression and influence immunotherapy outcomes. Growing evidence for CD274 SNPs in predicting immunotherapy benefit.

title: CDH1 rs16260 Gastric Cancer Risk (E-cadherin Promoter)
description: Promoter SNP in CDH1 (E-cadherin), a calcium-dependent cell adhesion molecule. The -160C>A polymorphism (rs16260) is located in the CDH1 promoter and the A allele reduces transcriptional activity of E-cadherin by approximately 68%. Loss of E-cadherin function increases cellular motility and invasiveness, promoting gastric cancer development particularly in diffuse-type gastric adenocarcinoma.

source.title: CDH1 promoter polymorphisms and gastric cancer susceptibility

CC.effect: baseline_gastric_cancer_risk
CC.recommendation: standard_surveillance

AA.effect: 1.4x_increased_diffuse_gastric_cancer_risk
AA.recommendation: aggressive_surveillance_aggressive_treatment

gene_description: Carboxylesterase 1 — primary enzyme responsible for hydrolysis and metabolism of methylphenidate in the liver. Loss-of-function variants lead to significantly elevated drug exposure.

source.title: The impact of CES1 genotypes on the pharmacokinetics of methylphenidate in healthy Danish subjects
source.finding: G143E (rs71647871) carriers showed 149% increase in d-methylphenidate AUC (2.5-fold higher exposure), and required significantly lower doses to achieve clinical response.

AA.effect: Homozygous for the G143E loss-of-function variant. Markedly decreased CES1 enzymatic activity leading to dramatically elevated methylphenidate plasma levels. Expected >4-fold increase in drug exposure. Extremely rare genotype but carries high risk of adverse effects including cardiovascular events, insomnia, and appetite suppression.
AA.recommendation: Use significantly reduced methylphenidate dose (consider ≤25% of standard dose) or avoid methylphenidate entirely. Consider non-CES1-metabolized alternatives such as amphetamine-based stimulants, atomoxetine, or guanfacine. Closely monitor blood pressure and heart rate.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier of the G143E variant. CES1 activity is reduced, resulting in approximately 2.5-fold higher d-methylphenidate exposure (149% increase in AUC). Clinical responders with this genotype required significantly lower doses than wild-type individuals.
AG.recommendation: Start methylphenidate at a lower dose (consider 50% of standard starting dose) and titrate cautiously. Monitor for dose-dependent side effects including insomnia, appetite loss, tachycardia, and elevated blood pressure. This variant also affects dabigatran metabolism.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier of the G143E variant. CES1 activity is reduced, resulting in approximately 2.5-fold higher d-methylphenidate exposure (149% increase in AUC). Clinical responders with this genotype required significantly lower doses than wild-type individuals.
GA.recommendation: Start methylphenidate at a lower dose (consider 50% of standard starting dose) and titrate cautiously. Monitor for dose-dependent side effects including insomnia, appetite loss, tachycardia, and elevated blood pressure. This variant also affects dabigatran metabolism.

GG.effect: Wild-type CES1 function. Normal methylphenidate hydrolysis and clearance. Standard pharmacokinetic profile expected.
GG.recommendation: Standard methylphenidate dosing per clinical guidelines. No pharmacogenomic dose adjustment needed.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the C allele. Associated with moderately reduced CES1 activity and increased methylphenidate exposure. Also relevant for dabigatran metabolism, where this variant has been associated with reduced bleeding risk due to lower active drug levels.
CC.recommendation: Monitor for enhanced methylphenidate effects. Consider dose reduction if side effects are prominent. For dabigatran, this variant may provide a modest protective effect against bleeding.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Slight reduction in CES1 activity with marginally increased drug exposure for CES1 substrates.
CT.recommendation: Standard dosing generally appropriate. Monitor clinical response and adjust as needed.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Slight reduction in CES1 activity with marginally increased drug exposure for CES1 substrates.
TC.recommendation: Standard dosing generally appropriate. Monitor clinical response and adjust as needed.

TT.effect: Wild-type alleles. Normal CES1 enzymatic function and standard drug metabolism.
TT.recommendation: Standard dosing per clinical guidelines.

source.title: The Joint Link of the rs1051730 and rs1902341 Polymorphisms and Cigarette Smoking to Peripheral Artery Disease

description: CHRNA3 rs1051730 (T allele) is strongly associated with peripheral artery disease and lung cancer, particularly in smokers. The variant affects nicotine receptor expression and smoking behavior.

CC.effect: Reference homozygote
CC.recommendation: Standard cardiovascular health maintenance; smoking cessation if applicable
