CT.phenotype: Intermediate PAD risk, elevated in smokers
CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier; modest increased risk especially with smoking
CT.recommendation: Smoking cessation strongly recommended; regular vascular assessment; manage cardiovascular risk factors

TC.phenotype: Intermediate PAD risk, elevated in smokers
TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier; modest increased risk especially with smoking
TC.recommendation: Smoking cessation strongly recommended; regular vascular assessment; manage cardiovascular risk factors

TT.phenotype: High PAD and lung disease risk
TT.effect: Risk allele homozygote; substantially increased risk in smokers and non-smokers
TT.recommendation: Avoid smoking completely; regular PAD screening; lipid management; consider antiplatelet therapy; monitor LDL cholesterol

title: CHRNA5 rs8034191 Lung Cancer Risk (Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor)
description: Common variant in CHRNA5 encoding alpha-5 subunit of nicotinic acetylcholine receptor, associated with increased lung cancer susceptibility and smoking behavior. This tag SNP marks the 15q25.1 susceptibility locus.

source.title: Genome-wide association scan of tag SNPs identifies a susceptibility locus for lung cancer at 15q25.1

TT.effect: baseline_lung_cancer_risk
TT.recommendation: standard_surveillance

TC.effect: increased_lung_cancer_susceptibility
TC.recommendation: enhanced_screening_smoking_cessation

CT.effect: increased_lung_cancer_susceptibility
CT.recommendation: enhanced_screening_smoking_cessation

CC.effect: 1.8x_increased_lung_cancer_risk
CC.recommendation: aggressive_screening_chemotherapy_optimization

gene_description: Cholinergic Receptor Nicotinic Alpha 5 Subunit — subunit of nicotinic acetylcholine receptors involved in reward processing and nicotine dependence; rs16969968 (Asp398Asn) variant affects receptor function and aversive response to nicotine

source.title: A variant associated with nicotine dependence, lung cancer and peripheral arterial disease
source.finding: CHRNA5 rs16969968 A allele (Asp398) is associated with increased nicotine dependence, higher cigarette consumption, and reduced response to varenicline therapy. The variant reduces the aversive effects of nicotine, promoting continued smoking behavior.

AA.phenotype: Higher nicotine dependence risk and reduced smoking cessation response
AA.effect: Homozygous for the risk allele (Asp398). Strong genetic predisposition to nicotine dependence with approximately 1.5× increased risk compared to GG carriers. Individuals typically smoke more cigarettes per day and demonstrate reduced aversive response to nicotine. Significantly lower efficacy of varenicline and reduced success with nicotine replacement therapy.
AA.recommendation: Intensive smoking cessation support recommended. Consider combination therapy: varenicline + bupropion, or extended nicotine replacement with behavioral counseling. More frequent follow-up and relapse prevention counseling essential. May benefit from additional pharmacotherapy (clonidine, nortriptyline) and referral to specialized smoking cessation programs.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate nicotine dependence risk. Partial genetic predisposition with modest increased smoking intensity. Moderate reduction in aversive nicotine response compared to GG carriers.
AG.recommendation: Standard smoking cessation counseling and first-line pharmacotherapy (varenicline or bupropion). Monitor response and consider combination therapy if inadequate response. Routine follow-up for relapse prevention.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate nicotine dependence risk. Partial genetic predisposition with modest increased smoking intensity. Moderate reduction in aversive nicotine response compared to GG carriers.
GA.recommendation: Standard smoking cessation counseling and first-line pharmacotherapy (varenicline or bupropion). Monitor response and consider combination therapy if inadequate response. Routine follow-up for relapse prevention.

GG.effect: Wild-type genotype (Asn398). Normal aversive response to nicotine with baseline nicotine dependence risk. Individuals typically smoke fewer cigarettes per day and show better response to cessation pharmacotherapy.
GG.recommendation: Standard smoking cessation support. First-line pharmacotherapy (varenicline, bupropion, or nicotine replacement) with good expected efficacy. Routine behavioral counseling.

gene_description: Chloride Voltage-Gated Channel Ka — kidney chloride channel (CLC-Ka) essential for NaCl reabsorption in the thin ascending limb of Henle and medullary collecting duct. Variants affect salt sensitivity and blood pressure response to sodium intake.

source.title: Common genetic variants and haplotypes in renal CLCNKA gene are associated to salt-sensitive hypertension
source.finding: CLCNKA rs848307 is significantly associated with salt-sensitive blood pressure increase (P=0.0026). Risk allele carriers show abnormally large BP increase following modest salt intake, with lower basal plasma renin activity indicating chronic volume expansion.

TT.effect: Homozygous risk carriers show significantly greater blood pressure increase following sodium load. Lower basal plasma renin activity and heart rate, suggesting chronic volume expansion. Predisposes to left ventricular hypertrophy and endothelial dysfunction. Salt-sensitive phenotype through altered renal chloride reabsorption.
TT.recommendation: Strict dietary sodium restriction (<1500mg/day). DASH diet strongly recommended. Loop diuretics (furosemide) may be particularly effective as they target the same nephron segment. Regular blood pressure monitoring. Potassium supplementation may help.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show moderate blood pressure sensitivity to sodium intake. Intermediate renal chloride handling.
CT.recommendation: Moderate sodium restriction (<2300mg/day). Regular blood pressure checks. Heart-healthy diet rich in fruits and vegetables.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show moderate blood pressure sensitivity to sodium intake. Intermediate renal chloride handling.
TC.recommendation: Moderate sodium restriction (<2300mg/day). Regular blood pressure checks. Heart-healthy diet rich in fruits and vegetables.

CC.effect: Wild-type with standard renal chloride channel function. Normal blood pressure response to dietary sodium.
CC.recommendation: Standard sodium intake guidelines.

gene_description: Circadian locomotor output cycles kaput — core clock transcription factor influencing sleep timing, appetite rhythm, and weight-loss response

source.title: CLOCK gene is implicated in weight reduction in obese patients participating in a dietary programme based on the Mediterranean diet
source.finding: rs1801260 (3111T>C) minor allele carriers had higher baseline BMI and smaller weight loss on a hypocaloric Mediterranean-style program vs major allele homozygotes.

TT.effect: Major-allele homozygotes at rs1801260 showed greater reduction on structured hypocaloric diet in intervention study.
TT.recommendation: Regular meal timing and consistent sleep still support all genotypes.

TC.effect: Heterozygotes: intermediate BMI and weight-loss trajectories in obesity diet trials; literature shows population heterogeneity by sex/context.
TC.recommendation: Prioritize sleep regularity, morning light, and aligned meal windows alongside diet quality.

CT.effect: Heterozygotes: intermediate BMI and weight-loss trajectories in obesity diet trials; literature shows population heterogeneity by sex/context.
CT.recommendation: Prioritize sleep regularity, morning light, and aligned meal windows alongside diet quality.

CC.phenotype: Greater BMI signal / diet resistance (some cohorts)
CC.effect: Minor-allele homozygotes linked to higher baseline BMI and blunted loss on low-energy Mediterranean program in Garaulet et al.; not all studies agree.
CC.recommendation: Stricter adherence support, chronotherapy (sleep/meal timing), and clinician-led weight management if goals are not met.

gene_description: Cannabinoid Receptor 1 — primary G-protein coupled receptor for endocannabinoids and THC in the central nervous system. Mediates psychoactive effects of cannabis and is the target of cannabinoid-based therapies.

source.title: The association between cannabinoid receptor 1 gene (CNR1) and cannabis dependence symptoms in adolescents and young adults
source.finding: CNR1 haplotypes significantly associated with cannabis dependence risk. rs1049353 T-allele carriers showed increased psychotic adverse events (OR=6.1, p=0.009) and augmented subjective effects of THC.

TT.phenotype: Enhanced Cannabis Sensitivity — High Psychosis Risk
TT.effect: Homozygous for the T allele. Significantly increased odds of psychotic adverse events from cannabis/THC use (OR=6.1). Enhanced subjective effects including altered satiety and mood responses. Greater vulnerability to cannabis dependence. The variant is synonymous but may affect CNR1 expression regulation or mRNA stability.
TT.recommendation: Strongly advise caution with any cannabis-based therapy. If cannabinoids are medically necessary, use lowest effective dose with close psychiatric monitoring. Prefer CBD-dominant preparations over THC. Screen for personal and family history of psychosis before initiating therapy.

TC.effect: Heterozygous T-allele carrier. Intermediate increase in subjective effects of THC and moderately elevated risk of psychotic adverse events. Lower satiety response to THC compared to CC genotype.
TC.recommendation: Use caution with THC-containing preparations. Monitor for unusual psychiatric symptoms. CBD-dominant products may be better tolerated. Consider lower starting doses.

CT.effect: Heterozygous T-allele carrier. Intermediate increase in subjective effects of THC and moderately elevated risk of psychotic adverse events. Lower satiety response to THC compared to CC genotype.
CT.recommendation: Use caution with THC-containing preparations. Monitor for unusual psychiatric symptoms. CBD-dominant products may be better tolerated. Consider lower starting doses.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the reference C allele. Standard CNR1 receptor function with typical response to cannabinoid medications. Lower baseline risk of psychotic adverse events from THC.
CC.recommendation: Standard dosing for cannabinoid-based medications per clinical guidelines. Monitor as usual for efficacy and side effects.

AA.effect: Homozygous for the A allele. Marginally associated with altered cannabis response and dependence risk in haplotype analyses. The variant is in linkage disequilibrium with other CNR1 regulatory variants.
AA.recommendation: Consider in context of overall CNR1 haplotype. Monitor response to cannabinoid medications.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Minimal standalone effect on cannabinoid pharmacology.
AG.recommendation: Standard dosing and monitoring.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Minimal standalone effect on cannabinoid pharmacology.
GA.recommendation: Standard dosing and monitoring.

GG.effect: Wild-type alleles. Normal CNR1 regulatory activity.
GG.recommendation: Standard dosing per clinical guidelines.

gene_description: Contactin Associated Protein 2 (CASPR2) — member of the neurexin superfamily that mediates cell-cell interactions in the nervous system. Essential for clustering potassium channels in myelinated axons and normal nervous system development. The rs7794745 polymorphism affects neural connectivity and language development pathways implicated in autism spectrum disorder.

source.title: A Common Genetic Variant in the Neurexin Superfamily Member CNTNAP2 Increases Familial Risk of Autism

TT.effect: Homozygous T-allele carriers show significantly elevated autism risk (approximately 1.2-1.4x relative risk). Associated with altered language development trajectories, reduced brain volumes in parahippocampal and temporal regions, decreased white matter integrity in language-associated tracts. Overtransmission of T allele confirmed in multiple independent autism family-based samples.

TA.phenotype: Mild increased autism spectrum disorder risk
TA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show modestly elevated risk for autism spectrum disorder. Intermediate effects on neural connectivity and language processing. Some studies show 30-40% increased risk relative to wild-type AA genotype.

AT.phenotype: Mild increased autism spectrum disorder risk
AT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show modestly elevated risk for autism spectrum disorder. Intermediate effects on neural connectivity and language processing. Some studies show 30-40% increased risk relative to wild-type AA genotype.

AA.effect: Homozygous A-allele carriers serve as reference group. Standard autism prevalence risk without specific CNTNAP2-mediated increase. Normal language development trajectory and neural connectivity patterns.

gene_description: Collagen type I alpha 1 chain (COL1A1) — structural protein in tendons, ligaments, and bones; first intron Sp1 binding site polymorphism affects type I collagen expression

source.title: Association of polymorphisms rs1800012 in COL1A1 with sports-related tendon and ligament injuries: a meta-analysis
source.finding: rs1800012 TT genotype (rare allele) is protective against sports-related tendon/ligament injuries (OR=0.17 in homozygote model). GG genotype increases collagen α1:α2 imbalance, reducing tendon/ligament structural integrity.

GG.effect: Homozygous for G allele. Enhanced COL1A1 expression creating imbalance in α1:α2 collagen chain ratio (>2:1). Alters tendon/ligament microarchitecture, reducing structural integrity and tensile strength. Increased susceptibility to sports-related ACL tears, Achilles tendon injuries, rotator cuff tears, and tennis elbow.
GG.recommendation: Enhanced injury prevention strategies essential. Emphasize proper warm-up, gradual training progression, strength training for joint stabilizers, flexibility work, and adequate recovery. Consider medical clearance before intense sports. Use protective equipment. Avoid sudden training intensity increases.

GT.effect: Heterozygous for G/T alleles. Intermediate collagen α1:α2 ratio. Intermediate tendon/ligament structural properties. Some increased injury risk compared to TT genotype but much lower than GG.
GT.recommendation: Standard injury prevention practices appropriate (warm-up, progressive training, strength training). Attention to technique important. Monitor for early signs of tendon/ligament stress.

TG.effect: Heterozygous for G/T alleles. Intermediate collagen α1:α2 ratio. Intermediate tendon/ligament structural properties. Some increased injury risk compared to TT genotype but much lower than GG.
TG.recommendation: Standard injury prevention practices appropriate (warm-up, progressive training, strength training). Attention to technique important. Monitor for early signs of tendon/ligament stress.

TT.effect: Homozygous for T allele (protective). Lower COL1A1 expression maintaining normal collagen α1:α2 chain ratio (2:1). Optimized tendon/ligament microarchitecture and structural integrity. Protective effect against sports-related soft tissue injuries.
TT.recommendation: Standard injury prevention practices adequate. Lower risk for tendon/ligament injuries. Regular training and conditioning sufficient.

gene_description: Catecholamine metabolism, stress response

source.title: COMT Val158Met polymorphism and cognition
source.finding: COMT Val158Met: Met/Met (AA) = higher prefrontal dopamine, better working memory, but higher stress sensitivity.

AA.effect: Slow dopamine clearance in prefrontal cortex. Better baseline cognition but more stress-sensitive. Stimulants may cause anxiety.
AA.recommendation: Start stimulants at lower dose. Monitor for anxiety and overstimulation.

AG.effect: Balanced dopamine metabolism. Moderate response to stimulants.
AG.recommendation: Standard dosing. Good baseline for most stimulant medications.

GG.effect: Fast dopamine clearance. Stress-resilient but may have lower baseline cognitive performance. Tends to respond well to stimulants.
GG.recommendation: Standard to higher stimulant doses may be needed for optimal effect.

source.title: Genetic basis for individual variations in pain perception and the development of a chronic pain condition
source.authors: Diatchenko et al.

AA.effect: Homozygous Met carriers have significantly reduced COMT enzymatic activity (~20-fold reduction compared to Val/Val), leading to elevated catecholamine levels. Associated with heightened pain sensitivity and increased pain perception across experimental paradigms. Higher opioid analgesic requirements for pain management. Increased risk for developing chronic pain conditions. May require dosage adjustments for analgesic medications.
AA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate COMT enzymatic activity with moderate catecholamine metabolism. Associated with mild to moderate increase in pain sensitivity relative to Val/Val genotype. Intermediate opioid requirements and moderate risk for chronic pain development.
AG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate COMT enzymatic activity with moderate catecholamine metabolism. Associated with mild to moderate increase in pain sensitivity relative to Val/Val genotype. Intermediate opioid requirements and moderate risk for chronic pain development.
GA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GG.effect: Homozygous Val carriers have high COMT enzymatic activity with efficient catecholamine metabolism and lower baseline catecholamine levels. Associated with lower pain sensitivity and reduced pain perception. Lower opioid requirements for analgesia. Lower baseline risk for chronic pain development. Standard analgesic dosing typically appropriate.
GG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

notes: COMT Val158Met (rs4680) is a functional polymorphism affecting catecholamine metabolism. Three common haplotypes (LPS/APS/HPS) show dramatic differences in enzyme activity driven by both the Val158Met coding change and regulatory synonymous variations affecting mRNA secondary structure. Met carriers have substantially increased pain sensitivity and higher risk for chronic pain conditions.

gene_description: Catecholamine metabolism, stress response

source.title: Pharmacogenetics of modafinil after sleep loss: catechol-O-methyltransferase genotype modulates waking functions but not recovery sleep
source.finding: COMT Val158Met strongly predicts modafinil efficacy. Val/Val (GG) subjects maintained baseline cognitive performance on 200mg modafinil during 40h sleep deprivation; Met/Met (AA) subjects showed virtually no benefit from the same dose.

AA.phenotype: Low COMT Activity (Met/Met) — Poor Modafinil Responder
AA.effect: High baseline prefrontal dopamine. Modafinil was virtually ineffective at maintaining cognitive performance and vigilant attention during sleep deprivation. Already-saturated dopamine system leaves little room for modafinil's dopaminergic action.
AA.recommendation: Modafinil may have minimal benefit. Consider alternative wakefulness agents or higher doses with close monitoring. Armodafinil may offer slightly different pharmacokinetics.

AG.phenotype: Intermediate COMT Activity (Val/Met) — Partial Modafinil Responder
AG.effect: Intermediate dopamine metabolism. Moderate response to modafinil expected.
AG.recommendation: Standard starting dose (100-200mg). Titrate based on response.

GG.phenotype: High COMT Activity (Val/Val) — Good Modafinil Responder
GG.effect: Fast dopamine clearance in prefrontal cortex. Modafinil potently improved vigor, well-being, executive functioning, and sustained vigilant attention throughout 40h sleep deprivation.
GG.recommendation: Standard dose (100-200mg) expected to be effective. Good candidate for modafinil therapy.

source.title: CRHR1 genetic variation and stress interact to influence reward learning and processing of social rejection
source.authors: Heim et al.

AA.effect: AA carriers show altered HPA axis function with increased cortisol reactivity to stress. Associated with significantly elevated anxiety risk, particularly in individuals with early life stress exposure. Enhanced stress sensitivity and potential for more pronounced anxiety symptoms.
AA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

AG.effect: AG carriers show intermediate HPA axis responsivity with moderate cortisol reactivity. Associated with mild increase in anxiety risk, particularly in stress-exposed populations. Show intermediate response to anxiolytic medications.
AG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GA.effect: GA carriers show intermediate HPA axis responsivity with moderate cortisol reactivity. Associated with mild increase in anxiety risk, particularly in stress-exposed populations. Show intermediate response to anxiolytic medications.
GA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GG.effect: GG carriers have normal HPA axis function and baseline cortisol reactivity to stress. Baseline anxiety risk with typical medication response patterns.
GG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

notes: CRHR1 rs110402 is associated with HPA axis function and stress reactivity. Risk alleles show strong gene-environment interaction with early life stress, a critical factor in anxiety disorder development.

gene_description: Cryptochrome Circadian Regulator 2 — flavin adenine dinucleotide-binding protein essential for circadian rhythm stability. Regulates glucose homeostasis through circadian clock-metabolic coupling. Variants affect fasting glucose and type 2 diabetes risk.

source.title: CRY2 (rs11605924) and G6PC2 (rs560887) single nucleotide polymorphisms increase risk of type 2 diabetes mellitus
source.finding: CRY2 rs11605924 recessive genotype is associated with 1.82-fold increased T2DM risk. The A allele affects 2h glucose concentrations (β=0.07 mmol/L per allele) with season-dependent effects (stronger in dark season). Gene-diet fat interactions modify energy expenditure response to weight loss.

AA.phenotype: Increased T2DM risk / circadian metabolic disruption
AA.effect: Homozygous A carriers show 1.82-fold increased type 2 diabetes risk. Elevated 2h post-load glucose levels, particularly during winter/dark season. Greater reduction in respiratory quotient (RQ) and increase in resting metabolic rate with weight-loss diets. Significant gene-diet fat interactions affect metabolic adaptation. Hepatic lipid content may be elevated.
AA.recommendation: Monitor fasting and post-load glucose regularly. Seasonal variation in glucose — extra vigilance in winter months. Low-fat dietary interventions show particular metabolic benefit for this genotype. Maintain consistent circadian schedule. Adequate light exposure during dark months.

AC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate glucose effects. Mild seasonal variation in metabolic parameters. Modest gene-diet interactions.
AC.recommendation: Standard metabolic monitoring. Maintain regular meal and sleep schedules. Moderate fat intake.

CA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate glucose effects. Mild seasonal variation in metabolic parameters. Modest gene-diet interactions.
CA.recommendation: Standard metabolic monitoring. Maintain regular meal and sleep schedules. Moderate fat intake.

CC.effect: Wild-type CRY2 function with standard circadian-metabolic coupling. Normal glucose homeostasis from this locus.
CC.recommendation: Standard dietary and lifestyle guidelines.

gene_description: Cytochrome P450 1A2 — major hepatic enzyme responsible for metabolism of caffeine, clozapine, theophylline, olanzapine, and several other drugs. Activity is highly inducible by smoking, cruciferous vegetables, and chargrilled meat.

source.title: PharmGKB summary: very important pharmacogene information for CYP1A2
source.finding: CYP1A2*1F (rs762551 A allele) is associated with increased enzyme inducibility — AA homozygotes who smoke or consume inducers show ultrarapid caffeine metabolism and lower plasma levels of CYP1A2 substrates like clozapine and theophylline.

AA.effect: Homozygous *1F carriers show significantly increased CYP1A2 inducibility. In smokers or heavy coffee drinkers, enzyme activity is markedly elevated — rapid caffeine clearance, lower clozapine/olanzapine plasma levels, and faster theophylline metabolism. Without inducers, activity may be near-normal. Risk of sub-therapeutic clozapine levels if patient smokes.
AA.recommendation: For clozapine: smokers with AA genotype may need higher doses to achieve therapeutic levels. Monitor plasma concentrations closely. If patient quits smoking, reduce dose promptly to avoid toxicity. For caffeine: fast metabolizer — higher tolerance, may need more for effect. For theophylline: monitor levels; may need dose increase in smokers.

AC.effect: Heterozygotes show moderately increased CYP1A2 inducibility compared to CC wild-type. Intermediate caffeine metabolism. Clozapine and theophylline levels may be somewhat reduced in the presence of inducers (smoking, omeprazole).
AC.recommendation: Standard dosing for most CYP1A2 substrates. Monitor clozapine levels if patient is a smoker. Caffeine metabolism is moderate.

CA.effect: Heterozygotes show moderately increased CYP1A2 inducibility compared to CC wild-type. Intermediate caffeine metabolism. Clozapine and theophylline levels may be somewhat reduced in the presence of inducers (smoking, omeprazole).
CA.recommendation: Standard dosing for most CYP1A2 substrates. Monitor clozapine levels if patient is a smoker. Caffeine metabolism is moderate.

CC.effect: Wild-type CYP1A2 with standard inducibility. Normal caffeine metabolism — slower clearance compared to *1F carriers. Standard clozapine and theophylline metabolism. Less variation in drug levels with smoking status changes.
CC.recommendation: Standard dosing per prescribing guidelines. Slow caffeine metabolizer — may be more sensitive to caffeine effects, higher cardiovascular risk with high coffee intake (>3 cups/day).

gene_description: Cytochrome P450 2B6 — key hepatic enzyme metabolizing efavirenz, methadone, bupropion, and ketamine; highly polymorphic with CYP2B6*6 (rs3745274 G516T) being the most common reduced-function allele across populations

source.title: Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium (CPIC) Guideline for CYP2B6 and Efavirenz-Containing Antiretroviral Therapy
source.finding: CYP2B6 poor metabolizers (e.g., *6/*6 homozygotes) have 3–4-fold higher efavirenz plasma concentrations, significantly increasing risk of CNS toxicity (dizziness, vivid dreams, depression, suicidality). CPIC recommends 50% dose reduction or alternative antiretroviral for poor metabolizers.

TT.effect: Markedly reduced CYP2B6 enzyme activity. Efavirenz plasma levels 3–4× higher than normal metabolizers, leading to high risk of persistent CNS adverse effects (insomnia, vivid dreams, dizziness, psychosis). Also slower methadone and bupropion clearance.
TT.recommendation: Reduce efavirenz dose by 50% (from 600 mg to 400 mg daily) or switch to an alternative antiretroviral (e.g., dolutegravir). For methadone: monitor for prolonged QTc and over-sedation. For bupropion: consider lower dose and monitor seizure risk.

GT.effect: Moderately reduced CYP2B6 activity. Efavirenz levels approximately 1.5–2× higher than normal metabolizers. Some increased risk of CNS side effects, especially in the first weeks of therapy.
GT.recommendation: Standard efavirenz dose (600 mg) is generally tolerable but monitor for persistent CNS side effects. If toxicity occurs, consider dose reduction to 400 mg. Standard precautions for methadone and bupropion.

TG.effect: Moderately reduced CYP2B6 activity. Efavirenz levels approximately 1.5–2× higher than normal metabolizers. Some increased risk of CNS side effects, especially in the first weeks of therapy.
TG.recommendation: Standard efavirenz dose (600 mg) is generally tolerable but monitor for persistent CNS side effects. If toxicity occurs, consider dose reduction to 400 mg. Standard precautions for methadone and bupropion.

GG.effect: Normal CYP2B6 enzyme activity. Standard efavirenz metabolism and expected plasma levels. Normal clearance of methadone, bupropion, and ketamine.
GG.recommendation: Use standard efavirenz dose (600 mg daily). No CYP2B6-based dose adjustments required.

gene_description: Metabolizes clopidogrel, SSRIs, PPIs, voriconazole

source.title: CPIC Guideline for CYP2C19 and Clopidogrel Therapy
source.finding: CYP2C19 poor metabolizers should avoid clopidogrel. Consider prasugrel or ticagrelor.

AA.effect: Cannot activate clopidogrel. Major cardiovascular risk.
AA.recommendation: Avoid clopidogrel. Use prasugrel or ticagrelor.

AG.effect: Reduced clopidogrel activation. Diminished antiplatelet effect.
AG.recommendation: Consider alternative antiplatelet or higher dose with monitoring.

GG.effect: Normal clopidogrel activation.
GG.recommendation: Standard dosing.

TT.effect: Increased drug activation. May have enhanced response or increased bleeding risk.
TT.recommendation: Standard dose; monitor for bleeding with clopidogrel.

CT.effect: Slightly increased drug metabolism.
CT.recommendation: Standard dosing.

CC.effect: Normal drug metabolism.
CC.recommendation: Standard dosing.

gene_description: Metabolizes clopidogrel, SSRIs, PPIs, voriconazole

source.title: Evaluation of modafinil as a perpetrator of metabolic drug-drug interactions using a model informed cocktail reaction phenotyping trial protocol
source.finding: Modafinil is a borderline moderate CYP2C19 inhibitor. Omeprazole AUC increased 77% with modafinil co-administration. CYP2C19 poor metabolizers face compounded risk when taking modafinil alongside CYP2C19 substrates.

AA.phenotype: Poor Metabolizer (*2/*2) — High Interaction Risk with Modafinil
AA.effect: Already minimal CYP2C19 activity. Modafinil's CYP2C19 inhibition compounds the deficit. Co-administered CYP2C19 substrates (omeprazole, clopidogrel, diazepam, phenytoin, SSRIs) may reach dangerously high levels.
AA.recommendation: Avoid co-administering modafinil with CYP2C19 substrates. If unavoidable, reduce substrate dose and monitor closely. Consider alternative to clopidogrel (prasugrel/ticagrelor).

AG.phenotype: Intermediate Metabolizer (*1/*2) — Moderate Interaction Risk with Modafinil
AG.effect: Reduced CYP2C19 activity further inhibited by modafinil. CYP2C19 substrates may accumulate.
AG.recommendation: Monitor CYP2C19 substrate levels when co-administered with modafinil. Dose adjustments may be needed for omeprazole, diazepam, or SSRIs.

GG.phenotype: Normal Metabolizer (*1/*1) — Standard Interaction Risk with Modafinil
GG.effect: Normal CYP2C19 activity partially inhibited by modafinil (omeprazole AUC +77%). Clinically manageable in most cases.
GG.recommendation: Be aware of potential interaction with CYP2C19 substrates. Routine monitoring sufficient.

gene_description: Cytochrome P450 2C19 — primary hepatic enzyme metabolizing proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole, lansoprazole, pantoprazole, esomeprazole), benzodiazepines (diazepam, clobazam), and antifungals (voriconazole). Loss-of-function variants (*2, *3) reduce metabolism; gain-of-function (*17) increases metabolism.

source.title: Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium (CPIC) Guideline for CYP2C19 and Proton Pump Inhibitor Dosing
source.finding: CYP2C19 poor metabolizers show significantly reduced PPI efficacy for acid suppression; ultrarapid metabolizers require higher doses or alternative agents.

AA.effect: Non-functional CYP2C19 enzyme. Homozygous carriers have ~2-5 fold decreased metabolism of PPIs, resulting in markedly elevated plasma concentrations and prolonged acid suppression. For benzodiazepines, 2-fold increased AUC compared to rapid metabolizers increases toxicity risk. Voriconazole accumulates to supratherapeutic levels, increasing risk of hepatotoxicity and visual disturbances.
AA.recommendation: For PPIs: consider alternative acid suppressant (H2-blocker) or increased monitoring. For diazepam/clobazam: reduce starting dose by 25-50%; start clobazam at 5 mg/day. For voriconazole: use alternative antifungal (isavuconazole, posaconazole, lipid amphotericin B) or significantly reduce dose with therapeutic drug monitoring.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show 30-50% reduction in CYP2C19 activity. Intermediate PPI metabolism results in modest elevation of plasma concentrations. Benzodiazepine AUC increased 1.46-fold compared to normal metabolizers. Voriconazole trough concentrations elevated but usually manageable.
AG.recommendation: For PPIs: standard dosing with enhanced monitoring for therapeutic response. For diazepam/clobazam: standard dosing with baseline monitoring; if adverse effects occur, consider 25% dose reduction. For voriconazole: standard dosing with therapeutic drug monitoring; target trough 1-5.5 mg/L.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show 30-50% reduction in CYP2C19 activity. Intermediate PPI metabolism results in modest elevation of plasma concentrations. Benzodiazepine AUC increased 1.46-fold compared to normal metabolizers. Voriconazole trough concentrations elevated but usually manageable.
GA.recommendation: For PPIs: standard dosing with enhanced monitoring for therapeutic response. For diazepam/clobazam: standard dosing with baseline monitoring; if adverse effects occur, consider 25% dose reduction. For voriconazole: standard dosing with therapeutic drug monitoring; target trough 1-5.5 mg/L.

GG.effect: Normal CYP2C19 enzyme activity. Standard metabolism of all substrates with expected plasma concentrations and clinical response.
GG.recommendation: Standard dosing for all drugs (omeprazole 20 mg daily, lansoprazole 30 mg daily, pantoprazole 40 mg daily, esomeprazole 40 mg daily, diazepam 2-10 mg per dose, clobazam 15-30 mg daily, voriconazole loading 6 mg/kg Q12h then 4 mg/kg Q12h).

AA.effect: Premature stop codon creates non-functional CYP2C19. Homozygous carriers show severe loss of function (~99% activity reduction), resulting in profound drug accumulation. PPIs achieve very high plasma levels. Benzodiazepines show dramatically elevated exposure. Voriconazole reaches toxic concentrations rapidly.
AA.recommendation: For PPIs: strongly consider alternative agent or minimal dosing with frequent monitoring. For diazepam/clobazam: significant dose reduction (start at 25-50% of standard); monitor closely for adverse effects. For voriconazole: DO NOT USE standard regimen; use alternative antifungal or consult pharmacokinetics specialist for dose adjustment.

AG.effect: Heterozygous loss-of-function; approximately 50% reduction in enzyme activity. Moderate elevation in drug plasma concentrations across all substrates. Moderate increase in both benzodiazepine exposure and voriconazole levels.
AG.recommendation: For PPIs: standard dosing with enhanced clinical monitoring. For diazepam/clobazam: standard dosing; monitor for adverse effects and consider dose reduction if needed. For voriconazole: standard dosing with therapeutic drug monitoring.

GA.effect: Heterozygous loss-of-function; approximately 50% reduction in enzyme activity. Moderate elevation in drug plasma concentrations across all substrates. Moderate increase in both benzodiazepine exposure and voriconazole levels.
GA.recommendation: For PPIs: standard dosing with enhanced clinical monitoring. For diazepam/clobazam: standard dosing; monitor for adverse effects and consider dose reduction if needed. For voriconazole: standard dosing with therapeutic drug monitoring.

GG.effect: Normal CYP2C19 enzyme function. Standard drug metabolism and plasma concentrations expected.
GG.recommendation: Standard dosing for all substrates per CPIC and product labeling.

TT.effect: Promoter gain-of-function variant (*17) increases CYP2C19 transcription and protein expression by ~2-fold. Homozygous carriers show markedly increased metabolism of PPIs, benzodiazepines, and voriconazole, resulting in subtherapeutic plasma concentrations. PPIs may not achieve adequate acid suppression. Voriconazole levels may be insufficient to treat invasive fungal infections.
TT.recommendation: For PPIs: may require higher doses (e.g., omeprazole 40 mg daily instead of 20 mg) or alternative agent; monitor symptom control. For diazepam/clobazam: standard to higher doses; monitor clinical response. For voriconazole: standard voriconazole dosing often inadequate; consider higher doses with careful therapeutic drug monitoring (target trough 1-5.5 mg/L) or alternative antifungal.

CT.effect: Heterozygous gain-of-function; ~1.25-fold increased enzyme activity. Slightly enhanced metabolism of all substrates, resulting in modestly lower plasma concentrations and potentially reduced clinical efficacy, particularly for PPIs.
CT.recommendation: For PPIs: standard dosing with monitoring for adequate acid suppression; if inadequate, consider dose increase. For diazepam/clobazam: standard dosing. For voriconazole: standard dosing with therapeutic drug monitoring.

TC.effect: Heterozygous gain-of-function; ~1.25-fold increased enzyme activity. Slightly enhanced metabolism of all substrates, resulting in modestly lower plasma concentrations and potentially reduced clinical efficacy, particularly for PPIs.
TC.recommendation: For PPIs: standard dosing with monitoring for adequate acid suppression; if inadequate, consider dose increase. For diazepam/clobazam: standard dosing. For voriconazole: standard dosing with therapeutic drug monitoring.

CC.effect: Wild-type promoter. Normal CYP2C19 expression and enzyme activity. Standard drug metabolism.
CC.recommendation: Standard dosing for all substrates per guidelines.

gene_description: CYP2C19 is involved in the metabolism of many drugs in the liver, including some antidepressants and anticonvulsants.

source.title: Disposition of Oral Cannabidiol-Rich Cannabis Extracts in Children with Epilepsy.
source.finding: Determined pharmacokinetic parameters of cannabidiol in children.

AA.effect: Increased plasma concentrations of CYP2C19 substrates.
AA.recommendation: Consider dose reduction or alternative therapy.

AG.effect: Moderate plasma concentrations of CYP2C19 substrates.
AG.recommendation: Monitor therapy, adjust dose if necessary.

GG.effect: Normal metabolism of CYP2C19 substrates.
GG.recommendation: Standard dosing.

gene_description: Metabolizes warfarin, NSAIDs, phenytoin

source.title: CPIC Guideline for Pharmacogenetics-Guided Warfarin Dosing
source.finding: CYP2C9 *2 and *3 alleles require warfarin dose reduction of 20-50%.

TT.effect: Significantly reduced CYP2C9 activity. Warfarin dose reduction ~50%.
TT.recommendation: Use pharmacogenomic dosing algorithm. Reduce warfarin and NSAID doses significantly.

CT.effect: Reduced CYP2C9 activity. Warfarin dose reduction ~20-30%.
CT.recommendation: Reduce initial warfarin dose. Monitor INR closely.

CC.effect: Normal CYP2C9 activity.
CC.recommendation: Standard dosing.

gene_description: Cytochrome P450 2C9 — major hepatic enzyme responsible for oxidative metabolism of THC, phenytoin, NSAIDs, sulfonylureas, and warfarin. *2 and *3 alleles cause 30% and 80% reduction in activity, respectively.

source.title: Pharmacogenomics of CYP2C9: Functional and Clinical Considerations
source.finding: CYP2C9*2 (rs1799853) and *3 (rs1057910) reduce enzyme activity by 30% and 80% respectively. THC metabolism is significantly reduced in *3 carriers, leading to prolonged psychoactive effects. Allele frequencies up to 18% in Europeans.

TT.effect: Homozygous for CYP2C9*2 (Arg144Cys). Approximately 30% reduction in CYP2C9 activity. THC clearance is moderately decreased, potentially leading to prolonged psychoactive effects and elevated plasma levels. Also impacts metabolism of phenytoin, NSAIDs, and sulfonylureas.
TT.recommendation: Reduce THC/dronabinol doses and extend dosing intervals. For phenytoin, reduce dose by 25% per CPIC guidelines. For sulfonylureas (glimepiride, glipizide), start at lower doses and monitor blood glucose. Avoid high-frequency cannabis use.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier of CYP2C9*2. Mildly reduced CYP2C9 activity (~15% reduction). Slightly prolonged THC effects and modest impact on clearance of other CYP2C9 substrates.
TC.recommendation: Standard dosing generally appropriate for most drugs. May require modest dose adjustment for narrow therapeutic index drugs. Monitor clinical response.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier of CYP2C9*2. Mildly reduced CYP2C9 activity (~15% reduction). Slightly prolonged THC effects and modest impact on clearance of other CYP2C9 substrates.
CT.recommendation: Standard dosing generally appropriate for most drugs. May require modest dose adjustment for narrow therapeutic index drugs. Monitor clinical response.

CC.phenotype: Normal CYP2C9 Activity at This Locus
CC.effect: Wild-type at the *2 position. Normal CYP2C9 activity at this locus (still check *3 variant rs1057910 for complete phenotype assignment).
CC.recommendation: Standard dosing per clinical guidelines. Check CYP2C9*3 status for complete metabolizer phenotype.

CC.effect: Homozygous for CYP2C9*3 (Ile359Leu). Approximately 80% reduction in CYP2C9 activity. Dramatically prolonged THC psychoactive effects and significantly elevated plasma concentrations. High risk of adverse effects from phenytoin, warfarin, NSAIDs, and sulfonylureas due to impaired clearance.
CC.recommendation: Avoid THC/dronabinol or use minimal doses with close monitoring. For phenytoin, reduce dose by 50% per CPIC. For warfarin, reduce initial dose significantly. For sulfonylureas, use alternative diabetes medications. Closely monitor for drug toxicity.

CA.effect: Heterozygous carrier of CYP2C9*3. Approximately 40% reduction in CYP2C9 activity. Moderately prolonged THC effects. Clinically significant for drugs with narrow therapeutic indices.
CA.recommendation: Reduce THC doses and consider longer dosing intervals. For phenytoin, reduce dose by 25%. Monitor for enhanced drug effects and adverse reactions with all CYP2C9 substrates.

AC.effect: Heterozygous carrier of CYP2C9*3. Approximately 40% reduction in CYP2C9 activity. Moderately prolonged THC effects. Clinically significant for drugs with narrow therapeutic indices.
AC.recommendation: Reduce THC doses and consider longer dosing intervals. For phenytoin, reduce dose by 25%. Monitor for enhanced drug effects and adverse reactions with all CYP2C9 substrates.

AA.phenotype: Normal CYP2C9 Activity at This Locus
AA.effect: Wild-type at the *3 position. Normal CYP2C9 activity at this locus (still check *2 variant rs1799853 for complete phenotype assignment).
AA.recommendation: Standard dosing per clinical guidelines. Check CYP2C9*2 status for complete metabolizer phenotype.

gene_description: Metabolizes ~25% of all drugs (codeine, tamoxifen, antidepressants)

source.title: PharmGKB summary: very important pharmacogene information for CYP2D6
source.finding: CYP2D6 metabolizes ~25% of clinically used drugs. Poor metabolizers at risk for toxicity or therapeutic failure.

AA.effect: No CYP2D6 activity. Codeine has no analgesic effect; risk of toxicity with other substrates.
AA.recommendation: Avoid codeine/tramadol (no conversion to active metabolite). Reduce dose of SSRIs, antipsychotics.

AG.effect: Reduced CYP2D6 activity. Partial drug metabolism.
AG.recommendation: Consider dose reduction for CYP2D6 substrates. Monitor for side effects.

GG.effect: Normal CYP2D6 activity.
GG.recommendation: Standard dosing.

TT.effect: CYP2D6*41/*41 — reduced metabolism, similar to intermediate metabolizer.
TT.recommendation: Consider dose adjustments for CYP2D6 substrates. Monitor for reduced efficacy of prodrugs (codeine, tamoxifen) and increased levels of active drugs.

CT.effect: One copy *41, slightly reduced metabolism.
CT.recommendation: Monitor for subtle changes in drug response. Dose adjustments may be needed for sensitive substrates.

TC.effect: One copy *41, slightly reduced metabolism.
TC.recommendation: Monitor for subtle changes in drug response. Dose adjustments may be needed for sensitive substrates.

CC.effect: No *41 variant. Normal CYP2D6 activity at this position.
CC.recommendation: Standard dosing.

gene_description: Cytochrome P450 Family 2 Subfamily E Member 1 — a hepatic microsomal enzyme involved in the oxidative metabolism of isoniazid. The rs2031920 (-1053C>T) promoter variant affects CYP2E1 expression and may influence isoniazid-induced hepatotoxicity risk.

source.title: Cytochrome P450 2E1 genotype and the susceptibility to antituberculosis drug-induced hepatitis
source.finding: CYP2E1 rs2031920 polymorphism affects isoniazid hepatotoxicity susceptibility, though evidence is inconsistent across populations. The T allele may influence hepatic oxidative stress from isoniazid toxic metabolites.

CC.effect: Wild-type genotype with normal CYP2E1 promoter activity and enzyme expression. Standard isoniazid oxidative metabolism producing acetylhydrazine and hydrazine metabolites.
CC.recommendation: Standard isoniazid dosing with routine hepatic function monitoring. Monitor serum aminotransferases (AST, ALT) and bilirubin at baseline and during TB treatment.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers with intermediate CYP2E1 expression. Moderate oxidative metabolism of isoniazid. Baseline isoniazid hepatotoxicity risk similar to wild-type.
CT.recommendation: Standard isoniazid dosing. Routine liver function monitoring at baseline, 2-4 weeks, and 8-12 weeks of treatment. Monitor for hepatotoxicity symptoms.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers with intermediate CYP2E1 expression. Moderate oxidative metabolism of isoniazid. Baseline isoniazid hepatotoxicity risk similar to wild-type.
TC.recommendation: Standard isoniazid dosing. Routine liver function monitoring at baseline, 2-4 weeks, and 8-12 weeks of treatment. Monitor for hepatotoxicity symptoms.

TT.effect: Homozygous T allele carriers with enhanced CYP2E1 promoter activity and higher enzyme expression. Increased oxidative metabolism of isoniazid to acetylhydrazine and hydrazine, potentially increasing hepatotoxicity risk through enhanced oxidative stress and reactive metabolite formation.
TT.recommendation: Standard isoniazid dosing with enhanced hepatic function monitoring. More frequent LFT monitoring recommended (baseline, week 2, week 4, week 8, week 12). Patient education on hepatotoxicity symptoms strongly advised. Consider therapeutic drug monitoring of isoniazid levels if available. Monitor for fever, jaundice, dark urine, abdominal pain, or malaise.

clinical_notes: The association between rs2031920 and isoniazid hepatotoxicity is inconsistent across populations, with some studies showing no significant association. NAT2 slow acetylator phenotype is a more established risk factor for isoniazid hepatotoxicity. CYP2E1 rs2031920 should be considered as part of a multi-gene risk assessment rather than as a standalone predictor.

gene_description: Cytochrome P450 Family 2 Subfamily R Member 1 — encodes vitamin D 25-hydroxylase, the enzyme catalyzing the first step of vitamin D metabolism; rs10741657 variant affects enzyme activity and circulating 25(OH)D levels

source.title: Common genetic determinants of vitamin D insufficiency: a genome-wide association study
source.finding: CYP2R1 rs10741657 A allele is associated with reduced 25-hydroxylation of cholecalciferol, resulting in lower circulating 25(OH)D levels. The variant affects CYP2R1 expression or enzyme activity, impairing the conversion of vitamin D3 to its active metabolite.

AA.phenotype: Reduced vitamin D 25-hydroxylation — increased vitamin D insufficiency risk
AA.effect: Homozygous for the risk allele (AA). Significantly reduced CYP2R1 enzyme activity or expression. Impaired conversion of vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) to 25-hydroxyvitamin D (25(OH)D). Individuals typically have lower circulating 25(OH)D levels (mean ~8-10 ng/mL lower than GG carriers) despite similar sun exposure and dietary intake. Increased risk of vitamin D insufficiency (25(OH)D <20 ng/mL) and deficiency (<12 ng/mL).
AA.recommendation: More aggressive vitamin D supplementation needed. Start with higher cholecalciferol doses (2000-4000 IU daily or weekly high-dose regimens) and monitor 25(OH)D levels every 8-12 weeks until target achieved. Target 25(OH)D >30 ng/mL (75 nmol/L). Consider alternative active metabolites (calcifediol 0.25-0.5 mcg BID) which bypass CYP2R1. Adequate calcium, phosphorus, and magnesium co-supplementation. Monitor for secondary hyperparathyroidism with PTH levels. Increased risk screening for bone mineral density loss and osteoporosis.

AG.phenotype: Intermediate vitamin D 25-hydroxylation — mildly reduced vitamin D status
AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate CYP2R1 enzyme activity. Modestly reduced 25-hydroxylation capacity with typical 25(OH)D levels 4-6 ng/mL lower than GG homozygotes. Increased susceptibility to vitamin D insufficiency with inadequate intake or sun exposure.
AG.recommendation: Standard vitamin D supplementation (1000-2000 IU daily). Monitor baseline 25(OH)D and recheck annually or with symptoms. Adjust dosing if levels <30 ng/mL. Ensure adequate calcium and magnesium intake.

GA.phenotype: Intermediate vitamin D 25-hydroxylation — mildly reduced vitamin D status
GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate CYP2R1 enzyme activity. Modestly reduced 25-hydroxylation capacity with typical 25(OH)D levels 4-6 ng/mL lower than GG homozygotes. Increased susceptibility to vitamin D insufficiency with inadequate intake or sun exposure.
GA.recommendation: Standard vitamin D supplementation (1000-2000 IU daily). Monitor baseline 25(OH)D and recheck annually or with symptoms. Adjust dosing if levels <30 ng/mL. Ensure adequate calcium and magnesium intake.

GG.effect: Homozygous for the reference allele (GG). Normal CYP2R1 enzyme activity and vitamin D 25-hydroxylation. Efficient conversion of cholecalciferol to 25(OH)D with typical circulating levels achieved through normal supplementation and sun exposure.
GG.recommendation: Standard vitamin D supplementation recommendations. RDA of 600-800 IU daily for most adults or 1000-2000 IU daily for higher-risk groups. Periodic monitoring if symptoms suggestive of insufficiency or in high-risk populations.

gene_description: Cytochrome P450 3A4 — the most abundant hepatic CYP enzyme, responsible for oxidative metabolism of approximately 50% of all clinically prescribed drugs including opioids, benzodiazepines, statins, immunosuppressants, and cannabinoids.

source.title: CYP3A4*22 Genotyping in Clinical Practice: Ready for Implementation?
source.finding: CYP3A4*22 (rs35599367) carriers showed 1.7- to 5-fold decreased CYP3A4 mRNA/protein expression, explaining 12% of CYP3A4 activity variability. Allele frequency 3-5% in Europeans, <1% in Asians.

TT.effect: Homozygous for CYP3A4*22. Markedly decreased CYP3A4 expression (up to 5-fold reduction in mRNA and protein levels). Dramatically reduced metabolism of CYP3A4 substrates leading to significantly elevated plasma concentrations of fentanyl, midazolam, statins, cannabinoids, and many other drugs. Extremely rare genotype (<0.25% in Europeans).
TT.recommendation: Reduce doses of all CYP3A4-metabolized drugs by 50-75%. For fentanyl and opioids, use significantly reduced doses with careful monitoring. Avoid high-dose statins (simvastatin ≤20mg). For cannabidiol, expect prolonged effects. Monitor drug levels when possible.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier of CYP3A4*22. Reduced CYP3A4 enzyme activity (approximately 1.7-2.5-fold decrease in expression). Moderately elevated plasma concentrations of CYP3A4 substrates. Clinically relevant for drugs with narrow therapeutic indices such as fentanyl, tacrolimus, and cyclosporine.
TC.recommendation: Consider 25-50% dose reduction for narrow therapeutic index CYP3A4 substrates. Monitor for enhanced effects of opioids, benzodiazepines, and statins. For tacrolimus/cyclosporine, target lower end of therapeutic drug monitoring range. Avoid grapefruit juice which further inhibits CYP3A4.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier of CYP3A4*22. Reduced CYP3A4 enzyme activity (approximately 1.7-2.5-fold decrease in expression). Moderately elevated plasma concentrations of CYP3A4 substrates. Clinically relevant for drugs with narrow therapeutic indices such as fentanyl, tacrolimus, and cyclosporine.
CT.recommendation: Consider 25-50% dose reduction for narrow therapeutic index CYP3A4 substrates. Monitor for enhanced effects of opioids, benzodiazepines, and statins. For tacrolimus/cyclosporine, target lower end of therapeutic drug monitoring range. Avoid grapefruit juice which further inhibits CYP3A4.

CC.effect: Wild-type CYP3A4 activity. Normal metabolism of all CYP3A4 substrates. Standard pharmacokinetic profiles expected for fentanyl, benzodiazepines, statins, cannabinoids, and other CYP3A4 substrates.
CC.recommendation: Standard dosing per clinical guidelines. Note that CYP3A4 activity is still subject to significant drug-drug interactions and food effects (grapefruit, St. John's wort).

GG.effect: Homozygous for CYP3A4*1B (-392A>G promoter variant). Associated with modestly altered CYP3A4 expression, though functional significance remains debated. Common in African populations. May contribute to interindividual variability in CYP3A4-mediated drug metabolism.
GG.recommendation: Clinical significance of *1B alone is uncertain. Consider in combination with other CYP3A variants. Standard dosing with monitoring is appropriate.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier of the *1B allele. Minimal to modest effect on CYP3A4 activity. Clinical impact likely negligible for most drugs.
GA.recommendation: Standard dosing per clinical guidelines. No dose adjustment recommended based on this variant alone.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier of the *1B allele. Minimal to modest effect on CYP3A4 activity. Clinical impact likely negligible for most drugs.
AG.recommendation: Standard dosing per clinical guidelines. No dose adjustment recommended based on this variant alone.

AA.effect: Wild-type promoter activity. Normal CYP3A4 transcription regulation.
AA.recommendation: Standard dosing per clinical guidelines.

gene_description: Metabolizes tacrolimus, many drugs

source.title: CPIC Guideline for CYP3A5 Genotype and Tacrolimus Dosing
source.finding: CYP3A5 expressers require higher tacrolimus starting doses (0.3 mg/kg/day vs 0.15).

CC.effect: No CYP3A5 expression. Slower tacrolimus metabolism.
CC.recommendation: Standard tacrolimus dose (0.15 mg/kg/day). Standard target trough levels.

CT.effect: Some CYP3A5 expression. Moderately faster tacrolimus metabolism.
CT.recommendation: May need tacrolimus dose increase (0.2-0.25 mg/kg/day). Monitor trough levels.

TT.effect: Full CYP3A5 expression. Rapid tacrolimus metabolism — may not reach therapeutic levels at standard dose.
TT.recommendation: Increase tacrolimus starting dose to 0.3 mg/kg/day. Monitor trough levels closely.

gene_description: DENN Domain Containing 1A (DENND1A) — member of the connecdenn family regulating Rab35-dependent endocytic trafficking and intracellular signaling. The rs2479106 polymorphism is located in an intron of DENND1A and is reproducibly associated with PCOS in Han Chinese and European populations. Risk variants are associated with elevated testosterone production, impaired insulin signaling, and altered follicle development.

source.title: Genome-wide association study identifies susceptibility loci for polycystic ovary syndrome on chromosome 2p16.3, 2p21 and 9q33.3

GG.phenotype: Increased PCOS risk with metabolic dysfunction
GG.effect: Homozygous G-allele carriers (in Asian populations) show elevated PCOS risk (approximately 1.4-1.5x relative risk in Han Chinese; inverse effect in European populations). Associated with elevated serum testosterone and androstenedione, increased 2-hour postglucose insulin levels indicating insulin resistance, impaired glucose tolerance, and follicle maturation defects. GG carriers show increased ovarian theca cell testosterone production, primarily through DENND1A-dependent signaling in endocytic trafficking. Higher rates of metabolic syndrome and diabetes in PCOS patients with GG genotype.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show modest PCOS risk elevation in Asian populations. Intermediate effects on androgen production and insulin sensitivity. Some heterozygotes show borderline elevated testosterone with normal glucose tolerance, representing milder disease phenotypes.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show modest PCOS risk elevation in Asian populations. Intermediate effects on androgen production and insulin sensitivity. Some heterozygotes show borderline elevated testosterone with normal glucose tolerance, representing milder disease phenotypes.

AA.effect: Homozygous A-allele carriers serve as reference group. Normal androgen levels and insulin sensitivity in Asian populations (increased PCOS risk in European populations). Standard PCOS prevalence without specific DENND1A-mediated risk elevation.

gene_description: 7-Dehydrocholesterol Reductase — enzyme that converts 7-dehydrocholesterol (7-DHC) to cholesterol. When DHCR7 activity is reduced, more 7-DHC is available for UV-mediated vitamin D3 synthesis in the skin. Key switch between cholesterol and vitamin D production.

source.title: DHCR7: A vital enzyme switch between cholesterol and vitamin D production
source.finding: DHCR7 rs12785878 is a strong GWAS determinant of circulating 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels. Variants that reduce DHCR7 activity increase 7-DHC substrate availability for vitamin D synthesis. These variants were evolutionarily favored in Northern European populations as adaptation to lower UV exposure.

GG.effect: Homozygous G carriers have higher DHCR7 activity, directing more 7-dehydrocholesterol toward cholesterol synthesis rather than vitamin D3 production. Lower circulating 25(OH)D levels. Greater susceptibility to vitamin D deficiency, especially in Northern latitudes with limited UV exposure. May also have slightly higher cholesterol.
GG.recommendation: Vitamin D supplementation recommended (1000-2000 IU daily), especially in winter and at higher latitudes. Regular 25(OH)D monitoring. Maximize safe sun exposure. Vitamin D-rich foods (fatty fish, fortified dairy). Higher supplementation doses may be needed to achieve optimal levels.

GT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate DHCR7 activity and 7-DHC availability for vitamin D production. Moderate vitamin D levels. Some seasonal variation.
GT.recommendation: Moderate vitamin D supplementation (600-1000 IU daily). Monitor 25(OH)D levels seasonally. Adequate sun exposure and dietary vitamin D.

TG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate DHCR7 activity and 7-DHC availability for vitamin D production. Moderate vitamin D levels. Some seasonal variation.
TG.recommendation: Moderate vitamin D supplementation (600-1000 IU daily). Monitor 25(OH)D levels seasonally. Adequate sun exposure and dietary vitamin D.

TT.effect: Homozygous T carriers have lower DHCR7 activity, leaving more 7-DHC available for UV-mediated vitamin D3 synthesis in skin. Higher circulating 25(OH)D levels. Evolutionarily favorable variant in Northern climates. Naturally better vitamin D status.
TT.recommendation: Favorable vitamin D genetics. Standard sun exposure and dietary vitamin D intake likely sufficient. Supplementation may still be needed at very high latitudes in winter.

gene_description: 5-FU/capecitabine toxicity risk

source.title: CPIC Guideline for Dihydropyrimidine Dehydrogenase and Fluoropyrimidine Dosing
source.finding: DPYD poor metabolizers: reduce fluoropyrimidine dose by 50% or use alternative. Potentially life-threatening toxicity.

AA.effect: Complete DPD deficiency. Life-threatening toxicity with fluoropyrimidines.
AA.recommendation: Avoid fluoropyrimidines entirely. Use alternative chemotherapy.

AG.effect: Partial DPD deficiency. High risk of severe toxicity.
AG.recommendation: Reduce fluoropyrimidine dose by at least 50%. Close monitoring required.

GG.effect: Normal DPD activity. Standard fluoropyrimidine metabolism.
GG.recommendation: Standard dosing.

AA.effect: DPYD*13 homozygous. Complete DPD deficiency. Life-threatening fluoropyrimidine toxicity.
AA.recommendation: Avoid fluoropyrimidines entirely. Use alternative chemotherapy.

AT.effect: DPYD*13 heterozygous. Partial DPD deficiency with high risk of severe toxicity.
AT.recommendation: Reduce fluoropyrimidine dose by at least 50%. Close monitoring required.

TA.effect: DPYD*13 heterozygous. Partial DPD deficiency with high risk of severe toxicity.
TA.recommendation: Reduce fluoropyrimidine dose by at least 50%. Close monitoring required.

TT.effect: No DPYD*13 variant. Normal DPD activity.
TT.recommendation: Standard dosing.

TT.effect: DPYD c.2846A>T homozygous. Complete DPD deficiency. Life-threatening fluoropyrimidine toxicity.
TT.recommendation: Avoid fluoropyrimidines entirely. Use alternative chemotherapy.

AT.effect: DPYD c.2846A>T heterozygous. Partial DPD deficiency with increased toxicity risk.
AT.recommendation: Reduce fluoropyrimidine dose by at least 50%. Close monitoring required.

TA.effect: DPYD c.2846A>T heterozygous. Partial DPD deficiency with increased toxicity risk.
TA.recommendation: Reduce fluoropyrimidine dose by at least 50%. Close monitoring required.

AA.effect: No c.2846A>T variant. Normal DPD activity.
AA.recommendation: Standard dosing.

GG.effect: DPYD HapB3 tag homozygous. Complete DPD deficiency. Life-threatening fluoropyrimidine toxicity.
GG.recommendation: Avoid fluoropyrimidines entirely. Use alternative chemotherapy.

CG.effect: DPYD HapB3 tag heterozygous. Partial DPD deficiency with increased toxicity risk.
CG.recommendation: Reduce fluoropyrimidine dose by at least 50%. Close monitoring required.

GC.effect: DPYD HapB3 tag heterozygous. Partial DPD deficiency with increased toxicity risk.
GC.recommendation: Reduce fluoropyrimidine dose by at least 50%. Close monitoring required.

CC.effect: No HapB3 variant. Normal DPD activity.
CC.recommendation: Standard dosing.

gene_description: Dopamine receptor D2 — primary target of antipsychotic medications; rs1800497 (Taq1A, located in ANKK1 adjacent to DRD2) modulates D2 receptor density in the striatum and influences antipsychotic efficacy and metabolic side effects

source.title: The DRD2 Taq1A polymorphism and its relationship with antipsychotic drug response: a systematic review and meta-analysis
source.finding: DRD2/ANKK1 Taq1A A1 allele (rs1800497 A) carriers have reduced striatal D2 receptor density, associated with diminished antipsychotic response and increased susceptibility to weight gain and hyperprolactinemia during antipsychotic treatment.

AA.effect: Significantly lower striatal D2 receptor availability. Associated with reduced clinical response to typical and atypical antipsychotics, higher risk of antipsychotic-induced weight gain, metabolic syndrome, and hyperprolactinemia. May require higher doses to achieve therapeutic effect, but side effect burden increases.
AA.recommendation: Consider atypical antipsychotics with lower metabolic profiles (aripiprazole, ziprasidone). Monitor weight, glucose, lipids, and prolactin closely. If poor response at standard doses, evaluate adherence and consider switching rather than dose escalation.

AG.effect: Moderately reduced D2 receptor binding. Intermediate antipsychotic response profile. Slightly elevated risk of weight gain compared to GG genotype.
AG.recommendation: Standard antipsychotic dosing. Monitor metabolic parameters (weight, fasting glucose, lipid panel) and prolactin. Adjust therapy based on clinical response and tolerability.

GA.effect: Moderately reduced D2 receptor binding. Intermediate antipsychotic response profile. Slightly elevated risk of weight gain compared to GG genotype.
GA.recommendation: Standard antipsychotic dosing. Monitor metabolic parameters (weight, fasting glucose, lipid panel) and prolactin. Adjust therapy based on clinical response and tolerability.

GG.effect: Normal striatal D2 receptor expression and binding. Expected antipsychotic response at standard doses. Lower baseline risk for antipsychotic-induced metabolic adverse effects related to this locus.
GG.recommendation: Standard antipsychotic prescribing guidelines apply. Routine metabolic monitoring as per treatment protocols.

gene_description: Dopamine D3 Receptor — a dopamine receptor subtype involved in reward processing, motor control, and antipsychotic drug response. The functional Ser9Gly polymorphism (rs6280) affects extracellular N-terminal domain structure and dopamine binding affinity, influencing antipsychotic medication response.

source.title: DRD3 Ser9Gly polymorphism and treatment response to antipsychotics in schizophrenia: A meta-analysis
source.finding: Meta-analysis of 13 studies with 1,769 patients found Ser9Gly polymorphism significantly associated with antipsychotic response in Caucasians (allele model OR=0.72, P=0.002). Ser allele and Ser/Ser genotype associated with poor clozapine response. Association not significant in Asian populations.

AA.effect: Homozygous Ser9 (A-allele) carriers show significantly reduced clozapine and risperidone response. Poor symptom remission rates. Higher rates of treatment resistance. Impaired dopamine D3 receptor sensitivity affects antipsychotic efficacy. More common poor responders in this group in Caucasian populations.
AA.recommendation: Consider higher clozapine doses or combination antipsychotic strategies. Monitor for adequate symptom response at 4-8 weeks; escalate dose if inadequate response. May benefit from augmentation with other agents (lithium, valproate). Close EPS and metabolic monitoring recommended. Consider alternative antipsychotics like aripiprazole which may have better response profile.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers (Ser9/Gly9) show intermediate antipsychotic response. Slightly reduced clozapine efficacy compared to Gly/Gly homozygotes. Variable symptom improvement rates.
AG.recommendation: Standard clozapine dosing with close clinical monitoring. Assess response at 4-6 weeks and optimize dose as needed. Monitor for extrapyramidal side effects and metabolic effects. Standard pharmacological and behavioral interventions.

GG.effect: Wild-type Gly9 homozygotes show better clozapine and antipsychotic response. Higher rates of symptom remission. More sensitive dopamine D3 receptor signaling. Better treatment outcomes in this genotype group.
GG.recommendation: Standard clozapine dosing with expected good response. Monitor at 4-6 weeks and maintain effective dose. Routine monitoring for metabolic and neurological effects. Standard behavioral interventions.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers (Ser9/Gly9) show intermediate antipsychotic response. Slightly reduced clozapine efficacy compared to Gly/Gly homozygotes. Variable symptom improvement rates.
GA.recommendation: Standard clozapine dosing with close clinical monitoring. Assess response at 4-6 weeks and optimize dose as needed. Monitor for extrapyramidal side effects and metabolic effects. Standard pharmacological and behavioral interventions.

gene_description: Dopamine receptor D4 (DRD4) — G protein-coupled dopamine receptor in prefrontal cortex regulating behavioral inhibition, novelty seeking, and reward sensitivity

source.title: Meta-analysis of DRD4 and personality: Evaluating evolutionary hypotheses of Novelty Seeking and Extraversion
source.finding: rs1800955 -521C/T SNP in DRD4 promoter associated with novelty seeking and approach-related personality traits. Meta-analysis supports association explaining ~2% of phenotypic variance. C allele associated with sensation seeking, particularly in sports contexts.

CC.effect: Homozygous for C allele at -521. Enhanced DRD4 promoter activity and higher dopamine D4 receptor expression in prefrontal cortex. Associated with increased novelty-seeking, sensation-seeking, and approach-related personality traits. Stronger attraction to new experiences, thrill-seeking, and risky behaviors. Meta-analysis shows C allele explains ~2% of novelty-seeking variance.
CC.recommendation: Awareness of risk-taking propensity important. Direct energy toward positive outlets (adventure sports, challenging careers, exploration). Be mindful of impulsive decision-making and risk assessment in high-stakes situations. Good candidates for careers requiring boldness and innovation. Substance abuse risk awareness warranted.

CT.effect: Heterozygous for C/T alleles. Intermediate DRD4 expression and novelty-seeking propensity. Balanced approach-related and cautious traits.
CT.recommendation: Standard personality assessment. Individual variation in novelty-seeking noted.

TC.effect: Heterozygous for C/T alleles. Intermediate DRD4 expression and novelty-seeking propensity. Balanced approach-related and cautious traits.
TC.recommendation: Standard personality assessment. Individual variation in novelty-seeking noted.

TT.effect: Homozygous for T allele at -521 (ancestral). Lower DRD4 expression and reduced novelty-seeking propensity. More cautious, less impulsive approach to new experiences. Preference for stability and familiar environments.
TT.recommendation: Natural cautious temperament. Suited for detailed, analytical work requiring careful risk assessment. Less attraction to thrill-seeking activities. Standard personality profile.

gene_description: Dopamine Receptor D4 — G-protein coupled receptor highly expressed in the prefrontal cortex, implicated in ADHD susceptibility and modulating stimulant medication response.

source.title: Pharmacogenetics of methylphenidate in childhood attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder: long-term effects
source.finding: DRD4 rs1800955 -521C/T promoter variant influenced long-term methylphenidate efficacy in ADHD children, with the T allele associated with altered DRD4 expression and variable stimulant response.

TT.effect: Homozygous for the T allele at the DRD4 -521 promoter position. This variant increases DRD4 transcription, altering dopaminergic signaling in the prefrontal cortex. Associated with variable long-term methylphenidate response in ADHD, potentially requiring more careful dose titration. DRD4 variants also contribute to ADHD susceptibility itself.
TT.recommendation: Initiate stimulant therapy at standard dose but monitor response carefully over the first 4-8 weeks. If methylphenidate response is inconsistent, consider amphetamine-based alternatives or combination with behavioral therapy. Long-term follow-up recommended.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier. One copy of the T allele with intermediate DRD4 promoter activity. Moderate influence on stimulant medication response with generally adequate treatment outcomes.
TC.recommendation: Standard stimulant dosing is appropriate. Monitor treatment response during initial titration and long-term maintenance.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier. One copy of the T allele with intermediate DRD4 promoter activity. Moderate influence on stimulant medication response with generally adequate treatment outcomes.
CT.recommendation: Standard stimulant dosing is appropriate. Monitor treatment response during initial titration and long-term maintenance.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the reference C allele. Normal DRD4 promoter activity and standard dopaminergic signaling. Expected typical response to stimulant medications.
CC.recommendation: Standard ADHD stimulant dosing per clinical guidelines. No pharmacogenomic adjustment needed.

gene_description: Ectodysplasin A receptor (EDAR) — signaling receptor controlling hair follicle development and fiber characteristics in East Asian populations

source.title: Enhanced ectodysplasin-A receptor (EDAR) signaling alters multiple fiber characteristics to produce the East Asian hair form
source.finding: rs3827760 V370A variant creates EDAR370Ala (enhanced signaling). G allele frequency 87.6% in East Asians vs 0% in Europeans/Africans. Associated with thicker hair shafts, straighter hair, more sweat glands, and modified tooth/ear morphology.

AA.effect: Homozygous for alanine at position 370 (enhanced EDAR signaling). Associated with thick, straight hair fibers with larger shaft diameter. Predominant in East Asian, Northeast Asian, and Native American populations due to ancient positive selection. Enhanced sweat gland density and modified ear/tooth morphology.
AA.recommendation: No clinical action needed. Hair typically requires less styling product due to natural thickness and straightness.

AG.effect: Heterozygous for mixed alanine/valine alleles. Intermediate hair thickness and straightness phenotype. Enhanced EDAR signaling but not to maximal level. Common in admixed populations and some South Asian populations.
AG.recommendation: No clinical action needed. Hair care tailored to individual phenotype presentation.

GA.effect: Heterozygous for mixed alanine/valine alleles. Intermediate hair thickness and straightness phenotype. Enhanced EDAR signaling but not to maximal level. Common in admixed populations and some South Asian populations.
GA.recommendation: No clinical action needed. Hair care tailored to individual phenotype presentation.

GG.effect: Homozygous for valine at position 370 (ancestral, normal EDAR signaling). Standard hair thickness and variable morphology (straight to wavy/curly). Predominant in European and African populations. Normal sweat gland density.
GG.recommendation: No clinical action needed. Hair care depends on individual hair characteristics.

gene_description: EGFR (Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor) encodes a transmembrane receptor tyrosine kinase. Activating mutations (particularly L858R and exon 19 deletions) in exons 18-21 predict excellent response to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors in lung cancer.

source.title: Gefitinib or carboplatin-paclitaxel in pulmonary adenocarcinoma
source.finding: EGFR mutations (L858R and exon 19 deletions) strongly predict gefitinib response; first demonstration that targeted therapy superior to chemotherapy in NSCLC

GG.phenotype: Homozygous tag allele associated with activating EGFR mutations
GG.effect: Strong predictor of EGFR-activating mutations; excellent TKI response expected
GG.recommendation: First-line osimertinib monotherapy or osimertinib + chemotherapy. Monitor for T790M resistance mutations. Consider 3rd-generation TKI upfront.

GA.effect: Intermediate predictor of EGFR mutations; likely TKI response
GA.recommendation: Genotype for specific L858R/exon19 mutations. If positive, treat with EGFR TKI. Consider combination with chemotherapy in some cases.

AG.effect: Intermediate predictor of EGFR mutations; likely TKI response
AG.recommendation: Genotype for specific L858R/exon19 mutations. If positive, treat with EGFR TKI. Consider combination with chemotherapy in some cases.

AA.effect: Lower likelihood of activating EGFR mutations; standard chemotherapy more likely appropriate
AA.recommendation: Perform targeted genotyping for EGFR mutations. If wild-type, consider chemotherapy or immunotherapy. Evaluate for other driver mutations (KRAS, ALK, ROS1).

clinical_significance: Landmark IPASS trial established EGFR mutation testing as standard-of-care companion diagnostic for NSCLC. Mutations confer 40-50% response rates to TKIs.

gene_description: Endothelial Per-Arnt-Sim (PAS) domain protein 1 (EPAS1/HIF-2a) — oxygen-dependent transcription factor regulating erythropoietin production and hypoxic cellular response

source.title: Genetic variants in EPAS1 contribute to adaptation to high-altitude hypoxia in Sherpas
source.finding: rs1868092 and rs4953354 SNPs significantly associated with altitude adaptation in Tibetan and Sherpa populations. Derived alleles reduce hemoglobin levels and risk of high-altitude polycythemia despite hypoxia. Evidence of ancient positive selection.

GG.effect: Homozygous for G allele. Optimized HIF-2a function for chronic hypoxia response. Controlled erythropoietin production prevents excessive hemoglobin elevation. Reduced risk of high-altitude polycythemia despite living at high altitude. Found at high frequency in Tibetan and Sherpa populations due to ancient positive selection (~10,000+ years). Maintains adequate oxygen delivery without pathological hemoglobin elevation.
GG.recommendation: Good tolerance for high-altitude living. Minimal risk for high-altitude polycythemia. Standard high-altitude precautions still appropriate (hydration, gradual ascent acclimatization).

AG.effect: Heterozygous for G/A alleles. Intermediate HIF-2a responsiveness and erythropoietin regulation. Moderate altitude tolerance with intermediate hemoglobin response to hypoxia. Common in admixed populations with recent altitude exposure.
AG.recommendation: High-altitude living possible but may require gradual acclimatization. Monitor hemoglobin/hematocrit if living at high altitude chronically.

GA.effect: Heterozygous for G/A alleles. Intermediate HIF-2a responsiveness and erythropoietin regulation. Moderate altitude tolerance with intermediate hemoglobin response to hypoxia. Common in admixed populations with recent altitude exposure.
GA.recommendation: High-altitude living possible but may require gradual acclimatization. Monitor hemoglobin/hematocrit if living at high altitude chronically.

AA.effect: Homozygous for A allele (ancestral). Standard HIF-2a and erythropoietin response to hypoxia. Normal hemoglobin elevation at high altitude. Higher risk of high-altitude polycythemia with chronic hypoxia exposure, as hemoglobin can rise excessively (>20 g/dL).
AA.recommendation: High-altitude living possible but requires careful monitoring. Gradual acclimatization essential. Monitor hemoglobin/hematocrit regularly if living at high altitude (>2,500m). Consider iron status and hydration. Phlebotomy may be needed if hemoglobin becomes elevated (>20 g/dL).

gene_description: Estrogen receptor alpha (ESR1) — nuclear receptor for estrogen; harbors PvuII polymorphism that modulates receptor expression and estrogen-mediated effects; significant for tamoxifen response in breast cancer

source.title: The role of ESR1 gene polymorphic markers in breast cancer development and tamoxifen therapy resistance
source.finding: rs2234693 T allele (PvuII) associated with better disease-free survival on tamoxifen in breast cancer (HR 0.69, 95% CI 0.48-0.99); prognostic marker independent of therapy

CC.effect: Homozygous C carriers have lower ESR1 expression and potentially altered estrogen receptor function. Associated with worse disease-free survival in tamoxifen-treated breast cancer patients in some studies. C alleles linked to reduced ER-mediated transcription.
CC.recommendation: For hormone receptor positive breast cancer: close monitoring recommended. Consider alternative endocrine therapies (aromatase inhibitors, fulvestrant) as first-line option. Regular surveillance for recurrence. Discuss prognosis and individualized treatment planning with oncologist.

CT.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate ESR1 expression levels. Mixed results on tamoxifen efficacy; intermediate prognosis in some populations.
CT.recommendation: Standard tamoxifen dosing appropriate (20 mg daily) if selected as endocrine therapy. Monitor response to therapy and compliance. Regular oncology follow-up and surveillance per standard breast cancer protocols.

TC.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate ESR1 expression levels. Mixed results on tamoxifen efficacy; intermediate prognosis in some populations.
