TC.recommendation: Standard tamoxifen dosing appropriate (20 mg daily) if selected as endocrine therapy. Monitor response to therapy and compliance. Regular oncology follow-up and surveillance per standard breast cancer protocols.

TT.effect: Wild-type TT carriers have higher ESR1 expression. Associated with better disease-free survival on tamoxifen therapy in early breast cancer. Better prognostic marker for endocrine therapy response.
TT.recommendation: Standard tamoxifen dosing (20 mg daily) appropriate for hormone receptor positive breast cancer. Favorable prognosis with tamoxifen therapy. Standard surveillance protocols per oncology guidelines.

gene_description: Coagulation Factor II (Prothrombin) — key protein in the coagulation cascade. The G20210A variant leads to elevated prothrombin levels and increased thrombotic risk, impacting anticoagulant therapy decisions.

source.title: Prothrombin G20210A (rs1799963) polymorphism increases myocardial infarction risk in an age-related manner: a systematic review and meta-analysis
source.finding: F2 G20210A (rs1799963) carriers have 2-5 fold increased risk of venous thromboembolism and myocardial infarction. Heterozygous carriers have elevated prothrombin plasma levels (~30% higher), necessitating careful anticoagulation management.

AA.effect: Extremely rare homozygous state. Very high prothrombin plasma levels and markedly elevated thrombotic risk (estimated >10-fold). Significant risk of recurrent VTE, DVT, and pulmonary embolism. May require lifelong anticoagulation.
AA.recommendation: Refer to hematologist. Long-term or lifelong anticoagulation therapy strongly recommended. Avoid estrogen-containing oral contraceptives. Consider thromboprophylaxis during high-risk situations (surgery, immobilization, pregnancy).

GA.effect: Carrier of one copy of G20210A. Prothrombin plasma levels ~30% higher than normal. 2-5 fold increased risk of venous thromboembolism (DVT, PE). 3-4 fold increased thrombosis risk in children. Risk further increased by combined oral contraceptives, smoking, obesity, and immobilization.
GA.recommendation: Anticoagulation for 3-6 months following thrombotic event. Avoid estrogen-containing contraceptives; consider progestin-only alternatives. Thromboprophylaxis during surgery/prolonged immobilization. Genetic counseling for family members. Choice of anticoagulant (warfarin vs DOAC) based on clinical context.

AG.effect: Carrier of one copy of G20210A. Prothrombin plasma levels ~30% higher than normal. 2-5 fold increased risk of venous thromboembolism (DVT, PE). 3-4 fold increased thrombosis risk in children. Risk further increased by combined oral contraceptives, smoking, obesity, and immobilization.
AG.recommendation: Anticoagulation for 3-6 months following thrombotic event. Avoid estrogen-containing contraceptives; consider progestin-only alternatives. Thromboprophylaxis during surgery/prolonged immobilization. Genetic counseling for family members. Choice of anticoagulant (warfarin vs DOAC) based on clinical context.

GG.effect: No G20210A mutation. Normal prothrombin levels and standard thrombotic risk from this locus. Other genetic and environmental factors still influence individual thrombosis risk.
GG.recommendation: Standard clinical assessment for thrombotic risk. No F2-specific anticoagulation adjustments needed.

gene_description: Coagulation Factor V — essential cofactor in the prothrombinase complex. The Factor V Leiden mutation (R506Q) causes resistance to activated protein C, leading to a hypercoagulable state and increased VTE risk.

source.title: Factor V Leiden Thrombophilia
source.finding: Factor V Leiden (rs6025, R506Q) is the most common inherited thrombophilia. Heterozygous carriers have ~3-8 fold increased VTE risk; homozygous carriers have ~33-80 fold increased risk. Impacts anticoagulant treatment duration decisions.

TT.effect: Homozygous R506Q mutation causing complete resistance to activated protein C (APC). 33-80 fold increased risk of venous thromboembolism. High risk of recurrent DVT, pulmonary embolism, and potentially cerebral vein thrombosis. Risk compounded by oral contraceptives, pregnancy, surgery, and immobilization.
TT.recommendation: Refer to hematologist. Long-term or lifelong anticoagulation therapy strongly recommended after first thrombotic event. Absolutely avoid estrogen-containing contraceptives. Thromboprophylaxis for all high-risk situations. Genetic counseling for family members. DOACs or warfarin based on clinical judgment.

CT.effect: One copy of R506Q mutation causing partial APC resistance. 3-8 fold increased risk of venous thromboembolism. Risk is substantially increased by combined oral contraceptives (~35-fold with OCP use), pregnancy (~7-16 fold), surgery, trauma, and immobilization. Most carriers never develop thrombosis without additional risk factors.
CT.recommendation: Anticoagulation for VTE events (duration based on clinical context, typically 3-6 months for provoked events). Avoid estrogen-containing contraceptives; progestin-only or non-hormonal alternatives preferred. Thromboprophylaxis during surgery and prolonged immobilization. Pregnancy management in consultation with hematologist.

TC.effect: One copy of R506Q mutation causing partial APC resistance. 3-8 fold increased risk of venous thromboembolism. Risk is substantially increased by combined oral contraceptives (~35-fold with OCP use), pregnancy (~7-16 fold), surgery, trauma, and immobilization. Most carriers never develop thrombosis without additional risk factors.
TC.recommendation: Anticoagulation for VTE events (duration based on clinical context, typically 3-6 months for provoked events). Avoid estrogen-containing contraceptives; progestin-only or non-hormonal alternatives preferred. Thromboprophylaxis during surgery and prolonged immobilization. Pregnancy management in consultation with hematologist.

CC.effect: No Factor V Leiden mutation. Normal activated protein C sensitivity. Standard thrombotic risk from this locus.
CC.recommendation: Standard clinical assessment for thrombotic risk. No F5-specific anticoagulation adjustments needed.

gene_description: Fatty Acid Amide Hydrolase — primary enzyme responsible for degradation of the endocannabinoid anandamide (AEA). Loss-of-function variants elevate endocannabinoid tone, modulating cannabinoid therapy response and substance use vulnerability.

source.title: Severity of alcohol dependence is associated with the fatty acid amide hydrolase Pro129Thr missense variant
source.finding: FAAH Pro129Thr (rs324420 A allele) reduces FAAH protein stability and elevates anandamide levels, associated with differential susceptibility to substance use disorders, anxiety modulation, and altered endocannabinoid signaling.

AA.effect: Homozygous for the Thr129 variant (Pro129Thr). Markedly reduced FAAH protein stability and enzymatic activity, leading to significantly elevated anandamide levels. Enhanced endogenous cannabinoid signaling may alter response to exogenous cannabinoids (CBD, THC). Associated with increased vulnerability to substance use disorders, altered anxiety reactivity, and modified pain perception.
AA.recommendation: Expect enhanced sensitivity to cannabinoid-based medications due to elevated endocannabinoid baseline. Start at lower doses of CBD/THC preparations. Monitor for excessive sedation or anxiolytic effects. Screen for substance use disorder risk. May benefit from lower CBD doses for anxiety management.

AC.effect: Heterozygous carrier (Pro/Thr). Partially reduced FAAH activity with moderately elevated anandamide levels. Intermediate endocannabinoid tone compared to wild-type. May show slightly enhanced responses to cannabinoid medications.
AC.recommendation: Standard cannabinoid dosing generally appropriate. Monitor for enhanced or prolonged effects. Consider individual variation in response during dose titration.

CA.effect: Heterozygous carrier (Pro/Thr). Partially reduced FAAH activity with moderately elevated anandamide levels. Intermediate endocannabinoid tone compared to wild-type. May show slightly enhanced responses to cannabinoid medications.
CA.recommendation: Standard cannabinoid dosing generally appropriate. Monitor for enhanced or prolonged effects. Consider individual variation in response during dose titration.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the Pro129 wild-type allele. Normal FAAH enzymatic function and anandamide degradation. Standard endocannabinoid tone and typical response to cannabinoid-based medications.
CC.recommendation: Standard dosing for cannabinoid preparations per clinical guidelines.

gene_description: Fatty Acid Binding Protein 2 (Intestinal) — exclusively expressed in intestinal enterocytes, mediates intracellular transport and absorption of dietary long-chain fatty acids. The Ala54Thr variant increases fat absorption efficiency.

source.title: The polymorphism at codon 54 of the FABP2 gene increases fat absorption in human intestinal explants
source.finding: FABP2 Ala54Thr (rs1799883) Thr-encoding allele increases intestinal triglyceride secretion, de novo apoB synthesis, and chylomicron output. The Thr54 protein has ~2-fold higher affinity for long-chain fatty acids, leading to enhanced fat absorption.

AA.effect: Homozygous Thr54 carriers have highest intestinal fat absorption efficiency (~2-fold higher fatty acid binding affinity). Increased triglyceride secretion, chylomicron output, and postprandial lipemia. Associated with increased risk of insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia, metabolic syndrome, and obesity. Higher fat oxidation rates but also hyperinsulinemia.
AA.recommendation: Moderate dietary fat intake (25-30% of calories). Favor unsaturated over saturated fats. Higher fiber intake to slow fat absorption. Consider orlistat if obese. Monitor postprandial triglycerides. Low-glycemic index foods to manage insulin response.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate fat absorption efficiency. Mildly elevated postprandial triglycerides and moderate metabolic risk increase. Interaction with high-saturated-fat diet affects lipid levels and insulin resistance.
GA.recommendation: Heart-healthy fat intake. Limit saturated fat. Regular metabolic screening.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate fat absorption efficiency. Mildly elevated postprandial triglycerides and moderate metabolic risk increase. Interaction with high-saturated-fat diet affects lipid levels and insulin resistance.
AG.recommendation: Heart-healthy fat intake. Limit saturated fat. Regular metabolic screening.

GG.effect: Wild-type with standard fatty acid binding affinity and normal intestinal fat absorption. Standard postprandial triglyceride clearance.
GG.recommendation: Standard dietary guidelines.

gene_description: Fatty Acid Desaturase 1 — encodes delta-5 desaturase (D5D), a key enzyme in the conversion of essential fatty acids (omega-3 and omega-6) to their long-chain polyunsaturated forms (EPA, DHA, arachidonic acid).

source.title: Joint effects of fatty acid desaturase 1 polymorphisms and dietary polyunsaturated fatty acid intake on circulating fatty acid proportions
source.finding: FADS1 rs174546 T allele carriers have lower delta-5 desaturase activity, resulting in reduced conversion of ALA to EPA/DHA and linoleic acid to arachidonic acid. These individuals benefit more from preformed EPA/DHA supplementation (fish oil) than from plant-based ALA sources.

TT.effect: Homozygous ancestral allele carriers have significantly lower FADS1 activity. Reduced conversion of alpha-linolenic acid (ALA) to EPA and DHA, and of linoleic acid to arachidonic acid. Lower circulating EPA, DHA, and arachidonic acid levels. Higher linoleic acid and DGLA levels. May need higher dietary EPA/DHA to achieve optimal omega-3 status.
TT.recommendation: Supplement with preformed EPA+DHA (fish oil, algae oil) rather than relying on plant-based ALA sources (flaxseed, chia). Target 1-2g EPA+DHA daily. Fatty fish 2-3x per week. ALA from plant sources will be poorly converted. Consider omega-3 index testing.

CT.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate delta-5 desaturase activity. Moderate conversion efficiency of essential fatty acids. Intermediate circulating EPA/DHA levels.
CT.recommendation: Include fatty fish regularly (2x per week minimum). May benefit from EPA/DHA supplementation (500mg-1g daily). Plant-based ALA sources provide some but incomplete conversion.

TC.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate delta-5 desaturase activity. Moderate conversion efficiency of essential fatty acids. Intermediate circulating EPA/DHA levels.
TC.recommendation: Include fatty fish regularly (2x per week minimum). May benefit from EPA/DHA supplementation (500mg-1g daily). Plant-based ALA sources provide some but incomplete conversion.

CC.effect: Derived allele homozygotes have highest FADS1 activity. Efficient conversion of ALA to EPA/DHA. Higher arachidonic acid levels which may promote inflammatory pathways. Lower linoleic acid levels.
CC.recommendation: Standard omega-3 intake from mixed sources (both plant and marine). Be mindful of omega-6 to omega-3 ratio as efficient arachidonic acid production may promote inflammation with excess omega-6 intake.

source.title: Genetic Variants in FBN-1 and Risk for Thoracic Aortic Aneurysm and Dissection

description: FBN1 rs2118181 is associated with thoracic aortic dissection and aortic aneurysm. The minor G allele increases TGF-β1 levels and increases aortic disease risk. Carriers have increased risk of aortic dilation.

AA.phenotype: Lower aortic aneurysm and dissection risk
AA.effect: Reference homozygote
AA.recommendation: Standard aortic screening based on family history; healthy lifestyle

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier; ~1.8-1.9x increased risk
AG.recommendation: Baseline aortic imaging (ultrasound or CT); periodic monitoring; manage hypertension closely

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier; ~1.8-1.9x increased risk
GA.recommendation: Baseline aortic imaging (ultrasound or CT); periodic monitoring; manage hypertension closely

GG.phenotype: Elevated aortic aneurysm and dissection risk
GG.effect: Homozygous risk allele; cumulative increased risk
GG.recommendation: Baseline aortic imaging; regular monitoring every 1-2 years; aggressive BP control; avoid contact sports; genetic counseling recommended

gene_description: FK506 Binding Protein 5 — co-chaperone that modulates glucocorticoid receptor sensitivity in the HPA axis. Regulates cortisol feedback and stress response, influencing antidepressant treatment outcomes.

source.title: The FKBP5 polymorphism rs1360780 influences the effect of an algorithm-based antidepressant treatment and is associated with remission in patients with major depression
source.finding: C-allele carriers of rs1360780 showed significantly better remission rates with algorithm-based antidepressant treatment. T-allele associated with higher FKBP5 induction by glucocorticoids and impaired HPA axis feedback.

TT.phenotype: Impaired Antidepressant Response — HPA Axis Dysregulation
TT.effect: Homozygous for the T allele. Higher FKBP5 induction by glucocorticoids, resulting in reduced glucocorticoid receptor sensitivity and impaired cortisol negative feedback. Associated with increased depression severity, greater vulnerability to childhood trauma effects, and reduced antidepressant remission rates. FKBP5 expression increases in non-responders and the T allele amplifies this effect.
TT.recommendation: Consider that standard antidepressant monotherapy may be less effective. Augmentation strategies (lithium, aripiprazole) or combination antidepressant therapy may be needed earlier. Psychotherapy addressing stress response (CBT, mindfulness) may be particularly beneficial. Monitor HPA axis function and cortisol patterns if clinically indicated.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate FKBP5 induction levels and partially impaired glucocorticoid receptor feedback. Moderate effect on antidepressant response. Gene-environment interaction with childhood adversity modifies risk.
TC.recommendation: Standard antidepressant dosing with careful monitoring. Allow adequate trial duration. Consider augmentation if response is partial after 6-8 weeks.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate FKBP5 induction levels and partially impaired glucocorticoid receptor feedback. Moderate effect on antidepressant response. Gene-environment interaction with childhood adversity modifies risk.
CT.recommendation: Standard antidepressant dosing with careful monitoring. Allow adequate trial duration. Consider augmentation if response is partial after 6-8 weeks.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the C allele. Normal FKBP5 expression regulation and intact glucocorticoid receptor feedback. Better remission rates with algorithm-based antidepressant treatment. Appropriate cortisol response normalization during successful treatment.
CC.recommendation: Standard antidepressant treatment expected to be effective. Good likelihood of achieving remission with adequate dosing and duration.

description: FLG rs61816761 (R501X) loss-of-function variant affecting the filaggrin protein critical for skin barrier function. Homozygous loss-of-function variants confer ~7x increased eczema risk and impaired epidermal barrier, with secondary increased asthma risk.

source.title: Common loss-of-function variants of the epidermal barrier protein filaggrin are a major predisposing factor for atopic dermatitis
source.authors: Palmer CN, Irvine AD, Terron-Kwiatkowski A, et al.

AA.phenotype: High genetic susceptibility to atopic dermatitis with severely impaired skin barrier
AA.effect: High genetic susceptibility to atopic dermatitis with severely impaired skin barrier
AA.recommendation: Aggressive skin care with frequent emolliation and ceramide-rich moisturizers. Early recognition and treatment of eczema flares. Topical corticosteroids or calcineurin inhibitors as first-line. Consider dupilumab or other biologics for moderate-severe disease. Monitor for asthma and allergic rhinitis. Avoid allergen triggers.

AG.phenotype: Moderate increased genetic susceptibility to atopic dermatitis
AG.effect: Moderate increased genetic susceptibility to atopic dermatitis
AG.recommendation: Regular skin care with emollients. Early treatment of eczema flares with topical agents. Avoid irritants and allergens. Monitor for asthma development. Dupilumab or tacrolimus/pimecrolimus for moderate disease.

GA.phenotype: Moderate increased genetic susceptibility to atopic dermatitis
GA.effect: Moderate increased genetic susceptibility to atopic dermatitis
GA.recommendation: Regular skin care with emollients. Early treatment of eczema flares with topical agents. Avoid irritants and allergens. Monitor for asthma development. Dupilumab or tacrolimus/pimecrolimus for moderate disease.

GG.phenotype: Normal skin barrier function; no increased FLG-related eczema risk
GG.effect: Normal skin barrier function; no increased FLG-related eczema risk
GG.recommendation: Standard dermatologic care if other causes of eczema present; genetic predisposition via FLG not a factor

title: FOXE1 rs965513 Thyroid Cancer Risk (Transcription Factor)
description: Common variant in the FOXE1 transcription factor region strongly associated with differentiated thyroid cancer risk in European populations. FOXE1 encodes a thyroid-specific transcription factor critical for thyroid development and function. The A risk allele increases susceptibility to papillary and follicular thyroid carcinoma.

source.title: Common genetic variants on FOXE1 contribute to thyroid cancer susceptibility

GG.effect: baseline_thyroid_cancer_risk
GG.recommendation: standard_surveillance

GA.effect: 1.62x_increased_thyroid_cancer_risk
GA.recommendation: enhanced_TSH_monitoring_ultrasound_surveillance

AG.effect: 1.62x_increased_thyroid_cancer_risk
AG.recommendation: enhanced_TSH_monitoring_ultrasound_surveillance

AA.effect: 1.74x_increased_thyroid_cancer_risk
AA.recommendation: aggressive_screening_aggressive_treatment

description: Forkhead box O3 genetic variation and human longevity

source.title: FOXO3A genotype is strongly associated with human longevity
source.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences USA
source.authors: Willcox BJ, Donlon TA, He Q, et al.

GG.effect: Homozygous for the G allele (risk allele). Associated with lower FOXO3 expression, standard aging profile, lower FOXO3-mediated stress resistance, reduced longevity association.
GG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GT.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate FOXO3 expression and longevity phenotype.

TG.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate FOXO3 expression and longevity phenotype.

TT.effect: Homozygous for the T allele (reference/protective allele). Associated with enhanced FOXO3 expression and activity, improved stress resistance, cellular quality control, and strong longevity association. Associated with lower prevalence of cardiovascular disease and cancer.
TT.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

gene_description: Follicle-stimulating hormone beta subunit (FSHB) — regulates FSH production and ovarian folliculogenesis, determines ovulation rate and twinning propensity

source.title: Biological insights into multiple birth: genome-wide association study of twinning
source.finding: rs11031006-G allele increases FSH levels and twin/multiple pregnancy risk. Associated with earlier menarche, earlier age at first child, higher lifetime parity. Effect size ~40% increased dizygotic twinning in carriers.

GG.phenotype: High FSH, Increased Twin Pregnancy Risk (Risk/Risk)
GG.effect: Homozygous for G allele. Elevated circulating FSH levels (~1.5-2x higher). Increased propensity for multiple ovulation per cycle, substantially increasing spontaneous dizygotic (fraternal) twinning probability. Associated with earlier menarche (~6 months earlier) and earlier age at first birth.
GG.recommendation: Awareness of increased twinning risk important for reproductive planning. Genetic counseling recommended if family history of multiples. Fertility treatments may result in higher risk of multiple gestation. Consider family planning timeline and resources for potential multiples.

AG.phenotype: Intermediate FSH, Moderately Increased Twin Risk (Heterozygous)
AG.effect: Heterozygous for G/A alleles. Moderately elevated FSH levels. Intermediate increased twinning propensity compared to AA genotype. Associated with slightly earlier reproductive milestones.
AG.recommendation: Modest awareness of twinning possibility. Standard reproductive planning applies. No special precautions needed.

GA.phenotype: Intermediate FSH, Moderately Increased Twin Risk (Heterozygous)
GA.effect: Heterozygous for G/A alleles. Moderately elevated FSH levels. Intermediate increased twinning propensity compared to AA genotype. Associated with slightly earlier reproductive milestones.
GA.recommendation: Modest awareness of twinning possibility. Standard reproductive planning applies. No special precautions needed.

AA.effect: Homozygous for A allele (ancestral). Normal circulating FSH levels and ovulation rates. Standard baseline dizygotic twinning probability (~1-2% in most populations).
AA.recommendation: Standard reproductive counseling applies. No increased twinning risk related to this locus.

gene_description: Fat mass and obesity–associated gene — common intron variant linked to BMI, fat mass, and obesity risk

source.title: A common variant in the FTO gene is associated with body mass index and predisposes to childhood and adult obesity
source.finding: rs9939609 A allele associates with higher BMI and ~1.67x obesity odds for AA vs TT in large European meta-analysis; effect on fat mass from childhood.

TT.effect: No A allele at rs9939609. On average lower BMI association than A carriers in population studies (environment and behavior still dominate).
TT.recommendation: Standard healthy weight guidance; genetics are not destiny.

AT.effect: Per-allele A associated with modestly higher BMI and obesity odds in replicated GWAS.
AT.recommendation: Structured diet/activity and sleep help offset polygenic obesity tendency.

TA.effect: Per-allele A associated with modestly higher BMI and obesity odds in replicated GWAS.
TA.recommendation: Structured diet/activity and sleep help offset polygenic obesity tendency.

AA.effect: Homozygous risk allele; strongest association with BMI/fat mass in discovery/replication cohorts (~3 kg heavier on average vs TT in Frayling et al.).
AA.recommendation: Emphasize sustainable caloric control, protein/fiber, resistance training, and sleep; medical support if obesity complicates health.

gene_description: B12 absorption (secretor status)

source.title: FUT2 nonsecretor status and vitamin B12 levels
source.finding: FUT2 nonsecretors (AA at rs601338) have lower B12 levels. Consider supplementation.

AA.effect: Lower vitamin B12 absorption. Reduced gut binding factor secretion.
AA.recommendation: Consider sublingual methylcobalamin supplementation. Monitor B12 levels.

AG.effect: Normal B12 absorption. One functional copy sufficient.
AG.recommendation: Standard dietary B12 intake sufficient.

GG.effect: Normal B12 absorption.
GG.recommendation: Standard dietary B12 intake sufficient.

description: G6PD rs1050828 (Val98Met, A- variant) X-linked enzyme deficiency affecting ability to metabolize oxidant drugs. The A- variant reduces G6PD enzyme activity to 10-40% of normal, causing hemolytic anemia when exposed to oxidative stressors including certain medications.

source.title: G6PD Deficiency Classification and variants in sub-Saharan African populations
source.authors: Howes RE, Bockarie MJ, Mwangi TW, et al.

TT.phenotype: G6PD deficiency with severe enzyme activity reduction; high risk of hemolytic anemia with oxidant exposure
TT.effect: G6PD deficiency with severe enzyme activity reduction; high risk of hemolytic anemia with oxidant exposure
TT.recommendation: Avoid oxidative triggers: primaquine, dapsone, rasburicase, methylene blue, nitrofurantoin, aspirin (high dose), sulfonamides. Screen before use of these drugs. Counsel on fava bean avoidance.

TC.phenotype: G6PD carrier with intermediate activity; moderate hemolysis risk with strong oxidant exposure
TC.effect: G6PD carrier with intermediate activity; moderate hemolysis risk with strong oxidant exposure
TC.recommendation: Exercise caution with oxidant drugs; dose adjustment or monitoring recommended. Avoid high-dose aspirin and sulfonamides. Screen before prescribing primaquine or other high-risk drugs.

CT.phenotype: G6PD carrier with intermediate activity; moderate hemolysis risk with strong oxidant exposure
CT.effect: G6PD carrier with intermediate activity; moderate hemolysis risk with strong oxidant exposure
CT.recommendation: Exercise caution with oxidant drugs; dose adjustment or monitoring recommended. Avoid high-dose aspirin and sulfonamides. Screen before prescribing primaquine or other high-risk drugs.

CC.phenotype: Normal G6PD activity; no special risk from oxidant medications
CC.effect: Normal G6PD activity; no special risk from oxidant medications
CC.recommendation: No specific G6PD-related restrictions; standard dosing of medications is appropriate

gene_description: GABA(A) receptor subunit alpha 2 — component of GABA(A) chloride channels. Variants alter benzodiazepine receptor binding affinity and GABAergic neurotransmission, affecting response to benzodiazepines and susceptibility to alcohol dependence and anxiety disorders.

source.title: GABRA2 Alcohol Dependence Risk Allele is Associated with Reduced Expression of Chromosome 4p12 GABAA Subunit Genes in Human Neural Cultures
source.finding: GABRA2 rs279858 C-allele carriers show significantly reduced GABRA2 mRNA expression in neural cultures; C-allele associated with increased subjective effects of alcohol ('feeling' and 'liking' alcohol effects) and elevated risk of alcohol dependence (p < 0.001).

CC.effect: Significantly reduced GABRA2 gene expression in neural tissue (~30-40% lower mRNA levels compared to T-allele carriers). Homozygous C-carriers show heightened subjective response to alcohol ('feel the effect' ratings 2-3 fold higher). Associated with reduced GABAergic inhibition, increasing vulnerability to benzodiazepine tolerance and cross-addiction. Elevated risk of alcohol dependence (OR~1.8-2.0) and anxiety disorders.
CC.recommendation: Cautious benzodiazepine use; consider lower initial doses or shorter-acting agents. Enhanced monitoring for tolerance development and dependence. Consider non-GABAergic anxiolytics (SSRIs, buspirone) as first-line alternatives. Educate regarding alcohol-benzodiazepine interaction risk and cross-addiction potential. Avoid concurrent alcohol use.

CT.effect: Intermediate GABRA2 expression levels. Moderate subjective response to alcohol. Intermediate risk for benzodiazepine tolerance and alcohol dependence (OR~1.2). Some individuals show enhanced benzodiazepine sensitivity; others show reduced response.
CT.recommendation: Standard benzodiazepine dosing with baseline monitoring for response and tolerance. Periodic reassessment of clinical efficacy. Counsel regarding alcohol interaction. Monitor for signs of dependence or tolerance development.

TC.effect: Intermediate GABRA2 expression levels. Moderate subjective response to alcohol. Intermediate risk for benzodiazepine tolerance and alcohol dependence (OR~1.2). Some individuals show enhanced benzodiazepine sensitivity; others show reduced response.
TC.recommendation: Standard benzodiazepine dosing with baseline monitoring for response and tolerance. Periodic reassessment of clinical efficacy. Counsel regarding alcohol interaction. Monitor for signs of dependence or tolerance development.

TT.effect: Normal or elevated GABRA2 gene expression. Typical subjective response to alcohol. Lowest risk for alcohol dependence and benzodiazepine-related adverse effects. Standard benzodiazepine sensitivity expected.
TT.recommendation: Standard benzodiazepine dosing per clinical guidelines. Standard monitoring for therapeutic response and adverse effects.

gene_description: GABA(A) receptor subunit alpha 6 — component of GABA(A) chloride channels primarily expressed in cerebellar granule cells. Variants may influence benzodiazepine sensitivity, seizure susceptibility, and stress response through altered GABAergic neurotransmission.

source.title: Association of GABRA6 1519 T>C (rs3219151) and Synapsin II (rs37733634) gene polymorphisms with the development of idiopathic generalized epilepsy
source.finding: GABRA6 rs3219151 C-allele associated with increased epilepsy susceptibility in some populations (South India OR=3.6, 95% CI 1.8-7.2); T-allele associated with greater stress response and potentially heightened benzodiazepine sensitivity.

CC.phenotype: Homozygous C-allele (high-risk variant in some populations)
CC.effect: Associated with altered GABRA6 function and increased seizure risk in Asian populations (OR~3.6). May indicate reduced GABAergic inhibition in cerebellum and brainstem. Potentially altered benzodiazepine sensitivity; some individuals may show enhanced response, others may have reduced therapeutic efficacy. Elevated baseline anxiety and stress responsivity reported.
CC.recommendation: Baseline EEG and seizure risk assessment recommended, particularly if Asian ancestry. For benzodiazepines: start with lower doses and titrate slowly; monitor for paradoxical reactions or inadequate response. Enhanced monitoring for seizure development. Consider alternative anxiolytics if benzodiazepine response suboptimal.

CT.effect: Intermediate phenotype. Reduced seizure risk compared to CC genotype. Variable benzodiazepine sensitivity; most individuals show standard response, some may show enhanced sensitivity.
CT.recommendation: Standard benzodiazepine dosing with assessment of therapeutic response. Monitor for both efficacy and adverse effects. Standard seizure precautions as clinically indicated.

TC.effect: Intermediate phenotype. Reduced seizure risk compared to CC genotype. Variable benzodiazepine sensitivity; most individuals show standard response, some may show enhanced sensitivity.
TC.recommendation: Standard benzodiazepam dosing with assessment of therapeutic response. Monitor for both efficacy and adverse effects. Standard seizure precautions as clinically indicated.

TT.effect: Associated with normal to reduced seizure risk. T-allele carriers show greater physiological and endocrine stress response, potentially indicating increased benzodiazepine sensitivity in some individuals. Standard to enhanced GABA(A) function.
TT.recommendation: Standard benzodiazepine dosing for most individuals; monitor clinical response. Those with elevated baseline stress response may benefit from lower initial doses with careful titration.

gene_description: Group-specific component (GC) — encodes vitamin D binding protein (VDBP), which transports 85-90% of circulating vitamin D (25-hydroxyvitamin D) and regulates bioavailability to tissues

source.title: Genetic variation of vitamin D binding protein affects vitamin D status and response to supplementation
source.finding: GC rs2282679 polymorphism associated with serum 25(OH)D concentrations; C allele carriers had 1.80-fold increased odds of vitamin D deficiency (OR 1.80, 95% CI 1.57-2.01)

CC.effect: Homozygous C carriers have significantly lower serum 25(OH)D concentrations compared to TT carriers. VDBP function is reduced, limiting vitamin D transport and tissue availability. Increased risk of vitamin D deficiency even with adequate dietary intake or sun exposure.
CC.recommendation: Consider vitamin D supplementation (1000-2000 IU daily) even with adequate sun exposure. Monitor 25(OH)D levels periodically. May benefit from higher supplementation doses to achieve optimal serum 25(OH)D (>30 ng/mL).

CT.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate serum 25(OH)D levels, between homozygous groups. Modest reduction in VDBP function and vitamin D transport capacity.
CT.recommendation: Standard vitamin D intake recommendations typically adequate. Monitor 25(OH)D status with periodic testing, especially if symptoms of deficiency present (bone pain, muscle weakness, fatigue). Supplementation recommended if levels <30 ng/mL.

TC.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate serum 25(OH)D levels, between homozygous groups. Modest reduction in VDBP function and vitamin D transport capacity.
TC.recommendation: Standard vitamin D intake recommendations typically adequate. Monitor 25(OH)D status with periodic testing, especially if symptoms of deficiency present (bone pain, muscle weakness, fatigue). Supplementation recommended if levels <30 ng/mL.

TT.effect: Wild-type homozygotes have normal VDBP function and vitamin D transport. Optimal serum 25(OH)D levels with standard sun exposure and dietary intake.
TT.recommendation: Standard vitamin D intake recommendations adequate (600-800 IU daily for most adults). Maintain adequate sun exposure and dietary sources (fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified milk).

gene_description: Glucokinase Regulator — hepatic protein that allosterically inhibits glucokinase and sequesters it in the nucleus under low glucose. The rs780094 variant modulates the glucose-triglyceride trade-off: improving glycemia while raising triglycerides.

source.title: Common missense variant in the glucokinase regulatory protein gene is associated with increased plasma triglyceride and C-reactive protein but lower fasting glucose concentrations
source.finding: GCKR rs780094 T allele increases hepatic glucokinase activity, leading to lower fasting glucose and insulin resistance but higher triglycerides and CRP. Represents a metabolic trade-off: improved glycemia at the cost of dyslipidemia and NAFLD risk.

TT.effect: Homozygous T carriers have reduced GCKR inhibition of glucokinase, leading to increased hepatic glucose uptake. Lower fasting glucose, lower insulin, and reduced T2DM risk. However, elevated triglycerides, higher CRP (inflammation), and increased NAFLD risk. This metabolic trade-off represents improved glycemia at the cost of lipid dysregulation.
TT.recommendation: Monitor triglycerides and liver function despite favorable glucose profile. Limit refined carbohydrates (which hepatic glucokinase converts to triglycerides). Low-glycemic, moderate-fat diet. Omega-3 supplementation for triglyceride management. Regular hepatic steatosis screening.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate effects: modestly lower fasting glucose with mildly elevated triglycerides.
CT.recommendation: Balanced diet. Monitor both glucose and lipid panels. Standard metabolic recommendations.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate effects: modestly lower fasting glucose with mildly elevated triglycerides.
TC.recommendation: Balanced diet. Monitor both glucose and lipid panels. Standard metabolic recommendations.

CC.effect: Wild-type with standard glucokinase regulatory function. Normal fasting glucose and triglyceride levels from this locus. Standard T2DM risk.
CC.recommendation: Standard dietary and metabolic guidelines.

gene_description: Growth hormone secretagogue receptor (ghrelin receptor) — promoter variants linked to weight change during lifestyle intervention and glucose metabolism

source.title: Variations in the Ghrelin Receptor Gene Associate with Obesity and Glucose Metabolism in Individuals with Impaired Glucose Tolerance
source.finding: Promoter rs490683: CC genotype associated with greatest weight loss during long-term lifestyle intervention; G allele linked to altered NF-1 binding and higher GHSR expression in reporter assays.

CC.phenotype: More favorable weight-loss response (Finnish DPS subgroup)
CC.effect: CC carriers showed the largest mean weight loss over follow-up in impaired-glucose-tolerance lifestyle trial.
CC.recommendation: Lifestyle programs remain first-line; genotype does not replace monitoring of glucose and weight.

CG.effect: Heterozygotes: intermediate longitudinal weight and glucose-metabolism patterns vs CC/GG in Mager et al.
CG.recommendation: Sustained dietary quality and activity; track metabolic markers with usual care.

GC.effect: Heterozygotes: intermediate longitudinal weight and glucose-metabolism patterns vs CC/GG in Mager et al.
GC.recommendation: Sustained dietary quality and activity; track metabolic markers with usual care.

GG.phenotype: Less weight loss / stronger promoter signal (trial context)
GG.effect: GG associated with lowest body weight at baseline in that cohort and smaller loss over intervention; functional data suggest higher GHSR expression with intact NF-1 motif.
GG.recommendation: Extra structure for appetite (meal timing, protein, fiber); interpret with clinician in context of diabetes risk.

gene_description: GIP receptor (Mounjaro/tirzepatide response)

source.title: GIPR rs1800437 and incretin response
source.finding: GIPR E354Q variant affects GIP receptor signaling. May influence tirzepatide response.

CC.effect: Impaired GIP receptor signaling. May have reduced response to GIP-targeting drugs like tirzepatide.
CC.recommendation: Monitor tirzepatide response. GLP-1-only agonists may be more effective.

CG.effect: Partially reduced GIP receptor signaling.

GG.effect: Normal GIP receptor function.
GG.recommendation: Standard dosing.

gene_description: GLP-1 receptor (Ozempic/semaglutide response)

source.title: GLP1R variants and response to GLP-1 receptor agonists
source.finding: GLP1R rs6923761 A allele associated with reduced GLP-1 agonist efficacy. Emerging evidence.

AA.effect: Lower efficacy with GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide).
AA.recommendation: May need higher dose or alternative therapy (e.g., tirzepatide which also targets GIP).

AG.effect: Moderately reduced response to GLP-1 agonists.
AG.recommendation: Monitor response closely. Dose adjustment may be needed.

GG.effect: Expected response to GLP-1 receptor agonists.
GG.recommendation: Standard dosing.

gene_description: Encodes the glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor, a G-protein coupled receptor that mediates the effects of GLP-1 in glucose homeostasis, insulin secretion, and appetite regulation. Target for GLP-1 receptor agonists used in diabetes and obesity treatment.

source.title: A GLP1R gene variant and sex influence the response to semaglutide treatment in patients with severe obesity.
source.finding: GLP1R rs6923761 AA genotype associated with 58% greater weight loss response to semaglutide compared to G allele carriers

GG.effect: Mean weight loss of 1.04% per month on semaglutide 2.4mg weekly. Standard response to GLP-1 receptor agonist therapy.
GG.recommendation: Standard semaglutide dosing. Monitor weight loss response and consider dose optimization or alternative therapies if inadequate response after 4-6 months.

GA.effect: Intermediate weight loss response to semaglutide, between GG and AA genotypes. Response falls between 1.04-1.64% weight loss per month.
GA.recommendation: Standard semaglutide dosing. Monitor weight loss response. May have intermediate benefit compared to homozygous genotypes.

AA.effect: Mean weight loss of 1.64% per month on semaglutide 2.4mg weekly. Approximately 58% greater weight loss response compared to G allele carriers.
AA.recommendation: Standard semaglutide dosing. Patients may achieve enhanced weight loss outcomes. Continue monitoring for efficacy and tolerability.

gene_description: Glucosamine-6-Phosphate Deaminase 2 — an enzyme involved in amino sugar metabolism and protein glycosylation. The rs10938397 variant's mechanism in obesity is not fully characterized but appears to influence energy homeostasis, possibly through effects on immune signaling, metabolic inflammation, or central appetite regulation pathways.

source.title: Six new loci associated with body mass index highlight a neuronal influence on body weight regulation
source.finding: Genome-wide meta-analysis of BMI in 15 GWAS cohorts (n > 32,000) with follow-up in 14 additional cohorts (n > 59,000) identifies six new BMI-associated loci: TMEM18, KCTD15, GNPDA2, SH2B1, MTCH2, and NEGR1. GNPDA2 rs10938397-G associated with increased BMI (p < 5 × 10⁻⁸). Most novel loci are highly expressed in the central nervous system, suggesting neuronal control of body weight regulation. Each risk allele contributes modestly to BMI variation.

GG.phenotype: GNPDA2 homozygous risk — elevated obesity susceptibility
GG.effect: Homozygous for G risk allele. ~1.2-fold increased BMI per risk allele (additive effect); compound effect of two alleles is ~1.44-fold BMI increase. Approximately 1–2 kg/m² BMI increase in homozygotes. Mechanism not fully characterized; likely involves central appetite regulation, metabolic inflammation, or immune signaling pathways. Modest but consistent effect across populations. Environmental factors (diet, physical activity) substantially modulate phenotypic expression.
GG.recommendation: Lifestyle intervention with emphasis on energy balance: caloric deficit of 500–750 kcal/day for gradual weight loss (0.5–1 kg/week). Structured diet program (Mediterranean, DASH, or low-carbohydrate approach). Regular physical activity: ≥150 min/week moderate-intensity aerobic exercise plus resistance training 2–3 times/week. Behavioral counseling and monitoring. If lifestyle alone insufficient after 6 months and BMI >30 (or >27 with comorbidities), consider GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide) or dual GLP-1/GIP agonist (tirzepatide) — particularly effective for GNPDA2 carriers. Orlistat (pancreatic lipase inhibitor) as adjunct. Screen for metabolic syndrome, type 2 diabetes, and dyslipidemia.

GA.effect: Heterozygous for G allele. ~1.2-fold increased BMI (per copy of G allele). ~0.5–1 kg/m² average BMI increase compared to AA homozygotes. Modest genetic contribution to obesity risk; environmental factors (diet, activity) play dominant role in phenotype expression.
GA.recommendation: Preventive lifestyle measures: maintain balanced, calorie-appropriate diet; regular physical activity (150 min/week moderate aerobic exercise, 2–3 times/week resistance training). Maintain healthy weight through portion control. Monitor BMI and weight trends. No specific pharmacotherapy indicated unless overweight/obese. Screen periodically for metabolic syndrome.

AG.effect: Heterozygous for G allele. ~1.2-fold increased BMI (per copy of G allele). ~0.5–1 kg/m² average BMI increase compared to AA homozygotes. Modest genetic contribution to obesity risk; environmental factors (diet, activity) play dominant role in phenotype expression.
AG.recommendation: Preventive lifestyle measures: maintain balanced, calorie-appropriate diet; regular physical activity (150 min/week moderate aerobic exercise, 2–3 times/week resistance training). Maintain healthy weight through portion control. Monitor BMI and weight trends. No specific pharmacotherapy indicated unless overweight/obese. Screen periodically for metabolic syndrome.

AA.phenotype: GNPDA2 wild-type homozygous — standard obesity risk
AA.effect: Homozygous for A reference allele. Normal GNPDA2 function. Standard obesity risk determined by environmental factors (diet, physical activity, sleep, stress) and other genetic loci. No increased genetic predisposition to obesity from this variant.
AA.recommendation: Standard healthy lifestyle guidelines: balanced diet appropriate for energy needs, regular physical activity (150 min/week), maintain healthy weight. No specific genetic risk factors from this locus warrant additional intervention.

gene_description: Glutamate Ionotropic Receptor Kainate Type Subunit 4 (KA1) — kainate-type glutamate receptor subunit that modulates excitatory neurotransmission. Identified in STAR*D as a predictor of SSRI antidepressant response.

source.title: Association of GRIK4 with outcome of antidepressant treatment in the STAR*D cohort
source.finding: GRIK4 rs1954787 C-allele homozygotes had 11% reduction in risk of non-response to citalopram in STAR*D (n=1816). Combined with HTR2A, homozygotes for both response alleles were 23% less likely to be non-responders (OR=1.22, 95% CI 1.035-1.445).

TT.effect: Homozygous for the T allele. Associated with poorer citalopram response in the STAR*D study (n=1816). The glutamate system plays an important role in modulating SSRI response, and this variant affects kainate receptor signaling. When combined with unfavorable HTR2A genotype, risk of treatment non-response increases substantially.
TT.recommendation: If SSRI monotherapy is insufficient after adequate trial, consider augmentation with glutamatergic modulators or switch to alternative antidepressant class. Evaluate HTR2A rs7997012 genotype for combined risk assessment. Consider SNRIs or bupropion as alternatives.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier. One C allele provides partial benefit. Intermediate likelihood of SSRI response.
TC.recommendation: Standard SSRI dosing is appropriate. Allow adequate trial duration (6-8 weeks) before considering alternatives.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier. One C allele provides partial benefit. Intermediate likelihood of SSRI response.
CT.recommendation: Standard SSRI dosing is appropriate. Allow adequate trial duration (6-8 weeks) before considering alternatives.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the C allele. 11% reduction in risk of non-response to citalopram. When combined with favorable HTR2A genotype (rs7997012 AA), provides the best pharmacogenomic prediction of SSRI response. Optimal kainate receptor signaling supporting serotonergic antidepressant action.
CC.recommendation: SSRIs are a good first-line choice. Standard dosing per clinical guidelines.

gene_description: G Protein-Coupled Receptor Kinase 4 — regulates renal dopamine D1 receptor signaling controlling natriuresis (sodium excretion). Activating variants desensitize D1 receptors, impairing sodium excretion and causing salt-sensitive hypertension.

source.title: The importance of G protein-coupled receptor kinase 4 (GRK4) in pathogenesis of salt sensitivity, salt sensitive hypertension and response to antihypertensive treatment
source.finding: GRK4 rs1024323 (A142V) variant desensitizes renal D1 dopamine receptor, impairing natriuresis. Number of GRK4 variant alleles inversely correlates with ability to excrete chronic sodium load. Affects response to dietary sodium restriction and antihypertensive drugs.

TT.effect: Homozygous A142V carriers have hyperactive GRK4, causing constitutive desensitization and internalization of renal D1 dopamine receptors. Markedly impaired natriuresis — blood pressure rises significantly with sodium intake. Increased angiotensin II type 1 receptor expression. High risk of essential hypertension with high-salt diet.
TT.recommendation: Strict sodium restriction (<1500mg/day). Dietary sodium modification should be first-line intervention. DASH diet strongly recommended. Thiazide diuretics may be particularly effective. Monitor blood pressure response to salt intake. Potassium-rich foods to counterbalance sodium.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate GRK4 activity and moderate salt sensitivity. Blood pressure modestly affected by sodium intake.
CT.recommendation: Moderate sodium restriction (<2300mg/day). Regular blood pressure monitoring. Emphasize potassium-rich fruits and vegetables.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate GRK4 activity and moderate salt sensitivity. Blood pressure modestly affected by sodium intake.
TC.recommendation: Moderate sodium restriction (<2300mg/day). Regular blood pressure monitoring. Emphasize potassium-rich fruits and vegetables.

CC.effect: Wild-type GRK4 with normal D1 receptor regulation. Adequate renal natriuresis. Blood pressure less affected by dietary sodium from this locus.
CC.recommendation: Standard sodium intake guidelines.

gene_description: Glutathione S-transferase Pi 1 — phase II detoxification enzyme that conjugates glutathione to platinum compounds, influencing efficacy and toxicity of cisplatin/carboplatin chemotherapy

source.title: Predictive value of GSTP1 Ile105Val polymorphism in clinical outcomes of chemotherapy in gastric and colorectal cancers: a systematic review and meta-analysis
source.finding: GSTP1 rs1695 Ile105Val polymorphism significantly affects platinum-based chemotherapy outcomes — Val carriers show reduced detoxification of platinum compounds, leading to higher drug exposure, increased toxicity but potentially better tumor response.

GG.effect: Homozygous Val105 carriers have significantly reduced glutathione-S-transferase activity toward platinum substrates. Lower detoxification of cisplatin/carboplatin leads to higher intracellular platinum concentration. Associated with 3.87-fold increased risk of disease progression in head/neck cancer but higher objective response rates in GI cancers. Greater risk of grade 2-3 nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.
GG.recommendation: Consider closer monitoring for platinum toxicity (nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity, ototoxicity). Dose adjustments may be warranted. May have better initial tumor response but worse long-term outcomes in some cancer types.

AG.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate enzyme activity. Moderate reduction in platinum detoxification. 4.28-fold higher likelihood of grade 2-3 vomiting with cisplatin-based therapy compared to Ile/Ile. Intermediate toxicity and efficacy profile.
AG.recommendation: Standard platinum dosing with enhanced monitoring for adverse effects. Watch for nephrotoxicity and severe nausea/vomiting. Consider supportive care adjustments.

GA.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate enzyme activity. Moderate reduction in platinum detoxification. 4.28-fold higher likelihood of grade 2-3 vomiting with cisplatin-based therapy compared to Ile/Ile. Intermediate toxicity and efficacy profile.
GA.recommendation: Standard platinum dosing with enhanced monitoring for adverse effects. Watch for nephrotoxicity and severe nausea/vomiting. Consider supportive care adjustments.

AA.effect: Wild-type with full glutathione conjugation activity. Efficient detoxification of platinum compounds. Associated with higher risk of severe hematotoxicity (OR 5.71) due to greater drug activation, but standard drug clearance. Standard toxicity profile for platinum agents.
AA.recommendation: Standard platinum-based chemotherapy dosing. Monitor for hematological toxicity as Ile/Ile carriers may paradoxically show higher hematotoxicity rates.

description: HBB rs334 (Glu6Val) variant causing HbS mutation, leading to sickle cell disease in homozygotes. The glutamic acid to valine substitution causes hemoglobin polymerization under low oxygen conditions, resulting in characteristic sickle-shaped red blood cells.

source.title: Global distribution of the sickle cell gene and geographical confirmation of the malaria hypothesis
source.authors: Piel FB, Patil AP, Howes RE, et al.

TT.phenotype: Sickle cell disease (HbSS); severe chronic hemolytic anemia with vaso-occlusive complications
TT.effect: Sickle cell disease (HbSS); severe chronic hemolytic anemia with vaso-occlusive complications
TT.recommendation: Requires comprehensive medical management including pain control, hydration, folic acid supplementation, and hydroxycurea or other disease-modifying therapies. Consider voxelotor, crizanlizumab, L-glutamine, or gene therapy.

TA.phenotype: Sickle cell trait (HbAS); usually asymptomatic but increased risk during stress, altitude, exercise
TA.effect: Sickle cell trait (HbAS); usually asymptomatic but increased risk during stress, altitude, exercise
TA.recommendation: Genetic counseling recommended; avoid extreme exercise/altitude/dehydration; no routine medical treatment needed

AT.phenotype: Sickle cell trait (HbAS); usually asymptomatic but increased risk during stress, altitude, exercise
AT.effect: Sickle cell trait (HbAS); usually asymptomatic but increased risk during stress, altitude, exercise
AT.recommendation: Genetic counseling recommended; avoid extreme exercise/altitude/dehydration; no routine medical treatment needed

AA.phenotype: Normal hemoglobin; no sickle cell disease or trait
AA.effect: Normal hemoglobin; no sickle cell disease or trait
AA.recommendation: No special considerations related to sickle cell

gene_description: HECT and RLD domains containing E3 ubiquitin protein ligase 2 (HERC2) — intron 86 contains regulatory element controlling OCA2 expression and eye pigmentation

source.title: Blue eye color in humans may be caused by a perfectly associated founder mutation in a regulatory element located within the HERC2 gene inhibiting OCA2 expression
source.finding: rs12913832 is the best-known predictor of blue vs brown eye color. Located 21.152 bp upstream of OCA2 promoter in HERC2 intron 86. G allele associated with blue eyes (~94% accuracy in Europeans) by reducing OCA2 expression.

GG.effect: Homozygous for G allele. Strongly associated with blue eye color. G allele is found in regulatory enhancer of HERC2 that suppresses OCA2 expression, reducing melanin production in the iris. ~94% of individuals with this genotype have blue eyes.
GG.recommendation: No clinical action needed. Blue eyes are more sensitive to light and sunglare; polarized sunglasses may improve comfort.

AG.effect: Heterozygous A/G genotype. Most commonly associated with brown eye color, though intermediate shades can occur. Permits higher OCA2 expression than GG genotype, resulting in increased melanin deposition.
AG.recommendation: No clinical action needed. Standard UV eye protection recommended for all individuals.

GA.effect: Heterozygous A/G genotype. Most commonly associated with brown eye color, though intermediate shades can occur. Permits higher OCA2 expression than GG genotype, resulting in increased melanin deposition.
GA.recommendation: No clinical action needed. Standard UV eye protection recommended for all individuals.

AA.effect: Homozygous for A allele (ancestral). Associated with brown eye color due to unrestricted OCA2 expression and high melanin production in the iris. Most common phenotype worldwide.
AA.recommendation: No clinical action needed. Standard UV eye protection recommended for all individuals.

description: Homeostatic iron regulator protein and iron metabolism

source.title: A novel MHC class I-like gene is mutated in patients with hereditary haemochromatosis
source.authors: Feder JN, Gnirke A, Thomas W, et al.

AA.effect: Homozygous for the A allele (risk allele), resulting in C282Y homozygosity. Strong risk for hereditary hemochromatosis with iron overload. Elevated transferrin saturation and serum ferritin. Associated with progressive organ damage including cirrhosis, cardiomyopathy, and endocrine dysfunction. Requires iron chelation therapy and avoid iron supplements.
AA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier with one C282Y mutation. Mild to moderate elevation in iron stores, mildly elevated transferrin saturation. Monitor ferritin levels. May develop iron overload later in life, particularly with additional risk factors.
AG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier with one C282Y mutation. Mild to moderate elevation in iron stores, mildly elevated transferrin saturation. Monitor ferritin levels. May develop iron overload later in life, particularly with additional risk factors.
GA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GG.effect: Homozygous for the G allele (reference/wild-type), no C282Y mutations. Normal iron metabolism and regulation.
GG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

gene_description: Immune-mediated drug hypersensitivity (abacavir)

source.title: HLA-B*5701 screening for hypersensitivity to abacavir (PREDICT-1 trial)
source.finding: Prospective HLA-B*5701 screening eliminated immunologically confirmed abacavir hypersensitivity (0% vs 2.7% in controls, P<0.001). 94% of patients do not carry the allele and are at low risk.

GG.effect: Very high risk of severe, potentially life-threatening abacavir hypersensitivity reaction. Symptoms include fever, rash, GI distress, respiratory issues within first 6 weeks.
GG.recommendation: DO NOT prescribe abacavir. Use alternative NRTI (tenofovir, emtricitabine). Record as abacavir allergy in medical record.

GT.effect: High risk of severe abacavir hypersensitivity reaction. One copy of the risk allele is sufficient to trigger the immune-mediated reaction.
GT.recommendation: DO NOT prescribe abacavir. Use alternative NRTI. Record as abacavir allergy.

TT.effect: Low risk of immunologically confirmed abacavir hypersensitivity. Standard abacavir use is appropriate.
TT.recommendation: Abacavir can be prescribed. Maintain standard clinical vigilance for any hypersensitivity symptoms.

gene_description: Human Leukocyte Antigen A — major histocompatibility complex class I gene. HLA-A*31:01 allele is associated with severe cutaneous adverse reactions (DRESS, SJS/TEN, MPE) to carbamazepine across multiple ethnicities.

source.title: Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium (CPIC) Guideline for HLA Genotype and Use of Carbamazepine and Oxcarbazepine: 2017 Update
source.finding: HLA-A*31:01 carriers have significantly increased risk of carbamazepine-induced DRESS, SJS/TEN, and MPE across European, Japanese, and other populations. CPIC recommends avoiding carbamazepine in HLA-A*31:01-positive patients.

AA.effect: Homozygous carriers of HLA-A*31:01 have highest risk for carbamazepine-induced severe cutaneous adverse reactions including DRESS (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms), Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS), toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN), and maculopapular exanthema (MPE). Risk is present regardless of ethnicity.
AA.recommendation: DO NOT USE carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine. Choose an alternative anticonvulsant that is not structurally related (e.g., levetiracetam, lamotrigine with caution, valproate). This is a CPIC Level A recommendation.

AC.effect: Heterozygous carrier of HLA-A*31:01. Significantly increased risk of carbamazepine-induced DRESS, SJS/TEN, and MPE. One copy of the allele is sufficient to confer risk. The allele has 2-5% prevalence in Northern Europeans, ~6% in Hispanics, and ~8% in Japanese.
AC.recommendation: DO NOT USE carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine if alternatives are available. If carbamazepine use is considered essential, ensure very close clinical monitoring and patient education about early signs of hypersensitivity (fever, rash, lymphadenopathy).

CA.effect: Heterozygous carrier of HLA-A*31:01. Significantly increased risk of carbamazepine-induced DRESS, SJS/TEN, and MPE. One copy of the allele is sufficient to confer risk. The allele has 2-5% prevalence in Northern Europeans, ~6% in Hispanics, and ~8% in Japanese.
CA.recommendation: DO NOT USE carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine if alternatives are available. If carbamazepine use is considered essential, ensure very close clinical monitoring and patient education about early signs of hypersensitivity (fever, rash, lymphadenopathy).

CC.effect: Non-carrier of HLA-A*31:01. Standard risk for carbamazepine-induced cutaneous adverse reactions from this locus. Note: HLA-B*15:02 should still be tested in Southeast Asian ancestry patients for SJS/TEN risk.
CC.recommendation: Carbamazepine may be used per standard prescribing guidelines. Still test for HLA-B*15:02 if patient is of Southeast Asian descent. Standard monitoring for drug reactions applies.

gene_description: Human Leukocyte Antigen B — major histocompatibility complex class I gene. HLA-B*15:02 allele is strongly associated with severe cutaneous adverse reactions (Stevens-Johnson Syndrome and Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis) to carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, and phenytoin, particularly in Southeast Asian populations.

source.title: Association between HLA-B*1502 and carbamazepine-induced severe cutaneous adverse drug reactions in a Thai population
source.finding: In Thai population, 88.1% of carbamazepine-induced SJS/TEN patients carried HLA-B*1502 (37/42 patients) vs 11.9% of carbamazepine-tolerant controls (5/42). Odds ratio 54.76, 95% CI 18.18-164.7. FDA black-box warning issued for carbamazepine based on SJS/TEN risk in HLA-B*15:02 carriers.

snps.note: Proxy SNP tagging HLA-B*15:02 allele in Southeast Asian populations. Direct HLA-B allele typing is gold standard but genetic marker rs3909184 is used in some screening protocols.

GG.effect: Homozygous carriers of HLA-B*15:02 allele have very high risk of life-threatening carbamazepine-induced Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS) and Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN). Risk of SJS/TEN is 100-1000 times higher than non-carriers. Typical onset is 1-8 weeks after initiation of carbamazepine. Mortality rate for TEN is 20-30%.
GG.recommendation: CONTRAINDICATED. DO NOT prescribe carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, or phenytoin to HLA-B*15:02-positive patients. This is an FDA black-box warning. Use alternative antiepileptic drugs (e.g., levetiracetam, lamotrigine with careful monitoring, valproate, or lacosamide). This is a CPIC/PharmGKB Level A recommendation.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers of HLA-B*15:02 allele have significantly increased risk of carbamazepine-induced SJS/TEN. The presence of one copy of the allele is sufficient to confer substantial risk. Risk remains 50-100 times higher than non-carriers.
GA.recommendation: CONTRAINDICATED or use with extreme caution. Avoid carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, and phenytoin if alternatives exist. HLA-B*15:02 testing is recommended for all patients with ancestry from Southeast Asia, South Asia (India), or other high-risk populations prior to initiating these medications. If use is deemed essential despite positive screening, implement intensive patient education about early warning signs (fever, rash, blistering, oral lesions, eye involvement) and arrange close clinical monitoring.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers of HLA-B*15:02 allele have significantly increased risk of carbamazepine-induced SJS/TEN. The presence of one copy of the allele is sufficient to confer substantial risk. Risk remains 50-100 times higher than non-carriers.
AG.recommendation: CONTRAINDICATED or use with extreme caution. Avoid carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, and phenytoin if alternatives exist. HLA-B*15:02 testing is recommended for all patients with ancestry from Southeast Asia, South Asia (India), or other high-risk populations prior to initiating these medications. If use is deemed essential despite positive screening, implement intensive patient education about early warning signs (fever, rash, blistering, oral lesions, eye involvement) and arrange close clinical monitoring.

AA.effect: Non-carrier of HLA-B*15:02. Standard population risk for carbamazepine-induced SJS/TEN from this locus. Note: Other HLA alleles (HLA-A*31:01, HLA-B*15:08, HLA-B*15:11, HLA-B*15:21) may still confer risk in some populations. Risk in non-carriers is still approximately 1-6 per 10,000 carbamazepine exposures (baseline population risk).
AA.recommendation: Carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, and phenytoin may be prescribed per standard clinical guidelines. Standard monitoring for dermatologic and systemic adverse effects applies. If patient develops signs of SJS/TEN (fever, rash, blistering, mucosal involvement), discontinue immediately and seek emergency care.

gene_description: HLA-B (Major Histocompatibility Complex, Class I, B) encodes a major histocompatibility complex class I molecule. The HLA-B*58:01 allele shows extremely strong association with allopurinol-induced severe cutaneous adverse reactions (SCAR), including Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS) and Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN).

source.title: HLA-B*5801 allele as a genetic marker for severe cutaneous adverse reactions caused by allopurinol
source.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences USA
source.finding: HLA-B*58:01 present in 100% of allopurinol-SJS/TEN cases vs. 15% of tolerant controls in Han-Chinese population; landmark pharmacogenomic discovery

TT.phenotype: Homozygous T allele; tag SNP for HLA-B*58:01
TT.effect: Very high likelihood of HLA-B*58:01 positivity; extreme risk for allopurinol-induced SJS/TEN
TT.recommendation: CONTRAINDICATION to allopurinol. Use alternative uric acid-lowering agents (febuxostat preferred, or use cautiously with monitoring). Consider uricosuric agents or pegloticase if needed.

TC.phenotype: Heterozygous T allele; possible tag SNP for HLA-B*58:01
TC.effect: Elevated risk of allopurinol-induced SJS/TEN; HLA-B*58:01 testing recommended
TC.recommendation: Perform HLA-B*58:01 genotyping. If positive, avoid allopurinol. If negative, allopurinol can be used but with careful monitoring. Alternative urate-lowering therapy preferred.

CT.phenotype: Heterozygous T allele; possible tag SNP for HLA-B*58:01
CT.effect: Elevated risk of allopurinol-induced SJS/TEN; HLA-B*58:01 testing recommended
CT.recommendation: Perform HLA-B*58:01 genotyping. If positive, avoid allopurinol. If negative, allopurinol can be used but with careful monitoring. Alternative urate-lowering therapy preferred.

CC.phenotype: Homozygous C allele; low likelihood of HLA-B*58:01
CC.effect: Very low risk of HLA-B*58:01-associated allopurinol SJS/TEN; allopurinol safe to use
CC.recommendation: Allopurinol can be safely prescribed at standard doses. Monitor for other ADRs. Standard uric acid target <6 mg/dL.

clinical_significance: Landmark CPIC-recommended pharmacogenomic test. HLA-B*58:01 screening prevents life-threatening allopurinol SJS/TEN. Particularly important in East Asian populations where allele frequency is 10-25%. FDA and FDA-cleared testing now standard-of-care.

gene_description: Human Leukocyte Antigen B — Major Histocompatibility Complex class I heavy chain. The rs4349859 A-allele is a tagging SNP for HLA-B*27, which carries the strongest genetic association with ankylosing spondylitis (AS). HLA-B*27 presents bacterial and commensal peptides to CD8+ T cells, initiating pathogenic immune responses through molecular mimicry and/or aberrant peptide presentation in genetically predisposed individuals.

source.title: Interaction between ERAP1 and HLA-B27 in ankylosing spondylitis implicates peptide handling in the mechanism for HLA-B27 in disease susceptibility

AA.effect: Homozygous A-allele carriers (highly correlated with HLA-B*27 positive genotype) show extremely elevated AS risk (50-100x relative risk in HLA-B*27 positive individuals compared to B*27 negative controls). The B*27 molecule presents peptides poorly to T cell receptors, resulting in aberrant peptide-independent activation of HPA and CD4+ T cells through unconventional mechanisms. Approximately 90-95% of AS patients carry HLA-B*27. AA homozygotes with B*27 show early-onset disease, severe spinal inflammation, and risk for extra-articular manifestations including uveitis and enthesitis.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers with one HLA-B*27 allele show significant but reduced AS risk compared to homozygotes (approximately 20-50x relative to B*27 negative controls). Intermediate B*27 expression and peptide presentation capacity. Still confers substantial disease susceptibility with variable progression.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers with one HLA-B*27 allele show significant but reduced AS risk compared to homozygotes (approximately 20-50x relative to B*27 negative controls). Intermediate B*27 expression and peptide presentation capacity. Still confers substantial disease susceptibility with variable progression.

GG.effect: Homozygous G-allele carriers lack HLA-B*27 allele and serve as reference group. Less than 5% of AS patients lack HLA-B*27. Very low risk for B*27-mediated autoimmunity; AS in these individuals is typically associated with rare HLA molecules or other genetic risk factors.

gene_description: HLA class I molecule HLA-C — presents antigens to immune cells; HLA-C*06:02 allele associated with psoriasis susceptibility and exceptional response to IL-12/23 biologics (ustekinumab, risankizumab)

source.title: A pharmacogenetic study of psoriasis risk variants and prediction of responses to anti-TNF-alpha and anti-IL-12/23 agents
source.finding: HLA-C*06:02+ patients had 92% PASI75 response rate vs 67% in HLA-C*06:02- patients after 6 months ustekinumab; tag SNP rs10484554 associated with response pattern

AA.effect: Homozygous A carriers correlate with HLA-C*06:02 allele presence. Exceptional response to IL-12/23 inhibitors (ustekinumab, risankizumab, guselkumab). 92% achieve PASI75 response within 6 months. Strong genetic predisposition to psoriasis but excellent biologic response.
AA.recommendation: IL-12/23 inhibitors (ustekinumab 45-90 mg dose, risankizumab 150 mg, guselkumab 100 mg) are first-line biologic choice. Expected response within 12-16 weeks. Consider these agents preferentially in this genotype for optimal outcomes.

AG.effect: Heterozygous A/G carriers likely represent HLA-C*06:02 allele carriers (one copy). Intermediate to good response to IL-12/23 biologics. Approximately 75-85% achieve PASI75 response.
AG.recommendation: IL-12/23 inhibitors remain good first-line biologic choice. Standard dosing recommended. May consider TNF-alpha inhibitors as alternative if IL-12/23 response suboptimal.

GA.effect: Heterozygous G/A carriers likely represent HLA-C*06:02 allele carriers (one copy). Intermediate to good response to IL-12/23 biologics. Approximately 75-85% achieve PASI75 response.
GA.recommendation: IL-12/23 inhibitors remain good first-line biologic choice. Standard dosing recommended. May consider TNF-alpha inhibitors as alternative if IL-12/23 response suboptimal.

GG.effect: Homozygous G carriers typically HLA-C*06:02 negative. Lower response rates to IL-12/23 inhibitors (approximately 50-67% PASI75 response). TNF-alpha inhibitors may be more effective in this population.
GG.recommendation: Consider TNF-alpha inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab) as first-line biologic therapy. If IL-12/23 inhibitor selected, use with close monitoring for response at 12-16 weeks; be prepared to switch if inadequate response.

gene_full_name: Major Histocompatibility Complex, Class II, DQ Alpha 1
region: Regulatory region, tag SNP for HLA-DQ2.5
description: HLA-DQA1 rs2187668 tag SNP for HLA-DQ2.5 heterodimer (DQA1*05 and DQB1*02 on same chromosome). The A allele (inferred DQ2.5) is necessary for celiac disease pathogenesis, present in ~95% of celiac patients vs 30% of general population.

source.title: A genome-wide association study for celiac disease identifies risk variants in the region harboring IL2 and IL21
source.authors: van Heel DA, Hunt K, Greco L, Dubois PC, et al.

TT.phenotype: High genetic predisposition to celiac disease; HLA-DQ2.5 homozygous
TT.effect: High genetic predisposition to celiac disease; HLA-DQ2.5 homozygous
TT.recommendation: Screen with tissue transglutaminase (tTG-IgA) serology. If positive, confirmed with endoscopic small bowel biopsy. Strict gluten-free diet if celiac disease confirmed. Monitor for nutritional deficiencies. Genetic counseling for family members. Screen for dermatitis herpetiformis, thyroiditis, type 1 diabetes.

TC.phenotype: Moderate genetic predisposition to celiac disease; HLA-DQ2.5 heterozygous
TC.effect: Moderate genetic predisposition to celiac disease; HLA-DQ2.5 heterozygous
TC.recommendation: Screen for celiac disease with tTG-IgA serology if symptoms suggestive. Follow-up with biopsy if serology positive. Strict gluten-free diet if diagnosed. Monitor for associated autoimmune conditions.

CT.phenotype: Moderate genetic predisposition to celiac disease; HLA-DQ2.5 heterozygous
CT.effect: Moderate genetic predisposition to celiac disease; HLA-DQ2.5 heterozygous
CT.recommendation: Screen for celiac disease with tTG-IgA serology if symptoms suggestive. Follow-up with biopsy if serology positive. Strict gluten-free diet if diagnosed. Monitor for associated autoimmune conditions.

CC.phenotype: Very low genetic risk for celiac disease; no HLA-DQ2.5
CC.effect: Very low genetic risk for celiac disease; no HLA-DQ2.5
CC.recommendation: Celiac disease unlikely if HLA-DQ2 and DQ8 both negative. Standard evaluation if celiac symptoms present despite negative HLA.

gene_description: Human Leukocyte Antigen DQB1 — part of the MHC class II antigen presentation system. Specific HLA-DQB1 alleles (particularly *05:02 and *02:01) are associated with clozapine-induced agranulocytosis (CIAG), a potentially fatal adverse drug reaction occurring in up to 1% of treated patients. HLA genetic testing can identify high-risk individuals.

source.title: Clozapine-induced agranulocytosis is associated with rare HLA-DQB1 and HLA-B alleles
source.finding: Two independent MHC loci associated with CIAG: HLA-DQB1 126Q (protective, OR=0.19) and HLA-DQB1*05:02 (risk, OR=7.12). HLA-DQB1*02:01 present in 100% of agranulocytosis cases (5/5) vs 54% controls in early studies. Meta-analysis shows HLA-DRB1*04:02 (OR=5.8) also strongly associated.

*02:01/*02:01.effect: Homozygous HLA-DQB1*02:01 carriers have markedly increased risk of clozapine-induced agranulocytosis. Early studies reported 100% presence in CIAG cases. Present in only 54% of controls. Altered antigen presentation leading to immune-mediated granulocyte destruction. Potentially life-threatening adverse reaction.
*02:01/*02:01.recommendation: CONTRAINDICATED or use with extreme caution. If clozapine is absolutely necessary for treatment-resistant schizophrenia, baseline and weekly absolute neutrophil count (ANC) monitoring is MANDATORY. Do not use clozapine unless no safer alternatives available and benefits clearly outweigh severe risks. Consider alternative antipsychotics: aripiprazole, quetiapine, risperidone, or olanzapine. If used, ANC must be monitored weekly for 6 months, then every 2 weeks.

*02:01/other.phenotype: Moderately elevated agranulocytosis risk, heterozygous carrier
*02:01/other.effect: Heterozygous *02:01 carriers show moderately increased risk of clozapine-induced agranulocytosis. Present in approximately 54% of control populations, suggesting heterozygous carriers have meaningful but lower risk than homozygotes. Immune-mediated mechanism still present.
*02:01/other.recommendation: Use clozapine with caution. Baseline ANC required before initiation. Mandatory weekly ANC monitoring for minimum 6 months, then every 2 weeks if continued. Patient education about agranulocytosis warning signs (fever, sore throat, unusual bruising) essential. Rapid ANC drop (>3000 to <2000 in 1 week) requires immediate discontinuation. Consider alternative antipsychotics if available.

other/other.effect: Absence of high-risk HLA-DQB1*02:01 and *05:02 alleles. Standard CIAG risk (approximately 0.8-1.0% of clozapine-treated patients). Baseline immune risk present but significantly lower than in *02:01/*05:02 carriers.
other/other.recommendation: Clozapine can be used for treatment-resistant schizophrenia. Baseline ANC required before initiation (target >3000/microliter). Baseline monitoring: ANC every 2 weeks for 6 months, then monthly. Patient must be counseled on agranulocytosis warning signs. Discontinue immediately if ANC <2000 or significant drop observed.

title: HLA-DRB1 rs9272143 Cervical Cancer Risk (MHC Class II)
description: Tag SNP upstream of HLA-DRB1 in the MHC class II region associated with cervical cancer susceptibility. HLA-DRB1 encodes the beta chain of HLA-DR molecules essential for antigen presentation to CD4+ T helper cells. Variants affecting HLA expression influence immune response to HPV infection, modulating cervical cancer risk. The C allele confers protection against cervical cancer development.

source.title: A variant upstream of HLA-DRB1 and multiple variants in MICA influence susceptibility to cervical cancer

TT.effect: reduced_HLA-DRB1_expression_increased_cervical_cancer_risk
TT.recommendation: HPV_vaccination_enhanced_screening

TC.effect: intermediate_HLA-DRB1_expression
TC.recommendation: HPV_vaccination_standard_screening

CT.effect: intermediate_HLA-DRB1_expression
CT.recommendation: HPV_vaccination_standard_screening

CC.effect: 0.73x_decreased_cervical_cancer_risk
CC.recommendation: standard_surveillance

gene_description: Human Leukocyte Antigen DRB1 — Major Histocompatibility Complex class II beta-1 chain critical for antigen presentation to CD4+ T cells. The rs3135388 A-allele is a tagging SNP for HLA-DRB1*15:01 (DRB1*1501), the strongest genetic risk factor for multiple sclerosis. HLA-DRB1*15:01 presents myelin epitopes with high affinity, driving autoreactive T-cell responses central to MS pathogenesis.

source.title: Genetic refinement and assessment of multiple sclerosis susceptibility and progression loci

AA.effect: Homozygous A-allele carriers (>99% correlated with HLA-DRB1*15:01 genotype) show severe multiple sclerosis risk (approximately 5-7x relative risk compared to GG carriers). The DRB1*15:01 allele presents myelin basic protein (MBP) and proteolipid protein (PLP) peptides with extraordinarily high affinity to autoreactive T cells. Associated with early disease onset, faster progression rates, and higher cumulative disability. AA carriers show 15-20-fold higher HLA-DRB1 expression compared to GG carriers, amplifying autoantigenic presentation.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers with one HLA-DRB1*15:01 allele show moderate MS risk (approximately 3-4x relative to GG carriers). Intermediate HLA-DRB1 expression and T-cell activation capacity. Still confers significant disease susceptibility but with somewhat delayed onset and variable progression compared to homozygotes.
