GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers with one HLA-DRB1*15:01 allele show moderate MS risk (approximately 3-4x relative to GG carriers). Intermediate HLA-DRB1 expression and T-cell activation capacity. Still confers significant disease susceptibility but with somewhat delayed onset and variable progression compared to homozygotes.

GG.effect: Homozygous G-allele carriers lack HLA-DRB1*15:01 allele and serve as reference group. Standard MS prevalence driven by other genetic and environmental factors. Substantially reduced risk for HLA-DRB1-mediated autoimmunity.

gene_description: HMG-CoA Reductase — rate-limiting enzyme of cholesterol biosynthesis and the direct target of statin drugs. Variants in HMGCR affect enzyme expression and statin-induced cholesterol lowering efficacy.

source.title: A paucimorphic variant in the HMG-CoA reductase gene is associated with lipid-lowering response to statin treatment in diabetes: a GoDARTS study
source.finding: HMGCR rs17238540 G allele carriers show 22-23% reduced statin efficacy for total cholesterol and triglyceride lowering. G allele frequencies vary by population; TT homozygotes show 28% treatment failure vs 51% in G allele carriers.

TT.phenotype: Normal statin response / strong cholesterol reduction
TT.effect: Homozygous for reference allele. Standard HMG-CoA reductase expression. Optimal statin efficacy. Mean total cholesterol reduction of 41.8 mg/dL with pravastatin. 28% fail to reach cholesterol targets. Represents ~95.7% of Europeans.
TT.recommendation: Standard statin dosing and monitoring. Expect good cholesterol reduction. Assess response at 4-6 weeks and titrate as needed per lipid guidelines.

TG.phenotype: Reduced statin response / moderate cholesterol reduction
TG.effect: Heterozygous G carrier. Modestly reduced HMG-CoA reductase expression or activity. Approximately 22-23% reduced statin efficacy compared to TT. Mean total cholesterol reduction of 32.5 mg/dL with pravastatin. Higher failure rates to reach targets (51% in some cohorts).
TG.recommendation: May require higher statin doses to achieve target LDL-C. Intensify lipid monitoring at 4-6 weeks. Consider combination therapy (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) if targets not met with standard dosing.

GT.phenotype: Reduced statin response / moderate cholesterol reduction
GT.effect: Heterozygous G carrier. Modestly reduced HMG-CoA reductase expression or activity. Approximately 22-23% reduced statin efficacy compared to TT. Mean total cholesterol reduction of 32.5 mg/dL with pravastatin. Higher failure rates to reach targets (51% in some cohorts).
GT.recommendation: May require higher statin doses to achieve target LDL-C. Intensify lipid monitoring at 4-6 weeks. Consider combination therapy (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) if targets not met with standard dosing.

GG.effect: Homozygous for minor allele (rare, ~0.06% in Europeans). Marked reduction in statin efficacy. Substantially impaired cholesterol lowering response. Highest failure rates for reaching lipid targets.
GG.recommendation: Likely to require higher statin doses or combination lipid-lowering therapy. Early intensive monitoring essential. Consider PCSK9 inhibitors or ezetimibe addition. Genetic counseling regarding familial hypercholesterolemia risk.

gene_description: 5-Hydroxytryptamine Receptor 1A — inhibitory serotonin autoreceptor and postsynaptic receptor in limbic regions. Modulates serotonergic tone and influences SSRI onset of action and treatment response.

source.title: Association between the 5-HT1A receptor gene polymorphism (rs6295) and antidepressants: a meta-analysis
source.finding: HTR1A rs6295 C-1019G: GG homozygotes showed higher baseline depressive symptoms and significantly lower SSRI response rates. G allele abolishes Deaf1 repressor binding, increasing presynaptic 5-HT1A expression and reducing serotonergic firing.

GG.phenotype: Reduced SSRI Response — Elevated Autoreceptor Expression
GG.effect: Homozygous for the G allele at C-1019G. The G allele abolishes binding of the Deaf1 and Hes5 transcriptional repressors, leading to increased presynaptic 5-HT1A autoreceptor expression in the raphe nuclei. This reduces serotonergic neuron firing rate, counteracting SSRI effects. Associated with higher baseline depressive symptoms, lower treatment response rates, and potentially longer time to onset of SSRI action. When combined with SLC6A4 SS genotype, risk of treatment failure increases 74%.
GG.recommendation: If SSRI response is slow or inadequate, consider augmentation with buspirone (a partial 5-HT1A agonist that may desensitize autoreceptors) or switch to vilazodone which combines SSRI and 5-HT1A partial agonist properties. Allow longer trial duration as autoreceptor desensitization may take longer. Pindolol augmentation has also been studied for this genotype.

GC.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Partial increase in presynaptic 5-HT1A expression. Intermediate effects on serotonergic firing and SSRI response kinetics.
GC.recommendation: Standard SSRI dosing with monitoring. May benefit from adequate trial duration of at least 6-8 weeks before switching.

CG.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Partial increase in presynaptic 5-HT1A expression. Intermediate effects on serotonergic firing and SSRI response kinetics.
CG.recommendation: Standard SSRI dosing with monitoring. May benefit from adequate trial duration of at least 6-8 weeks before switching.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the C allele. Intact Deaf1 repressor binding maintains appropriate presynaptic 5-HT1A autoreceptor expression. Normal serotonergic firing dynamics support effective SSRI action. Associated with better treatment response to fluoxetine and other SSRIs.
CC.recommendation: SSRIs expected to be effective with standard dosing and timeline. Good pharmacogenomic profile for first-line SSRI therapy.

gene_description: 5-Hydroxytryptamine Receptor 2A — major postsynaptic serotonin receptor mediating mood, cognition, and perception. Key pharmacological target for SSRIs, atypical antipsychotics, and psychedelic-assisted therapy.

source.title: Gene-Based Association Analysis Suggests Association of HTR2A With Antidepressant Treatment Response in Depressed Patients
source.finding: HTR2A rs7997012 A-allele homozygotes showed significantly better citalopram response in STAR*D (n=1953). Combined GRIK4+HTR2A risk alleles showed 23% reduction in non-response risk.

GG.effect: Homozygous for the G allele. Associated with lower remission rates to citalopram in the STAR*D study. When combined with GRIK4 rs1954787 TT genotype, risk of non-response increases significantly. The variant affects intronic regulation of HTR2A expression and serotonergic signaling dynamics.
GG.recommendation: If SSRI response is suboptimal after 4-6 weeks, consider earlier switch to alternative antidepressant class or augmentation. Evaluate GRIK4 genotype for combined risk assessment. Consider SNRIs or atypical antidepressants as alternatives.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate likelihood of SSRI response. One copy of the protective A allele partially compensates for the G allele effect.
GA.recommendation: Standard SSRI dosing and monitoring. Allow adequate trial duration before switching.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate likelihood of SSRI response. One copy of the protective A allele partially compensates for the G allele effect.
AG.recommendation: Standard SSRI dosing and monitoring. Allow adequate trial duration before switching.

AA.effect: Homozygous for the A allele. Best citalopram response profile in STAR*D. When combined with GRIK4 C-allele, remission likelihood is significantly enhanced. Favorable HTR2A expression profile for serotonergic antidepressant response.
AA.recommendation: SSRIs are likely to be effective. Standard dosing per clinical guidelines.

GG.effect: Homozygous for the G allele at the -1438A/G promoter position. Associated with altered HTR2A transcription, though results across studies are inconsistent regarding direction of effect on antidepressant response. In linkage disequilibrium with rs6313 (102T/C).
GG.recommendation: Consider in context of overall pharmacogenomic profile. Standalone predictive value is limited. Standard antidepressant dosing with monitoring.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate effect on HTR2A promoter activity.
GA.recommendation: Standard dosing per clinical guidelines.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate effect on HTR2A promoter activity.
AG.recommendation: Standard dosing per clinical guidelines.

AA.effect: Wild-type promoter. Standard HTR2A expression and typical serotonergic receptor signaling.
AA.recommendation: Standard dosing per clinical guidelines.

gene_description: 5-Hydroxytryptamine receptor 2C — serotonin receptor involved in appetite regulation and energy homeostasis; rs3813929 (-759C/T) promoter variant modulates receptor expression and influences antipsychotic-induced weight gain

source.title: Polymorphisms of the HTR2C gene and antipsychotic-induced weight gain: an update and meta-analysis
source.finding: HTR2C rs3813929 C allele (common, -759C) is associated with significantly greater antipsychotic-induced weight gain, particularly with olanzapine and clozapine. T allele carriers (-759T) show a protective effect against weight gain during atypical antipsychotic treatment.

CC.phenotype: Higher risk of antipsychotic-induced weight gain
CC.effect: Homozygous for the common -759C allele associated with higher HTR2C promoter activity. Significantly greater weight gain during treatment with atypical antipsychotics, especially olanzapine and clozapine. Meta-analyses show CC carriers gain on average 2–3 kg more than T carriers over treatment periods.
CC.recommendation: Prefer antipsychotics with lower metabolic liability (aripiprazole, ziprasidone, lurasidone) when clinically appropriate. If olanzapine or clozapine is necessary, implement proactive weight management: dietary counseling, exercise program, baseline and monthly weight/BMI/waist monitoring. Consider metformin co-prescription for weight mitigation.

CT.phenotype: Intermediate risk of antipsychotic-induced weight gain
CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers have intermediate weight gain risk. The T allele confers partial protection against antipsychotic-induced metabolic effects. Weight gain typically less pronounced than CC homozygotes.
CT.recommendation: Standard antipsychotic prescribing. Monitor weight and metabolic markers. Implement lifestyle counseling as part of routine care. No specific agent restriction based on this genotype alone.

TC.phenotype: Intermediate risk of antipsychotic-induced weight gain
TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers have intermediate weight gain risk. The T allele confers partial protection against antipsychotic-induced metabolic effects. Weight gain typically less pronounced than CC homozygotes.
TC.recommendation: Standard antipsychotic prescribing. Monitor weight and metabolic markers. Implement lifestyle counseling as part of routine care. No specific agent restriction based on this genotype alone.

TT.phenotype: Lower risk of antipsychotic-induced weight gain (protective)
TT.effect: Homozygous -759T carriers show the lowest weight gain during atypical antipsychotic therapy. The T allele reduces HTR2C promoter activity, attenuating the serotonergic appetite-stimulating pathway that mediates antipsychotic weight gain.
TT.recommendation: Reduced metabolic concern for this pharmacogenomic locus. Standard antipsychotic selection and dosing. Routine metabolic monitoring still recommended as weight gain is multifactorial.

gene_description: Interleukin-23 receptor (IL23R) — component of IL-23 signaling pathway; Arg381Gln variant reduces receptor function and mediates response to IL-12/23 biologics in psoriasis and inflammatory bowel disease

source.title: Polymorphisms of IL12B and IL23R genes are associated with psoriasis
source.finding: rs11209026 Gln381 allele (protective variant) showed strong association with psoriasis (OR=1.40, p=3.8×10^-4 for C allele); confers protection from Crohn's disease; molecular basis for IL-23 targeted therapy

CC.effect: Homozygous C carriers (Arg381Arg) have full IL-23 receptor function. Enhanced IL-23 signaling contributes to T-cell mediated inflammation. Strong genetic susceptibility to psoriasis and inflammatory bowel disease. Normal or reduced response to IL-23 targeted biologics in some studies.
CC.recommendation: IL-12/23 inhibitors (ustekinumab, risankizumab, guselkumab) indicated. However, TNF-alpha inhibitors or other biologic classes may offer comparable or better efficacy in this genotype. Discuss treatment options with dermatologist. Monitor response closely at 12-16 weeks and adjust therapy if needed.

CA.effect: Heterozygous C/A carriers show intermediate IL-23R function. Heterozygotes carry one protective allele, reducing but not eliminating IL-23 pathway contribution to psoriasis. Favorable response to IL-23 targeted biologics.
CA.recommendation: IL-12/23 inhibitors are effective first-line biologic choice. Standard dosing of ustekinumab (45-90 mg), risankizumab (150 mg), or guselkumab (100 mg) recommended. Expected response within 12-16 weeks.

AC.effect: Heterozygous A/C carriers show intermediate IL-23R function. Heterozygotes carry one protective allele, reducing but not eliminating IL-23 pathway contribution to psoriasis. Favorable response to IL-23 targeted biologics.
AC.recommendation: IL-12/23 inhibitors are effective first-line biologic choice. Standard dosing of ustekinumab (45-90 mg), risankizumab (150 mg), or guselkumab (100 mg) recommended. Expected response within 12-16 weeks.

AA.effect: Homozygous A carriers (Gln381Gln) have reduced IL-23 receptor function due to Arg381Gln missense mutation. Protective against psoriasis and IBD development. Minimal contribution of IL-23 pathway to disease. Exceptional response to IL-23 targeted biologics.
AA.recommendation: IL-12/23 inhibitors are highly effective in this genotype with excellent response rates. Ustekinumab, risankizumab, and guselkumab all appropriate; expect strong PASI response within 12-16 weeks. This genotype predicts favorable biologic therapy outcomes.

gene_description: Interleukin 28B / Interferon Lambda 3 (IL28B/IFNL3) — type III interferon essential for innate immune response to viral infection and mucosal immunity

source.title: Genetic variation in IL28B predicts hepatitis C treatment-induced viral clearance
source.finding: rs12979860 CC genotype 2-3 times more likely to clear HCV spontaneously or with treatment (~80%) vs TT genotype (~25%). C allele associated with better innate viral immunity. Extends beyond HCV to other viral infections including respiratory viruses.

CC.effect: Homozygous for C allele. Optimal IL28B/IFNL3 expression and innate immune response to viral pathogens. Enhanced interferon lambda production and mucosal immunity. Approximately 80% clearance rate for HCV (vs 25% in TT genotype). Faster viral clearance observed in respiratory viral infections. Better spontaneous recovery from viral infections.
CC.recommendation: Good innate antiviral immunity. Standard prevention practices (vaccination, hygiene) sufficient. Favorable prognosis if exposed to hepatitis C or other viral infections.

CT.effect: Heterozygous for C/T alleles. Intermediate IL28B expression and immune response. HCV clearance rate approximately 50-60%. Intermediate response to viral infections.
CT.recommendation: Standard infection prevention practices. Monitor for signs of viral infection. Vaccination recommended for preventable viral diseases.

TC.effect: Heterozygous for C/T alleles. Intermediate IL28B expression and immune response. HCV clearance rate approximately 50-60%. Intermediate response to viral infections.
TC.recommendation: Standard infection prevention practices. Monitor for signs of viral infection. Vaccination recommended for preventable viral diseases.

TT.effect: Homozygous for T allele. Reduced IL28B/IFNL3 expression and weaker innate antiviral response. Only ~25% spontaneous HCV clearance rate. Higher risk of viral persistence and chronic infection. Slower clearance of respiratory viral infections.
TT.recommendation: Enhanced infection prevention essential: regular hand hygiene, vaccinations, avoid unnecessary pathogen exposure. Early antiviral treatment important if infected with hepatitis C or other serious viruses. Monitor for prolonged viral symptoms. Consider prophylaxis for high-risk exposures.

source.title: Interleukin gene polymorphisms and alopecia areata: A systematic review and meta-analysis

description: IL2RA rs3118470 is an intronic T>C polymorphism associated with increased risk of alopecia areata. The CC genotype increases autoimmune-mediated hair loss risk through altered IL-2 receptor signaling.

TT.effect: Reference homozygote
TT.recommendation: Standard hair and scalp health; baseline assessment only

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier; modest increased risk
TC.recommendation: Baseline dermatologic evaluation; monitor for patchy hair loss; education on stress management; early treatment if disease develops

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier; modest increased risk
CT.recommendation: Baseline dermatologic evaluation; monitor for patchy hair loss; education on stress management; early treatment if disease develops

CC.effect: Homozygous for risk allele; substantially increased autoimmune hair loss susceptibility
CC.recommendation: Regular dermatologic monitoring; early intervention recommended; topical corticosteroids or minoxidil; intralesional corticosteroids if patches develop; counseling on psychological impact; platelet-rich plasma or JAK inhibitors for severe cases

gene_description: Interleukin-6 (IL6) — pro-inflammatory cytokine mediating exercise-induced inflammatory response and muscle repair and remodeling

source.title: A functional polymorphism in the IL6 gene promoter (-174G/C) regulates gene expression and associates with disease risk in adults
source.finding: rs1800795 -174G allele associated with higher IL-6 expression and enhanced post-exercise IL-6 response. G carriers show less exercise-induced muscle damage (lower CK) and faster recovery vs CC carriers.

CC.effect: Homozygous for C allele (-174C/-174C). Low baseline IL-6 expression but exaggerated post-exercise IL-6 response. Higher circulating IL-6 after eccentric exercise. Increased muscle soreness and higher levels of creatine kinase (muscle damage marker). Prolonged recovery time following intense or damaging exercise.
CC.recommendation: Emphasize recovery strategies: adequate protein (1.6-2.2 g/kg/day), carbohydrate around training, omega-3 supplementation, anti-inflammatory foods (berries, leafy greens, fatty fish). Prioritize sleep (7-9 hours). Consider active recovery and stretching. Allow longer recovery between intense sessions. Cold water immersion may help reduce soreness.

GC.effect: Heterozygous for G/C alleles. Intermediate IL-6 production and post-exercise response. Moderate exercise-induced muscle inflammation and recovery time.
GC.recommendation: Standard training recovery practices. Adequate nutrition, sleep, and progressive training progression. Monitor recovery between intense sessions.

CG.effect: Heterozygous for G/C alleles. Intermediate IL-6 production and post-exercise response. Moderate exercise-induced muscle inflammation and recovery time.
CG.recommendation: Standard training recovery practices. Adequate nutrition, sleep, and progressive training progression. Monitor recovery between intense sessions.

GG.effect: Homozygous for G allele (-174G/-174G). Higher baseline and post-exercise IL-6 expression. Optimal IL-6 mediated muscle repair response. Lower exercise-induced muscle damage markers (CK). Faster recovery and less DOMS (delayed-onset muscle soreness) following eccentric exercise.
GG.recommendation: Favorable recovery profile allows more frequent intense training. Standard training nutrition and recovery practices sufficient. Can tolerate higher training frequency and volume.

gene_description: Interleukin-6 — pro-inflammatory cytokine involved in immune response, inflammation, and metabolic regulation. The -174G/C promoter polymorphism affects IL-6 expression and modulates insulin resistance, cardiovascular risk, and dietary fat response.

source.title: IL-6 gene rs1800795 polymorphism and diabetes mellitus: a comprehensive analysis involving 42,150 participants from a meta-analysis
source.finding: IL6 rs1800795 -174G/C polymorphism modulates diabetes risk in interaction with obesity and dietary fat intake. The G allele is associated with higher IL-6 levels and increased inflammatory tone, while the C allele in obese individuals paradoxically shows highest insulin resistance.

GG.effect: Homozygous G carriers have highest IL-6 transcription and serum levels. Associated with elevated systemic inflammation, increased cardiovascular disease risk (meta-analysis of 74 studies, 86,229 subjects), and modified insulin sensitivity response to dietary fat. Higher inflammatory response to high-fat diets.
GG.recommendation: Anti-inflammatory dietary pattern recommended (Mediterranean diet, rich in omega-3, polyphenols, fiber). Limit saturated fat and ultra-processed foods. Regular exercise to counter chronic inflammation. Monitor CRP and inflammatory markers.

GC.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate IL-6 levels. Moderate inflammatory response. Dietary fat-insulin resistance interaction is present but attenuated.
GC.recommendation: Balanced anti-inflammatory diet recommended. Moderate saturated fat intake. Regular physical activity.

CG.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate IL-6 levels. Moderate inflammatory response. Dietary fat-insulin resistance interaction is present but attenuated.
CG.recommendation: Balanced anti-inflammatory diet recommended. Moderate saturated fat intake. Regular physical activity.

CC.effect: Homozygous C carriers have lower baseline IL-6 levels. However, in obese individuals, the CC genotype was paradoxically associated with highest insulin resistance and greatest T2DM risk, possibly through alternative inflammatory pathways. Lower cardiovascular inflammation risk in non-obese individuals.
CC.recommendation: Weight management is critical — CC genotype in obesity shows highest insulin resistance. Standard dietary guidelines for non-obese carriers. Monitor metabolic health if overweight.

source.title: Association of IRF5 rs2004640 polymorphism and systemic lupus erythematosus: A meta-analysis

description: IRF5 rs2004640 is a functional G/T polymorphism that alters splicing and increases interferon-alpha production. The T allele is associated with increased risk of Sjogren syndrome and systemic lupus erythematosus.

GG.phenotype: Lower SLE and Sjogren's disease risk
GG.effect: Reference homozygote; protective genotype
GG.recommendation: Standard health maintenance; family counseling if autoimmune disease in family

GT.recommendation: Baseline ANA and anti-Ro/La antibody screening; monitor for symptoms (dry eyes, dry mouth, joint pain); periodic rheumatologic evaluation if symptoms

TG.recommendation: Baseline ANA and anti-Ro/La antibody screening; monitor for symptoms (dry eyes, dry mouth, joint pain); periodic rheumatologic evaluation if symptoms

TT.phenotype: Elevated SLE and Sjogren's disease risk
TT.effect: Risk allele homozygote; substantially increased autoimmune risk across populations
TT.recommendation: Comprehensive autoimmune screening (ANA, anti-Ro/La, anti-Smith, complement levels); regular rheumatology monitoring; ophthalmology evaluation for ocular involvement; renal function monitoring

gene_full_name: Potassium Voltage-Gated Channel Subfamily H Member 2
description: KCNH2 rs1805123 (K897T) variant associated with altered cardiac repolarization and QT interval duration. This common polymorphism affects the hERG potassium channel, predisposing carriers to prolonged QT and increased risk of drug-induced torsades de pointes.

source.title: Common genetic variation in the cardiac ion channel hERG (KCNH2) and QTc prolongation
source.authors: Bezzina CR, Verkerk AO, Buxton AE, et al.

TT.phenotype: High genetic risk for QT prolongation and drug-induced torsades de pointes
TT.effect: High genetic risk for QT prolongation and drug-induced torsades de pointes
TT.recommendation: Avoid or use with extreme caution: sotalol, amiodarone, dofetilide, erythromycin, haloperidol, methadone. Baseline and periodic ECG monitoring recommended. Electrolyte management (especially potassium, magnesium) important.

TC.phenotype: Moderate increased risk of QT prolongation with QT-prolonging drugs
TC.effect: Moderate increased risk of QT prolongation with QT-prolonging drugs
TC.recommendation: Use QT-prolonging drugs cautiously. Consider alternative agents when possible. ECG baseline and monitoring recommended, especially with multiple QT-prolonging drugs.

CT.phenotype: Moderate increased risk of QT prolongation with QT-prolonging drugs
CT.effect: Moderate increased risk of QT prolongation with QT-prolonging drugs
CT.recommendation: Use QT-prolonging drugs cautiously. Consider alternative agents when possible. ECG baseline and monitoring recommended, especially with multiple QT-prolonging drugs.

CC.phenotype: Normal QT interval risk; no specific KCNH2-related concerns
CC.effect: Normal QT interval risk; no specific KCNH2-related concerns
CC.recommendation: Standard dosing and monitoring for QT-prolonging drugs as per clinical practice

title: KITLG rs995030 Testicular Germ Cell Cancer Risk (Stem Cell Growth Factor)
description: Common variant in KITLG (KIT ligand), encoding the ligand for the KIT receptor tyrosine kinase. KIT signaling is critical for germ cell development and maintenance of the germline stem cell pool. The G allele (major/risk allele) is strongly associated with testicular germ cell tumor (TGCT) development, particularly seminomas. Homozygotes for the risk allele have approximately sevenfold increased TGCT risk.

source.title: Common variation in KITLG and at 5q31.3 predisposes to testicular germ cell cancer

AA.effect: baseline_testicular_cancer_risk
AA.recommendation: standard_surveillance

AG.effect: 2x_increased_testicular_cancer_risk
AG.recommendation: enhanced_self_examination_ultrasound_surveillance

GA.effect: 2x_increased_testicular_cancer_risk
GA.recommendation: enhanced_self_examination_ultrasound_surveillance

GG.effect: 7x_increased_testicular_cancer_risk
GG.recommendation: aggressive_surveillance_early_intervention_bleomycin_monitoring

gene_description: KRAS (KRAS Proto-Oncogene, GTPase) encodes a small GTPase in the MAPK signaling pathway. Activating mutations (G12C, G12D, G12V, G13D) are oncogenic drivers in ~40% of colorectal cancers and ~30% of NSCLC. Mutations in codons 12 and 13 confer resistance to anti-EGFR monoclonal antibodies.

source.title: K-ras mutations and benefit from cetuximab in advanced colorectal cancer
source.finding: KRAS mutations (codons 12/13) predict lack of benefit from cetuximab; established KRAS testing as essential before anti-EGFR therapy

AA.effect: Constitutively active KRAS; 100% resistant to anti-EGFR antibodies (cetuximab, panitumumab); responsive to G12C-specific inhibitors
AA.recommendation: Use sotorasib or adagrasib (KRAS G12C inhibitors). Do NOT use cetuximab/panitumumab. Consider combination with chemotherapy or immunotherapy.

AT.effect: KRAS G12C present; resistant to anti-EGFR therapy; sensitive to G12C-specific inhibitors
AT.recommendation: Use G12C inhibitors (sotorasib or adagrasib). Avoid cetuximab/panitumumab monotherapy. Consider combinations for improved efficacy.

TA.effect: KRAS G12C present; resistant to anti-EGFR therapy; sensitive to G12C-specific inhibitors
TA.recommendation: Use G12C inhibitors (sotorasib or adagrasib). Avoid cetuximab/panitumumab monotherapy. Consider combinations for improved efficacy.

TT.effect: Sensitive to anti-EGFR antibodies; cetuximab/panitumumab expected to be effective
TT.recommendation: Anti-EGFR monoclonal antibodies (cetuximab or panitumumab) with chemotherapy recommended. Also test for NRAS, BRAF, PI3KCA mutations.

clinical_significance: KRAS testing mandated before anti-EGFR therapy in colorectal cancer. Mutations present in ~40% of CRC and confer complete resistance to cetuximab/panitumumab. Newer G12C inhibitors offer therapeutic opportunity.

gene_description: Low-Density Lipoprotein Receptor — membrane receptor that mediates hepatic uptake of LDL cholesterol from circulation. Variants in the promoter/intron-1 region modulate receptor expression and LDL-C levels.

source.title: Identification of the Functional Variant(s) that Explain the Low-Density Lipoprotein Receptor (LDLR) GWAS SNP rs6511720 Association with Lower LDL-C and Risk of CHD
source.finding: LDLR rs6511720 T allele causes 29% increased LDLR expression via enhanced transcription factor binding, leading to lower LDL-C (effect size -0.15 to -0.26 mmol/L) and reduced coronary heart disease risk.

GG.effect: Homozygous major allele carriers have baseline LDLR expression levels. Higher LDL-C levels compared to T allele carriers. Standard coronary heart disease risk from this locus. Represents the majority genotype (~81% in Europeans).
GG.recommendation: Standard lipid management per clinical guidelines. Heart-healthy diet with focus on reducing saturated fat and increasing soluble fiber. Statin therapy if LDL-C elevated per risk assessment.

GT.effect: Heterozygous T carriers have ~15% higher LDLR expression. LDL-C reduced by approximately 0.15-0.26 mmol/L (6-10 mg/dL). Modest reduction in coronary heart disease and myocardial infarction risk.
GT.recommendation: Favorable genetic profile for LDL-C. Maintain heart-healthy lifestyle. May respond particularly well to statins. Other cardiovascular risk factors still apply.

TG.effect: Heterozygous T carriers have ~15% higher LDLR expression. LDL-C reduced by approximately 0.15-0.26 mmol/L (6-10 mg/dL). Modest reduction in coronary heart disease and myocardial infarction risk.
TG.recommendation: Favorable genetic profile for LDL-C. Maintain heart-healthy lifestyle. May respond particularly well to statins. Other cardiovascular risk factors still apply.

TT.effect: Homozygous minor allele carriers (rare, ~1%) have ~29% increased LDLR expression. Significantly lower LDL-C levels. Reduced risk of coronary heart disease and abdominal aortic aneurysm. Naturally efficient hepatic LDL clearance.
TT.recommendation: Highly favorable lipid genetics. Standard healthy lifestyle. Lowest risk from this locus.

gene_description: Leukocyte Immunoglobulin Like Receptor B5 — immune regulatory receptor involved in inflammatory response; Asp247Gly variant (rs12975366) modulates muscle injury response and susceptibility to statin-induced myopathy

source.title: A common missense variant of LILRB5 is associated with statin intolerance and myalgia
source.finding: LILRB5 rs12975366 T allele (Asp247) is associated with statin intolerance and myalgia, with elevated serum creatine kinase (CK) and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH). TT homozygotes show 2-fold increased risk of developing statin-induced myalgia and statin intolerance.

TT.phenotype: Higher risk of statin-induced myalgia and intolerance
TT.effect: Homozygous for the risk allele (Asp247). Approximately 2-fold increased risk of developing statin myalgia, muscle pain, and creatine kinase elevation. Associated with raised baseline serum CK and LDH levels indicating greater susceptibility to muscle injury during statin therapy. Higher risk of treatment discontinuation due to intolerance.
TT.recommendation: Assess baseline CK before initiating therapy. Consider alternative lipid-lowering strategies (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors, bempedoic acid) as first-line. If statin required, use lower doses with frequent CK monitoring (baseline, 1 month, 3 months, then every 6-12 months). Educate patient about myalgia symptoms. Consider pravastatin or rosuvastatin (lower myalgia rates). Avoid concurrent use of drugs that inhibit statin metabolism (clarithromycin, itraconazole, cyclosporine). Advise against intense physical exertion while initiating therapy.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate risk of myalgia and myopathy. Moderately elevated susceptibility to muscle symptoms during statin therapy compared to CC carriers. Some elevation in baseline CK/LDH observed.
TC.recommendation: Standard statin prescribing with baseline and periodic CK monitoring. Patient education about myalgia symptoms. If myalgia develops, consider dose reduction or alternative agent. Monitor for intolerance phenotype.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate risk of myalgia and myopathy. Moderately elevated susceptibility to muscle symptoms during statin therapy compared to CC carriers. Some elevation in baseline CK/LDH observed.
CT.recommendation: Standard statin prescribing with baseline and periodic CK monitoring. Patient education about myalgia symptoms. If myalgia develops, consider dose reduction or alternative agent. Monitor for intolerance phenotype.

CC.phenotype: Lower risk of statin-induced myalgia (normal susceptibility)
CC.effect: Wild-type genotype (Gly247). Normal baseline CK/LDH levels and lower susceptibility to statin-induced muscle injury. Standard tolerance profile expected during statin therapy.
CC.recommendation: Standard statin dosing and monitoring per lipid management guidelines. Routine CK monitoring if symptomatic. No specific pharmacogenomic dose adjustment needed.

gene_description: Hepatic Lipase — enzyme that hydrolyzes triglycerides and phospholipids in circulating lipoproteins, playing a central role in HDL remodeling and LDL conversion. Promoter variants affect enzyme activity and are modified by dietary fat intake.

source.title: Dietary fat modulation of hepatic lipase variant -514 C/T for lipids: a crossover randomized dietary intervention trial in Caribbean Hispanics
source.finding: LIPC rs1800588 (C-514T) T allele carriers show reduced hepatic lipase activity and higher HDL-C levels. Significant gene-diet interaction: dietary fat composition modulates the lipid effects. HDL response varies by genotype with dietary fat modification.

CC.phenotype: High hepatic lipase activity / lower HDL
CC.effect: Homozygous C carriers have highest hepatic lipase activity. Lower HDL-C levels. Increased CAD risk from this locus. Strong response to dietary fat modification — HDL may improve significantly with reduced saturated fat intake. More atherogenic lipoprotein profile with high-fat diets.
CC.recommendation: Reduce saturated fat intake to improve HDL. Increase monounsaturated fats (olive oil, avocado, nuts). Regular lipid monitoring. Consider niacin or fibrate therapy if HDL persistently low. Exercise improves HDL in this genotype.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate hepatic lipase activity and HDL levels. Moderate gene-diet interaction with dietary fat composition.
CT.recommendation: Heart-healthy fat profile. Monitor lipids annually. Balanced saturated/unsaturated fat ratio.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate hepatic lipase activity and HDL levels. Moderate gene-diet interaction with dietary fat composition.
TC.recommendation: Heart-healthy fat profile. Monitor lipids annually. Balanced saturated/unsaturated fat ratio.

TT.phenotype: Low hepatic lipase activity / higher HDL (protective)
TT.effect: Homozygous T carriers have lowest hepatic lipase activity. Higher HDL-C levels (+0.09 mmol/L vs CC). Cardioprotective lipoprotein profile from this locus. Less sensitive to high-fat dietary effects on lipids.
TT.recommendation: Favorable lipid genetics. Standard dietary guidelines. Maintain healthy lifestyle to preserve HDL advantage.

gene_description: Lipoprotein Lipase — rate-limiting enzyme for hydrolysis of triglycerides in circulating lipoproteins (chylomicrons and VLDL). Released from endothelial surface of capillaries in muscle and adipose tissue. Key determinant of plasma triglyceride and HDL levels.

source.title: Seven Lipoprotein Lipase Gene Polymorphisms, Lipid Fractions, and Coronary Disease: A HuGE Association Review and Meta-Analysis
source.finding: LPL rs285 (PvuII) and rs320 (HindIII) polymorphisms are significantly associated with triglyceride and HDL-C levels. High-fat diet modifies the association, with risk genotypes showing greater triglyceride elevation on high-fat diets.

TT.effect: Homozygous risk carriers show reduced lipoprotein lipase efficiency. Elevated plasma triglycerides. Potentially reduced HDL-C. Greater triglyceride response to high-fat diets. Meta-analysis shows association with diminished stroke risk protection.
TT.recommendation: Limit total fat intake, especially saturated fats. Omega-3 supplementation to support triglyceride management. Regular exercise (stimulates LPL activity). Monitor fasting triglycerides. Consider fibrate therapy if >500 mg/dL.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate triglyceride and HDL levels. Moderate gene-diet fat interaction.
CT.recommendation: Balanced fat intake. Regular lipid monitoring. Standard cardiovascular risk management.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate triglyceride and HDL levels. Moderate gene-diet fat interaction.
TC.recommendation: Balanced fat intake. Regular lipid monitoring. Standard cardiovascular risk management.

CC.phenotype: Normal LPL function at this locus
CC.effect: Wild-type with standard lipoprotein lipase activity. Normal triglyceride clearance from this variant.
CC.recommendation: Standard dietary guidelines.

GG.effect: Homozygous G carriers (HindIII H+H+) show elevated triglycerides and altered HDL-C levels. The variant affects transcription factor binding in intron 8, modulating LPL expression. Significantly associated with both triglycerides and HDL-C in meta-analysis. Gene-diet interaction: high-fat diet amplifies triglyceride elevation.
GG.recommendation: Low-fat dietary pattern with emphasis on unsaturated fats. Regular triglyceride monitoring. Omega-3 fatty acids for lipid management. Exercise to enhance LPL activity.

GT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate triglyceride and HDL levels.
GT.recommendation: Heart-healthy diet. Regular lipid panel monitoring.

TG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate triglyceride and HDL levels.
TG.recommendation: Heart-healthy diet. Regular lipid panel monitoring.

TT.phenotype: Normal LPL expression at this locus
TT.effect: Wild-type with standard LPL expression from this regulatory region. Normal triglyceride clearance.
TT.recommendation: Standard dietary guidelines.

gene_description: Lysophospholipase Like 1 — enzyme involved in triglyceride metabolism and fat distribution. Variants at this locus show strong sexual dimorphism, primarily affecting waist-hip ratio and visceral fat distribution in women.

source.title: Meta-analysis identifies 13 new loci associated with waist-hip ratio and reveals sexual dimorphism in the genetic basis of fat distribution
source.finding: LYPLAL1 rs2605100 is associated with waist-hip ratio with marked sexual dimorphism (stronger effect in women). The G allele associates with increased fasting triglycerides, fasting insulin, and central adiposity pattern.

GG.effect: Homozygous G carriers show increased waist-hip ratio, primarily in women. Associated with elevated fasting triglycerides and fasting insulin. Central fat distribution pattern with higher visceral fat tendency. May associate with hepatic steatosis risk. Sexual dimorphism: effect is significantly stronger in females.
GG.recommendation: Focus on waist circumference reduction through exercise (both aerobic and resistance training). Anti-inflammatory diet. For women: regular waist circumference monitoring. Reduce refined carbohydrates to manage triglycerides and insulin.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show modest central adiposity tendency, more pronounced in women. Mildly elevated triglycerides.
GA.recommendation: Standard healthy diet and exercise. Monitor waist circumference.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show modest central adiposity tendency, more pronounced in women. Mildly elevated triglycerides.
AG.recommendation: Standard healthy diet and exercise. Monitor waist circumference.

AA.effect: Wild-type with standard fat distribution pattern. Lower central adiposity tendency from this locus.
AA.recommendation: Standard dietary and lifestyle guidelines.

gene_description: Melanocortin 1 receptor (MC1R) — G protein-coupled receptor controlling melanin synthesis and determining hair and skin color

source.title: Red hair color is due to the loss of function in MC1R
source.finding: Loss-of-function variants in MC1R including rs1805007 (R151C, Arg151Cys) cause red hair and fair skin phenotype. Approximately 1-2% of global population has red hair (1-13% in Northern Europe).

CC.effect: Homozygous for cysteine at position 151 (loss-of-function mutation). Strong red hair and pale skin phenotype due to loss of MC1R receptor function. Dramatically reduced eumelanin (brown/black) production and increased pheomelanin (red/yellow) production. Highest photosensitivity among pigmentation phenotypes.
CC.recommendation: High risk for sunburn and skin cancer. Daily SPF 50+ broad-spectrum sunscreen mandatory. Avoid sun exposure during peak hours. Regular dermatologic screening recommended. Consider protective clothing and hats. Increased melanoma risk even with sun protection.

CT.effect: Heterozygous for cysteine/arginine. Variable red hair phenotype (from light red to darker red depending on other MC1R variants). Intermediate pheomelanin production. Increased photosensitivity compared to wild-type.
CT.recommendation: Enhanced sun protection needed. Daily SPF 30+ sunscreen, protective clothing, and regular skin examinations. Increased risk of sun damage and skin cancer compared to wild-type.

TC.effect: Heterozygous for cysteine/arginine. Variable red hair phenotype (from light red to darker red depending on other MC1R variants). Intermediate pheomelanin production. Increased photosensitivity compared to wild-type.
TC.recommendation: Enhanced sun protection needed. Daily SPF 30+ sunscreen, protective clothing, and regular skin examinations. Increased risk of sun damage and skin cancer compared to wild-type.

TT.effect: Homozygous for wild-type arginine at position 151. Fully functional MC1R receptor. Normal eumelanin production, brown to black hair color, and darker skin pigmentation. Normal photosensitivity.
TT.recommendation: Standard sun protection (SPF 15+). Normal risk for sun damage and skin cancer. Standard skin care recommended.

gene_description: Melanocortin 1 Receptor — G-protein coupled receptor on melanocytes controlling pigmentation. Loss-of-function variants (red hair phenotype) alter pain sensitivity and increase anesthetic requirement by ~19% through melanocortin-opioid receptor system crosstalk.

source.title: Anesthetic Requirement is Increased in Redheads
source.finding: Anesthetic requirement in redheads (MC1R variant carriers) was increased by 19% (desflurane MAC 6.2 vs 5.2 vol%), a highly statistically significant difference (p=0.0004), confirming altered pain sensitivity in MC1R loss-of-function carriers.

TT.phenotype: Increased Anesthetic Requirement — Red Hair Phenotype
TT.effect: Homozygous for the R151C variant. Major determinant of red hair phenotype. Approximately 19% higher anesthetic requirement (MAC increase). Enhanced pain sensitivity across multiple pain modalities. Increased responsiveness to certain kappa-opioid agonists (pentazocine, nalbuphine). Mechanism involves melanocortin-opioid receptor system crosstalk in both peripheral and central pathways.
TT.recommendation: Increase volatile anesthetic doses by approximately 20%. Plan for potentially higher analgesic requirements perioperatively. Local anesthetics may also have reduced efficacy — consider higher doses of lidocaine/bupivacaine. However, kappa-opioid agonists may be more effective. Communicate MC1R status to anesthesia team preoperatively.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier of R151C. May contribute to red or auburn hair depending on other MC1R variants. Intermediate increase in anesthetic requirement and pain sensitivity. Partial loss of MC1R function.
TC.recommendation: Be aware of potentially increased anesthetic and analgesic needs. Monitor depth of anesthesia carefully. Standard starting doses with readiness to titrate upward.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier of R151C. May contribute to red or auburn hair depending on other MC1R variants. Intermediate increase in anesthetic requirement and pain sensitivity. Partial loss of MC1R function.
CT.recommendation: Be aware of potentially increased anesthetic and analgesic needs. Monitor depth of anesthesia carefully. Standard starting doses with readiness to titrate upward.

CC.effect: Wild-type at this position. Normal MC1R function and standard pain sensitivity. Typical anesthetic requirement.
CC.recommendation: Standard anesthetic and analgesic dosing per clinical protocols.

TT.phenotype: Increased Anesthetic Requirement — Red Hair Variant
TT.effect: Homozygous for the R160W variant. Another major red hair determinant. Combined with rs1805007, accounts for approximately 60% of red hair cases. Similar increase in anesthetic requirement and pain sensitivity as R151C.
TT.recommendation: Same precautions as rs1805007 TT. Increase anesthetic doses by ~20%. Plan for higher analgesic needs. Inform anesthesia team.

TC.effect: Heterozygous R160W carrier. Partial effect on pigmentation and pain sensitivity.
TC.recommendation: Monitor anesthetic depth. Be prepared to titrate upward if needed.

CT.effect: Heterozygous R160W carrier. Partial effect on pigmentation and pain sensitivity.
CT.recommendation: Monitor anesthetic depth. Be prepared to titrate upward if needed.

CC.effect: Wild-type at this position. Normal MC1R function.
CC.recommendation: Standard dosing per clinical protocols.

gene_description: Melanocortin-4 Receptor — critical regulator of appetite, energy homeostasis, and body weight in the hypothalamus. Variants near MC4R are consistently associated with increased BMI and obesity risk across populations.

source.title: MC4R rs489693: a clinical risk factor for second generation antipsychotic-related weight gain?
source.finding: MC4R rs489693 A-allele carriers show 2.2-3.1 times higher weight gain with antipsychotic use. The variant also associates with higher BMI, triglycerides, leptin, and insulin levels in the general population.

AA.effect: Homozygous risk allele carriers show 2.2-3.1 fold higher weight gain, particularly with antipsychotic medications. Elevated triglycerides, leptin, and insulin levels. Increased appetite drive and reduced satiety signaling through melanocortin pathway disruption.
AA.recommendation: Focus on caloric deficit strategies and high-protein diets to enhance satiety. Monitor metabolic parameters closely if on antipsychotics. GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, tirzepatide) may be particularly beneficial. Regular physical activity strongly recommended.

AC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate weight gain susceptibility. Mildly elevated appetite drive. Modest increase in antipsychotic-related weight gain risk.
AC.recommendation: Standard dietary and lifestyle recommendations with emphasis on satiety-promoting foods. Monitor weight if starting antipsychotic medications.

CA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate weight gain susceptibility. Mildly elevated appetite drive. Modest increase in antipsychotic-related weight gain risk.
CA.recommendation: Standard dietary and lifestyle recommendations with emphasis on satiety-promoting foods. Monitor weight if starting antipsychotic medications.

CC.effect: Wild-type with standard melanocortin signaling. Normal appetite regulation and energy homeostasis from this locus.
CC.recommendation: Standard dietary guidelines. Normal expected weight response to medications.

AA.effect: Homozygous minor allele carriers show increased BMI and higher overweight/obesity risk. Associated with higher energy intake in some populations.
AA.recommendation: Calorie-controlled diet recommended. Emphasis on portion control and regular meal timing. Monitor weight trends.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show marginally increased BMI tendency.
GA.recommendation: Standard dietary and lifestyle recommendations. Maintain regular physical activity.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show marginally increased BMI tendency.
AG.recommendation: Standard dietary and lifestyle recommendations. Maintain regular physical activity.

GG.effect: Wild-type at this locus. Standard BMI and appetite regulation.
GG.recommendation: Standard dietary guidelines.

gene_description: Melanocortin-4 Receptor — critical regulator of appetite, energy homeostasis, and body weight in the hypothalamus. Variants near MC4R are consistently associated with increased BMI and obesity risk across populations. Despite MC4R pathway disruption, GLP-1 receptor agonists maintain efficacy through alternative central and peripheral mechanisms.

source.title: Tirzepatide leads to weight reduction in people with obesity due to MC4R deficiency
source.finding: In SURMOUNT-1 trial, MC4R mutation carriers (1.4%, n=32) showed comparable weight loss to non-carriers over 72 weeks (18.3% vs 19.9%). GLP-1 agonists exert anti-obesity effects through MC4R-independent pathways, maintaining effectiveness even with MC4R deficiency.

CC.phenotype: Reduced GLP-1 response, higher appetite drive
CC.effect: Homozygous C-allele carriers have significantly reduced appetite suppression with GLP-1 agonists. Higher baseline appetite and BMI. May require higher GLP-1 RA doses for equivalent weight loss outcomes. Attenuated GLP-1 signaling response due to MC4R pathway involvement in appetite regulation.
CC.recommendation: Consider higher therapeutic doses of semaglutide, tirzepatide, or liraglutide for target weight loss. Monitor appetite suppression closely and titrate dose accordingly. Combination therapy with additional weight loss medications may be warranted. Intensive behavioral support and structured dietary counseling recommended.

CT.effect: Heterozygous C-allele carriers show intermediate appetite suppression response. Moderately elevated baseline appetite. May require standard or slightly higher GLP-1 RA dosing for optimal weight loss.
CT.recommendation: Standard GLP-1 RA dosing with close monitoring of response. Dietary counseling and behavioral support recommended. Assess response at 12-16 weeks and adjust dose if needed.

TC.effect: Heterozygous C-allele carriers show intermediate appetite suppression response. Moderately elevated baseline appetite. May require standard or slightly higher GLP-1 RA dosing for optimal weight loss.
TC.recommendation: Standard GLP-1 RA dosing with close monitoring of response. Dietary counseling and behavioral support recommended. Assess response at 12-16 weeks and adjust dose if needed.

TT.phenotype: Normal GLP-1 response, standard appetite regulation
TT.effect: Wild-type at this locus with intact MC4R signaling. Normal appetite suppression and expected weight loss response to GLP-1 agonists. Standard appetite drive and satiety signaling.
TT.recommendation: Standard GLP-1 RA dosing for obesity treatment. Expected weight loss 15-22% with semaglutide or tirzepatide. Regular monitoring and standard behavioral support.

description: MCM6 regulatory variant associated with lactase persistence and lactose intolerance. The -13910 C>T variant in the regulatory region upstream of the LCT gene affects lactase enzyme expression into adulthood.

source.title: Identification of a variant associated with adult-type hypolactasia
source.authors: Enattah NS, Sahi T, Savilahti E, et al.

GG.phenotype: Lactase non-persistence with high probability of adult lactose intolerance
GG.effect: Lactase non-persistence with high probability of adult lactose intolerance
GG.recommendation: Reduce or avoid dairy products; consider lactase supplements, lactose-free alternatives, or fermented dairy products

GA.phenotype: Partial lactase persistence; mild lactose intolerance possible
GA.effect: Partial lactase persistence; mild lactose intolerance possible
GA.recommendation: May tolerate some dairy; individual tolerance varies; trial and error approach recommended

AG.phenotype: Partial lactase persistence; mild lactose intolerance possible
AG.effect: Partial lactase persistence; mild lactose intolerance possible
AG.recommendation: May tolerate some dairy; individual tolerance varies; trial and error approach recommended

AA.phenotype: Lactase persistence; able to digest dairy throughout life
AA.effect: Lactase persistence; able to digest dairy throughout life
AA.recommendation: No dietary restrictions related to lactose; normal dairy consumption is tolerable

gene_description: Meis Homeobox 1 (MEIS1) — a transcription factor essential for iron metabolism regulation in the central nervous system and circadian rhythm control. Risk variants reduce MEIS1 expression, impairing iron homeostasis and midbrain dopamine neuron function. Pleiotropic association with both insomnia and restless legs syndrome suggests shared pathways in sleep-wake regulation and iron-dependent neuronal function.

source.title: Genome-wide association analysis of insomnia complaints identifies risk genes and genetic overlap with psychiatric and metabolic traits

GG.phenotype: Increased insomnia and restless legs syndrome risk
GG.effect: Homozygous G-allele carriers show elevated insomnia risk (approximately 1.3-1.5x relative to AA carriers) and significant RLS risk (odds ratio 1.47-1.78). Reduced MEIS1 expression impairs iron metabolism in midbrain dopamine neurons, resulting in sleep onset difficulties, sleep maintenance insomnia, and periodic leg movements during sleep. Pleiotropy with RLS suggests shared iron metabolism dysfunction.

GA.phenotype: Mild increased insomnia and RLS risk
GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show modest elevation in insomnia complaints and RLS symptoms. Intermediate MEIS1 expression and iron homeostasis. Approximately 15-30% increased risk compared to AA wild-type.

AG.phenotype: Mild increased insomnia and RLS risk
AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show modest elevation in insomnia complaints and RLS symptoms. Intermediate MEIS1 expression and iron homeostasis. Approximately 15-30% increased risk compared to AA wild-type.

AA.effect: Homozygous A-allele carriers serve as reference group. Normal MEIS1 expression and central nervous system iron metabolism. Standard sleep-wake regulation and baseline insomnia prevalence without specific MEIS1-mediated risk.

gene_description: Folate metabolism, methylation cycle

source.title: MTHFR 677C→T polymorphism and risk of coronary heart disease: meta-analysis
source.finding: MTHFR 677TT genotype associated with 16% increased CHD risk. Folate supplementation may mitigate.

TT.effect: ~70% reduced MTHFR enzyme activity. Elevated homocysteine. Impaired folate metabolism.
TT.recommendation: Supplement with methylfolate (not folic acid). Monitor homocysteine levels.

CT.effect: ~35% reduced MTHFR activity. Mildly elevated homocysteine risk.
CT.recommendation: Consider methylfolate supplementation. Ensure adequate dietary folate.

CC.effect: Normal folate metabolism and MTHFR enzyme activity.
CC.recommendation: Standard dietary folate intake sufficient.

gene_description: Melatonin Receptor 1B — G-protein coupled receptor for melatonin in pancreatic beta cells. Variant rs10830963 increases receptor expression, enhancing melatonin-mediated inhibition of insulin secretion and raising fasting glucose.

source.title: Variants in MTNR1B influence fasting glucose levels
source.finding: MTNR1B rs10830963 G allele is strongly associated with increased fasting glucose (+0.07 mmol/L per allele, P=3.2×10⁻⁵⁰), decreased early-phase insulin response, and 9% increased risk of type 2 diabetes per allele (OR=1.09).

GG.phenotype: Elevated fasting glucose / impaired insulin secretion
GG.effect: Homozygous G carriers show significantly elevated fasting glucose (~0.14 mmol/L above baseline), reduced HOMA-B (beta-cell function), and impaired early-phase insulin secretion. Melatonin exerts stronger inhibition of insulin release. Eating late at night (when melatonin is high) worsens glucose tolerance in these individuals. Increased type 2 diabetes risk.
GG.recommendation: Avoid eating close to bedtime (finish meals 3+ hours before sleep). If using melatonin supplements, monitor blood glucose. Prefer early time-restricted feeding. Regular fasting glucose monitoring recommended. If diabetic, melatonin supplementation may impair glycemic control.

CG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate fasting glucose elevation (~0.07 mmol/L). Modest reduction in early-phase insulin secretion. Melatonin-glucose interaction is present but less pronounced.
CG.recommendation: Maintain regular meal timing and avoid late-night eating. Standard monitoring. Melatonin supplements unlikely to cause major issues but be aware of potential glucose effects.

GC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate fasting glucose elevation (~0.07 mmol/L). Modest reduction in early-phase insulin secretion. Melatonin-glucose interaction is present but less pronounced.
GC.recommendation: Maintain regular meal timing and avoid late-night eating. Standard monitoring. Melatonin supplements unlikely to cause major issues but be aware of potential glucose effects.

CC.effect: Wild-type with standard MTNR1B signaling. Normal fasting glucose regulation and insulin secretion from this locus. Melatonin does not excessively inhibit insulin release.
CC.recommendation: Standard dietary and metabolic guidelines. Melatonin supplements may be used without specific glucose concerns from this locus.

description: Methionine synthase and one-carbon metabolism, folate and B12 dependency

source.title: Analysis of methionine synthase reductase polymorphisms for neural tube defects risk association
source.authors: Gaughan DJ, Barbaux S, Kluijtmans LA, et al.

GG.effect: Homozygous for the G allele (risk allele), resulting in D919G homozygosity. Altered methionine synthase enzyme activity and reduced efficiency in B12-dependent remethylation. Associated with elevated homocysteine levels, impaired one-carbon metabolism.
GG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier with one G allele. Intermediate methionine synthase activity with mildly reduced efficiency in folate-B12 dependent remethylation.
AG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier with one G allele. Intermediate methionine synthase activity with mildly reduced efficiency in folate-B12 dependent remethylation.
GA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

AA.effect: Homozygous for the A allele (reference allele). Normal methionine synthase enzyme function and efficient B12-dependent remethylation.
AA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

source.title: Association of the MUC5B Promoter Polymorphism With Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis

description: MUC5B rs35705950 is the strongest genetic risk factor for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. The minor T allele increases MUC5B expression in terminal bronchi and is associated with substantially elevated IPF risk.

GG.effect: Reference homozygote
GG.recommendation: Standard pulmonary health; avoid environmental lung toxins; smoking cessation if applicable

GT.effect: Heterozygous carrier; ~6.8-9.0x increased risk for familial/sporadic IPF
GT.recommendation: Baseline high-resolution CT chest; annual pulmonary function testing; occupational exposure assessment; smoking cessation strongly recommended; family counseling

TG.effect: Heterozygous carrier; ~6.8-9.0x increased risk for familial/sporadic IPF
TG.recommendation: Baseline high-resolution CT chest; annual pulmonary function testing; occupational exposure assessment; smoking cessation strongly recommended; family counseling

TT.effect: Homozygous for risk allele; ~20.8-21.8x increased risk; dramatically elevated disease susceptibility
TT.recommendation: Baseline HRCT and PFTs immediately; referral to IPF specialist; antifibrotic therapy consideration (pirfenidone or nintedanib) even for subclinical disease; close monitoring every 3-6 months; strict avoidance of environmental triggers; genetic counseling for family members

source.title: Mutation Screening of the Myocilin Gene in Patients with Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma

description: MYOC rs74315329 (Q368X truncation) is a pathogenic variant causing primary open-angle glaucoma through impaired myocilin trafficking and aqueous humor outflow dysfunction. Most common in European populations.

CC.effect: Reference homozygote; normal myocilin function
CC.recommendation: Standard eye health; baseline intraocular pressure screening after age 40

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier for Q368X; modest increased risk with variable penetrance
CT.recommendation: Annual intraocular pressure measurement; visual field baseline; ophthalmology evaluation; family member screening; consider prophylactic monitoring

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier for Q368X; modest increased risk with variable penetrance
TC.recommendation: Annual intraocular pressure measurement; visual field baseline; ophthalmology evaluation; family member screening; consider prophylactic monitoring

TT.effect: Homozygous for pathogenic Q368X truncation; substantially increased POAG susceptibility (~15.5x risk in population studies)
TT.recommendation: Comprehensive ophthalmologic evaluation including Goldmann applanation tonometry, visual fields, and optic nerve imaging; regular monitoring every 3-6 months; topical prostaglandin analogs as first-line therapy; escalation to combination therapy if IOP control inadequate; genetic counseling for family members

title: NAT2 rs1799930 Bladder Cancer Risk (Slow Acetylator Variant)
description: Loss-of-function variant in N-acetyltransferase 2 defining slow acetylator phenotype. NAT2 encodes a phase II detoxification enzyme that inactivates aromatic amines. Slow acetylators have reduced capacity to detoxify bladder carcinogens, increasing cancer risk. This SNP is part of the NAT2*6 haplotype.

source.title: NAT2 slow acetylation and bladder cancer risk: a meta-analysis of case-control studies

GG.effect: baseline_bladder_cancer_risk
GG.recommendation: standard_surveillance

GA.effect: 1.2x_increased_bladder_cancer_risk
GA.recommendation: enhanced_surveillance_avoid_aromatic_amines

AG.effect: 1.2x_increased_bladder_cancer_risk
AG.recommendation: enhanced_surveillance_avoid_aromatic_amines

AA.effect: 1.4-2.1x_increased_bladder_cancer_risk
AA.recommendation: aggressive_surveillance_optimal_chemotherapy

gene_description: N-Acetyltransferase 2 — catalyzes the acetylation and detoxification of isoniazid and other xenobiotics. Multiple genetic variants (rs1801280, rs1799930, rs1208) define rapid, intermediate, and slow acetylator phenotypes with profound effects on isoniazid efficacy and toxicity.

source.title: Accuracy of various human NAT2 SNP genotyping panels to infer rapid, intermediate and slow acetylator phenotypes
source.finding: NAT2 polymorphisms define acetylator phenotypes with major clinical implications for isoniazid tuberculosis therapy. Slow acetylators accumulate acetylhydrazine leading to 36.2% risk of drug-induced liver injury versus 20.5% in rapid acetylators.

TT.effect: Wild-type genotype contributing to rapid acetylation phenotype. Rapid conversion of isoniazid to acetylisoniazid.
TT.recommendation: Standard isoniazid dosing 5 mg/kg daily. Monitor TB treatment response and ensure adequate plasma levels (8-24 mg/L at 2-6 hours post-dose).

TC.effect: Heterozygous NAT2*5 allele. Intermediate acetylation capacity.
TC.recommendation: Standard isoniazid dosing with therapeutic drug monitoring if available.

CT.effect: Heterozygous NAT2*5 allele. Intermediate acetylation capacity.
CT.recommendation: Standard isoniazid dosing with therapeutic drug monitoring if available.

CC.effect: Homozygous NAT2*5 allele with reduced isoniazid acetylation.
CC.recommendation: Standard dosing but with increased hepatotoxicity monitoring. Monitor liver function tests (ALT, AST, bilirubin) more frequently.

GG.effect: Wild-type genotype with normal NAT2 enzymatic activity at this position.
GG.recommendation: Standard isoniazid dosing per TB treatment guidelines.

GA.effect: Heterozygous or homozygous NAT2*6 allele reduces NAT2 activity. Decreased isoniazid acetylation and accumulation.
GA.recommendation: Standard isoniazid dosing with enhanced hepatotoxicity monitoring. Monitor LFTs at baseline, weeks 2, 4, 8, and 12 of TB treatment.

AG.effect: Heterozygous or homozygous NAT2*6 allele reduces NAT2 activity. Decreased isoniazid acetylation and accumulation.
AG.recommendation: Standard isoniazid dosing with enhanced hepatotoxicity monitoring. Monitor LFTs at baseline, weeks 2, 4, 8, and 12 of TB treatment.

AA.effect: Homozygous NAT2*6 variant with impaired isoniazid acetylation. Significant accumulation of isoniazid and toxic acetylhydrazine metabolites.
AA.recommendation: Standard isoniazid dosing but mandatory hepatic function monitoring. Consider dose reduction if baseline liver impairment or if LFT elevation develops. Enhanced patient counseling on hepatotoxicity symptoms (jaundice, dark urine, abdominal pain, pruritus).

CC.effect: Wild-type genotype with normal NAT2 catalytic activity.
CC.recommendation: Standard isoniazid dosing per TB guidelines.

CT.effect: Heterozygous NAT2*7 allele with intermediate enzyme activity.
CT.recommendation: Standard dosing with routine hepatic monitoring.

TC.effect: Heterozygous NAT2*7 allele with intermediate enzyme activity.
TC.recommendation: Standard dosing with routine hepatic monitoring.

TT.effect: Homozygous NAT2*7 with substantially reduced isoniazid acetylation capacity. Impaired detoxification of isoniazid leading to systemic accumulation.
TT.recommendation: Standard isoniazid dosing with mandatory enhanced hepatotoxicity surveillance. Monitor LFTs at baseline and weeks 2, 4, 8, and 12. Consider lower dosing if liver dysfunction develops. Educate patient on hepatotoxicity warning signs.

phenotype_interpretation.rapid_acetylator: Diplotype with functional NAT2 alleles (*4/*4). Population frequency 5-10% (higher near equator). Normal to rapid isoniazid acetylation.
phenotype_interpretation.intermediate_acetylator: One functional and one reduced-function allele (*4/*5, *4/*6, *4/*7). Population frequency 40-60%. Intermediate acetylation capacity.
phenotype_interpretation.slow_acetylator: Two reduced-function alleles (*5/*5, *5/*6, *5/*7, *6/*6, *6/*7, *7/*7). Population frequency 10-80% (variable by ancestry). Slow acetylation with drug accumulation and increased hepatotoxicity risk.

gene_description: NBPF3 (NACHT, LRR and PYD domains-containing protein 3) — variant located in intergenic region affecting ALPL gene function; ALPL is primary enzyme responsible for catabolism and clearance of vitamin B6 in plasma

source.title: Genome-wide association study of vitamin B6, vitamin B12, folate, and homocysteine blood concentrations
source.finding: rs4654748 C allele associated with 1.38 ng/mL lower plasma vitamin B6 concentrations (p<0.001); variant influences ALPL-mediated B6 catabolism

CC.effect: Homozygous C carriers have significantly lower plasma pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (PLP, active B6 form) concentrations. Variant associated with enhanced ALPL enzyme activity and accelerated B6 metabolism. Requires higher dietary intake to maintain adequate B6 status.
CC.recommendation: Increase dietary vitamin B6 intake to 1.5-2.0 mg daily (above standard RDA of 1.3-1.7 mg). Monitor for B6 deficiency symptoms (peripheral neuropathy, anemia, dermatitis). Consider B6 supplementation (50-100 mg daily) if dietary intake insufficient.

CT.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate plasma B6 levels, between homozygous groups. Modest increase in ALPL-mediated catabolism compared to TT wild-type.
CT.recommendation: Standard vitamin B6 RDA typically adequate (1.3-1.7 mg daily for adults). Monitor dietary intake, particularly if consuming alcohol or medications that increase B6 requirements. Supplementation if deficiency signs develop.

TC.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate plasma B6 levels, between homozygous groups. Modest increase in ALPL-mediated catabolism compared to TT wild-type.
TC.recommendation: Standard vitamin B6 RDA typically adequate (1.3-1.7 mg daily for adults). Monitor dietary intake, particularly if consuming alcohol or medications that increase B6 requirements. Supplementation if deficiency signs develop.

TT.effect: Wild-type homozygotes have normal ALPL activity and vitamin B6 catabolism. Normal plasma B6 levels with standard dietary intake.
TT.recommendation: Standard dietary vitamin B6 recommendations adequate (1.3-1.7 mg daily for adults). Maintain varied diet including poultry, fish, potatoes, chickpeas, and whole grains.

gene_description: Nucleotide Binding Oligomerization Domain Containing 2 — a cytoplasmic pattern recognition receptor that senses bacterial lipopolysaccharide and peptidoglycans. Frameshift and missense variants impair bacterial sensing, NF-kB signaling, and innate immune response, predisposing to Crohn's disease through dysregulated intestinal inflammation.

source.title: Association of NOD2 leucine-rich repeat variants with susceptibility to Crohn's disease
source.finding: Three independent NOD2 variants associated with Crohn's disease susceptibility: a frameshift mutation (1007fs) and two missense variants (R702W and G908R). Homozygous carriers of any variant have 17-fold increased Crohn's disease risk. Variants disrupt NOD2 protein structure in leucine-rich repeat domain or adjacent region, impairing bacterial sensing and NF-kB activation. NOD2 mediates recognition of bacterial peptidoglycans through intracellular receptors.

TT.phenotype: NOD2 R702W homozygous — highest Crohn's disease risk
TT.effect: Homozygous for R702W risk variant. ~17-fold increased Crohn's disease risk (strongest genetic association identified in 2001). Severe impairment of NOD2-mediated bacterial sensing and NF-kB activation in intestinal innate immune cells (macrophages, dendritic cells). Defective recognition of bacterial lipopolysaccharide and peptidoglycans. Loss of negative feedback on pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8). Dysregulated intestinal inflammation and early-onset, more severe Crohn's disease. Higher rates of intestinal complications (strictures, fistulae).
TT.recommendation: Early aggressive immunosuppressive therapy: TNF-α antagonists (infliximab IV, adalimumab SC) as first-line biologics. Azathioprine or 6-mercaptopurine for maintenance immunosuppression. Methotrexate if TNF inhibitors insufficient or contraindicated. Mesalamine for mild-to-moderate colonic disease. Nutritional support: consider elemental diet or parenteral nutrition if severe malabsorption. Regular monitoring for disease activity (CRP, fecal calprotectin) and complications. Preventive screening for colorectal cancer (higher risk in inflammatory bowel disease). Genetic counseling: recurrence risk in siblings ~10-15%.

TC.phenotype: NOD2 R702W heterozygous — intermediate Crohn's disease risk
TC.effect: Heterozygous for R702W variant. ~2-4 fold increased Crohn's disease risk. Partial impairment of NOD2-mediated bacterial sensing and NF-kB signaling. Reduced but not absent intestinal innate immune responsiveness. Intermediate-risk genotype with variable penetrance; not all carriers develop Crohn's disease.
TC.recommendation: Monitor for Crohn's disease symptoms: chronic diarrhea, abdominal pain, blood in stool, weight loss, anemia. Avoid known triggers if symptomatic: NSAIDs, smoking, high-stress periods. If diagnosed with Crohn's disease, consider TNF-α antagonist or other biologic therapy given NOD2-conferred susceptibility. Maintain healthy gut microbiome: consider probiotics, fermented foods, high-fiber diet (if tolerated). Screening colonoscopy if family history of IBD-related complications.

CT.phenotype: NOD2 R702W heterozygous — intermediate Crohn's disease risk
CT.effect: Heterozygous for R702W variant. ~2-4 fold increased Crohn's disease risk. Partial impairment of NOD2-mediated bacterial sensing and NF-kB signaling. Reduced but not absent intestinal innate immune responsiveness. Intermediate-risk genotype with variable penetrance; not all carriers develop Crohn's disease.
CT.recommendation: Monitor for Crohn's disease symptoms: chronic diarrhea, abdominal pain, blood in stool, weight loss, anemia. Avoid known triggers if symptomatic: NSAIDs, smoking, high-stress periods. If diagnosed with Crohn's disease, consider TNF-α antagonist or other biologic therapy given NOD2-conferred susceptibility. Maintain healthy gut microbiome: consider probiotics, fermented foods, high-fiber diet (if tolerated). Screening colonoscopy if family history of IBD-related complications.

CC.phenotype: NOD2 wild-type homozygous — standard Crohn's disease risk
CC.effect: Homozygous for wild-type C allele. Normal NOD2 protein structure and function. Standard intestinal innate immune responsiveness to bacteria. Crohn's disease risk from this locus is minimal; other genetic loci and environmental factors (smoking, microbiota, infections) play primary role.
CC.recommendation: Standard preventive measures: avoid smoking (strong Crohn's risk factor), maintain balanced diet, manage stress, avoid excessive NSAID use. Regular check-ups to detect early GI symptoms. Family screening for NOD2 if close relatives develop Crohn's disease.

gene_description: Nuclear Receptor Subfamily 1 Group D Member 1 (REV-ERBα) — heme-binding nuclear receptor that represses BMAL1 transcription, forming a critical link between the circadian clock and metabolic regulation (glucose/lipid homeostasis).

source.title: Associations between polymorphisms in the NR1D1 gene encoding for nuclear receptor REV-ERBα and circadian typologies
source.finding: NR1D1 rs12941497 genotypes are significantly associated with chronotype classification (morning/intermediate/evening types), supporting REV-ERBα's role in circadian rhythm regulation and its downstream metabolic effects.

CC.effect: Homozygous C carriers show stronger association with evening chronotype. Altered REV-ERBα-mediated circadian repression may shift metabolic timing, affecting glucose and lipid homeostasis. Evening chronotype is associated with later meal timing, increased metabolic syndrome risk, and impaired glucose tolerance.
CC.recommendation: Align meal timing with circadian biology — avoid late-night eating. Try to maintain consistent sleep-wake schedule. Morning light exposure to help entrain circadian clock. Time-restricted feeding (earlier eating window) may counteract evening chronotype metabolic effects.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate chronotype tendency. Standard circadian metabolic regulation with minor variations.
CT.recommendation: Maintain regular sleep schedule. Standard meal timing recommendations.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate chronotype tendency. Standard circadian metabolic regulation with minor variations.
TC.recommendation: Maintain regular sleep schedule. Standard meal timing recommendations.

TT.effect: Homozygous T carriers show stronger association with morning chronotype. REV-ERBα circadian repression cycle is well-aligned with standard metabolic timing. Generally favorable metabolic profile associated with morning chronotype.
TT.recommendation: Natural circadian alignment is favorable. Maintain regular schedule to preserve this advantage.

gene_description: Thiopurine toxicity (common in Asian populations)

source.title: CPIC Guideline Update for TPMT and NUDT15 Genotypes and Dosing of Thiopurines
source.finding: NUDT15 *3 homozygous: consider alternative to thiopurines. Common in Asian populations.

TT.effect: Complete NUDT15 deficiency. Very high risk of life-threatening myelosuppression with thiopurines. Especially prevalent in East Asian populations (~2%).
TT.recommendation: Avoid thiopurines or use <10% of standard dose. Consider alternative immunosuppressant.

CT.effect: Reduced NUDT15 activity. Increased risk of leukopenia with thiopurines.
CT.recommendation: Reduce thiopurine dose to 25-50% of standard. Monitor CBC weekly at start.

CC.effect: Normal NUDT15 activity.
CC.recommendation: Standard thiopurine dosing with routine monitoring.

gene_description: Mu-opioid receptor — endogenous opioid signalling in reward, analgesia, and food preference (Asn40Asp, A118G)

source.title: Opiates, overeating and obesity: a psychogenetic analysis
source.finding: OPRM1 rs1799971 (A118G): G/G carriers reported higher preference for sweet and fatty foods vs A-allele groups; preferences correlated with overeating traits and BMI.

AA.effect: Asn40 homozygotes (A/A): lowest palatable-food preference scores in the IJO 2011 cohort.
AA.recommendation: Behavioral and environmental factors still drive intake; no change to medical opioid dosing from this finding alone.

AG.effect: Heterozygotes: intermediate preference for sweet/fat foods vs AA/GG extremes in the IJO 2011 cohort.
AG.recommendation: Structure food environment; mindfulness for stress/emotional eating patterns.

GA.effect: Heterozygotes: intermediate preference for sweet/fat foods vs AA/GG extremes in the IJO 2011 cohort.
GA.recommendation: Structure food environment; mindfulness for stress/emotional eating patterns.

GG.effect: Asp40 homozygotes reported strongest preference for sweet and fatty foods, linked via questionnaires to overeating phenotypes and BMI in this cohort.
GG.recommendation: Practical: reduce cue exposure, prioritize protein/fiber meals; discuss disordered eating with a clinician if relevant. Distinct from naltrexone pharmacogenomics.

description: Oxytocin receptor genetic variation and empathy/social cognition

source.title: Oxytocin receptor genetic variation relates to empathy and stress reactivity in humans
source.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences USA
source.authors: Rodrigues SM, Saslow LR, Garcia N, et al.

AA.effect: Homozygous for the A allele (risk allele). Associated with lower empathy scores, reduced empathic accuracy on emotion recognition tasks, diminished stress buffering effect from social support.
AA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate empathy and social cognition with moderate oxytocin receptor responsiveness.
AG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate empathy and social cognition with moderate oxytocin receptor responsiveness.
GA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

GG.effect: Homozygous for the G allele (reference/favorable allele). Associated with higher empathy scores, better social cognition and empathic accuracy, enhanced stress buffering from social support.
GG.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

source.title: Phenylalanine Hydroxylase Deficiency and Phenylketonuria

description: PAH rs5030858 (R408W mutation) is the most common pathogenic variant causing phenylketonuria in European populations. Homozygous or compound heterozygous carriers develop classic PKU with severe intellectual disability if untreated.

CC.effect: Wildtype homozygote; no PKU risk
CC.recommendation: Standard newborn screening; normal diet

CT.effect: Carrier; normal phenylalanine levels but ~50% enzyme activity
