CT.recommendation: No dietary restrictions; genetic counseling if planning pregnancy; carrier testing of partner

TC.effect: Carrier; normal phenylalanine levels but ~50% enzyme activity
TC.recommendation: No dietary restrictions; genetic counseling if planning pregnancy; carrier testing of partner

TT.effect: Homozygous or compound heterozygous for pathogenic mutations; severe PAH deficiency
TT.recommendation: Immediate dietary restriction of phenylalanine; low-Phe medical formula; periodic monitoring of Phe levels; neuropsychological follow-up; ongoing metabolic management with specialist

gene_description: Proprotein Convertase Subtilisin/Kexin Type 1 — endoprotease that converts prohormones (proinsulin, POMC) to active forms. Critical for appetite regulation and energy balance. Variants increase obesity risk through impaired prohormone processing.

source.title: Contribution of common non-synonymous variants in PCSK1 to body mass index variation and risk of obesity: a systematic review and meta-analysis with evidence from up to 331,175 individuals
source.finding: PCSK1 rs6232 (N221D) and rs6235 (S690T) minor alleles are associated with 1.34-fold and 1.22-fold increased obesity risk respectively. Effects consistent across Caucasian, African, and Hispanic ethnicities. Variants impair prohormone processing including proinsulin and POMC.

AA.effect: Homozygous N221D carriers have significantly impaired proprotein convertase 1/3 activity. Elevated proinsulin levels indicating impaired proinsulin-to-insulin conversion. Increased BMI and strong obesity risk. May present with features of mild proprotein convertase deficiency.
AA.recommendation: Intensive weight management and metabolic monitoring. Proinsulin levels should be monitored. GLP-1 agonists may help with appetite regulation. High-protein, low-glycemic diet to manage insulin resistance. Consider referral to endocrinologist.

GA.effect: Heterozygous N221D carriers show mildly elevated proinsulin/insulin ratio and increased BMI. Moderate obesity risk increase. Partially impaired prohormone processing.
GA.recommendation: Weight management strategies. Regular metabolic screening. Balanced caloric intake with emphasis on satiety-promoting foods.

AG.effect: Heterozygous N221D carriers show mildly elevated proinsulin/insulin ratio and increased BMI. Moderate obesity risk increase. Partially impaired prohormone processing.
AG.recommendation: Weight management strategies. Regular metabolic screening. Balanced caloric intake with emphasis on satiety-promoting foods.

GG.phenotype: Normal PCSK1 activity at this locus
GG.effect: Wild-type N221 with full proprotein convertase activity. Normal proinsulin processing.
GG.recommendation: Standard dietary guidelines.

GG.effect: Homozygous S690T carriers show reduced PCSK1 biosynthesis and activity. Elevated proinsulin levels. Increased BMI and 1.22-fold obesity risk per allele. Effect consistent across Caucasian, African, and Hispanic populations but not seen in East Asians.
GG.recommendation: Weight management focus. Monitor proinsulin/insulin ratio. High-fiber, high-protein diet to enhance satiety. Regular physical activity.

CG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate PCSK1 activity and modest BMI increase.
CG.recommendation: Standard healthy diet with emphasis on portion control. Regular exercise.

GC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate PCSK1 activity and modest BMI increase.
GC.recommendation: Standard healthy diet with emphasis on portion control. Regular exercise.

CC.phenotype: Normal PCSK1 activity at this locus
CC.effect: Wild-type S690 with full proprotein convertase activity.
CC.recommendation: Standard dietary guidelines.

gene_description: Proprotein Convertase Subtilisin/Kexin Type 1 — converts proglucagon to active GLP-1 in the central nervous system. PCSK1 genetic variants affect GLP-1 biosynthesis, metabolism, and circulating levels. Critical for appetite regulation, satiety signaling, and glucose homeostasis.

source.title: Contribution of common non-synonymous variants in PCSK1 to body mass index variation and obesity risk
source.finding: Meta-analysis of 331,175 individuals found rs6232 (N221D) and rs6235 (S690T) associated with obesity. Each additional minor allele of rs6232 increased obesity risk 1.34-fold; rs6235 increased risk 1.22-fold. Variants affect GLP-1 processing and metabolism.

TT.phenotype: Reduced GLP-1 production, impaired appetite suppression
TT.effect: Homozygous T-allele (N221D risk variant) carriers have significantly reduced PCSK1 enzymatic activity. Impaired proglucagon-to-GLP-1 conversion in enteric neurons and CNS. Reduced circulating GLP-1 levels and attenuated appetite suppression response. Higher baseline BMI and increased appetite.
TT.recommendation: May benefit from higher doses of GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide) to overcome reduced endogenous GLP-1 response. Monitor appetite suppression and consider dose escalation if inadequate response at 4-6 weeks. Combination therapy with other weight loss medications may be needed. Intensive dietary intervention and behavioral counseling recommended.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate PCSK1 activity. Mildly reduced GLP-1 production. Intermediate appetite suppression response to GLP-1 agonists.
CT.recommendation: Standard GLP-1 RA dosing with careful monitoring of response. Reassess at 8-12 weeks; consider dose optimization if inadequate weight loss. Dietary support and regular monitoring recommended.

CC.phenotype: Normal GLP-1 production, normal appetite response
CC.effect: Wild-type at this locus with normal PCSK1 enzyme activity. Normal proglucagon processing and GLP-1 production. Expected appetite suppression and weight loss response to GLP-1 agonists.
CC.recommendation: Standard GLP-1 RA dosing for obesity treatment. Expected weight loss response 12-20% with liraglutide or semaglutide. Regular monitoring and standard behavioral support.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate PCSK1 activity. Mildly reduced GLP-1 production. Intermediate appetite suppression response to GLP-1 agonists.
TC.recommendation: Standard GLP-1 RA dosing with careful monitoring of response. Reassess at 8-12 weeks; consider dose optimization if inadequate weight loss. Dietary support and regular monitoring recommended.

TT.effect: Homozygous T-allele (S690T risk variant) carriers show reduced PCSK1 activity affecting GLP-1 processing. Moderate reduction in GLP-1 levels and appetite suppression efficacy. Increased BMI and higher obesity risk.
TT.recommendation: Consider dose optimization of GLP-1 RA therapy. Monitor response carefully at 6-8 weeks and escalate dose if needed. Dietary intervention and lifestyle modification strongly recommended. May benefit from combination weight loss therapy.

CT.effect: Heterozygous T-allele carriers show slightly reduced GLP-1 pathway function. Mildly increased BMI compared to CC homozygotes.
CT.recommendation: Standard GLP-1 RA dosing with monitoring. Lifestyle and dietary support recommended. Reassess response at 8-12 weeks.

CC.phenotype: Normal GLP-1 signaling and appetite regulation
CC.effect: Wild-type with normal PCSK1 enzyme function. Normal GLP-1 production and appetite suppression. Standard response to GLP-1 agonists expected.
CC.recommendation: Standard GLP-1 RA dosing for obesity treatment. Expected weight loss 12-20% with standard doses. Regular monitoring and behavioral support.

TC.effect: Heterozygous T-allele carriers show slightly reduced GLP-1 pathway function. Mildly increased BMI compared to CC homozygotes.
TC.recommendation: Standard GLP-1 RA dosing with monitoring. Lifestyle and dietary support recommended. Reassess response at 8-12 weeks.

gene_description: Proprotein Convertase Subtilisin/Kexin Type 9 — regulates LDL receptor degradation. Loss-of-function variants reduce PCSK9 levels, enhancing LDL receptor-mediated cholesterol clearance. PCSK9 inhibitors (evolocumab, alirocumab) block this pathway.

source.title: The effect of genetic variation in PCSK9 on the LDL-cholesterol response to statin therapy
source.finding: PCSK9 rs11591147 R46L loss-of-function variant carriers show 55.6% greater LDL-C reduction with rosuvastatin. T allele (loss-of-function) associated with 0.5 mmol/L lower LDL-C and 23% reduced coronary heart disease risk.

CC.phenotype: Normal PCSK9 function / standard LDL response
CC.effect: Homozygous for normal function allele. Standard PCSK9 expression and LDL receptor degradation. Standard statin response. LDL-C reduction follows typical response curves. Baseline coronary heart disease risk from this locus.
CC.recommendation: Standard statin dosing per clinical guidelines. Assess LDL-C response at 4-6 weeks. If targets not met, consider dose escalation or combination with ezetimibe or PCSK9 inhibitors.

CT.effect: One copy of T allele (loss-of-function). Reduced PCSK9 levels and enhanced LDL receptor activity. Approximately 27.8% greater LDL-C reduction with statins (in carriers vs non-carriers). Approximately 0.25 mmol/L lower LDL-C. Reduced coronary heart disease risk (~11-12% reduction).
CT.recommendation: Favorable statin response profile. Standard statin dosing likely effective. May achieve target LDL-C more easily. Excellent candidacy for PCSK9 inhibitor therapy if additional cholesterol lowering needed.

TC.effect: One copy of T allele (loss-of-function). Reduced PCSK9 levels and enhanced LDL receptor activity. Approximately 27.8% greater LDL-C reduction with statins (in carriers vs non-carriers). Approximately 0.25 mmol/L lower LDL-C. Reduced coronary heart disease risk (~11-12% reduction).
TC.recommendation: Favorable statin response profile. Standard statin dosing likely effective. May achieve target LDL-C more easily. Excellent candidacy for PCSK9 inhibitor therapy if additional cholesterol lowering needed.

TT.effect: Homozygous for loss-of-function allele (rare, ~1.5% in Caucasians, 3-5% in some Asian populations). Markedly reduced PCSK9 activity. Maximum LDL receptor-mediated cholesterol clearance. 55.6% greater LDL-C reduction with statins. Significantly lower baseline LDL-C. Substantially reduced coronary heart disease risk (23% reduction).
TT.recommendation: Excellent genetic profile for cholesterol lowering. Standard statin dosing likely highly effective at achieving target LDL-C. Strong candidate for PCSK9 inhibitor therapy if further reduction needed. May benefit from combination therapy for very aggressive LDL lowering.

gene_description: Phosphatidylethanolamine N-methyltransferase (PEMT) — catalyzes endogenous synthesis of phosphatidylcholine from phosphatidylethanolamine, primary source of choline for liver and brain

source.title: Genetic variants in phosphatidylethanolamine N-methyltransferase (PEMT) and methylenetetrahydrofolate dehydrogenase (MTHFD1) influence biomarkers of choline metabolism when folate intake is restricted
source.finding: rs12325817 variant allele associated with reduced estrogen-induced PEMT expression. 40-75% of women carry this polymorphism. Two copies increase choline deficiency organ dysfunction risk to 80% on low-choline diet vs 13% in non-carriers.

GG.effect: Homozygous for variant G allele. Significantly reduced PEMT expression and choline synthesis capacity, particularly in response to estrogen signaling. Premenopausal women show 80% risk of organ dysfunction (fatty liver, muscle damage) on low-choline diet. Reduced phosphatidylcholine synthesis increases VLDL lipid export demands.
GG.recommendation: Higher dietary choline intake essential, especially for premenopausal women. Target 550 mg/day for women and 550-625 mg/day for men. Include choline-rich foods (eggs, liver, fish, nuts, seeds, Brussels sprouts) or consider supplementation. Monitor liver function with adequate choline intake.

AG.effect: Heterozygous for variant G/wild-type A alleles. Intermediate PEMT expression and choline synthesis capacity. Premenopausal women show ~43% risk of organ dysfunction on low-choline diet. Some reduction in estrogen-responsive choline synthesis.
AG.recommendation: Adequate dietary choline intake recommended (425-550 mg/day depending on sex). Include regular dietary sources of choline. Monitor for signs of choline deficiency (muscle pain, fatty liver) especially on restrictive diets.

GA.effect: Heterozygous for variant G/wild-type A alleles. Intermediate PEMT expression and choline synthesis capacity. Premenopausal women show ~43% risk of organ dysfunction on low-choline diet. Some reduction in estrogen-responsive choline synthesis.
GA.recommendation: Adequate dietary choline intake recommended (425-550 mg/day depending on sex). Include regular dietary sources of choline. Monitor for signs of choline deficiency (muscle pain, fatty liver) especially on restrictive diets.

AA.effect: Homozygous for wild-type A allele. Normal PEMT expression and capacity for endogenous choline synthesis, particularly responsive to estrogen in women. Low risk (13%) of organ dysfunction even on low-choline diet. Adequate de novo choline production from dietary folate and methionine.
AA.recommendation: Standard dietary choline intake sufficient (425-550 mg/day). No special supplementation needed unless on very restricted diet. Regular balanced diet with diverse nutrient sources adequate.

gene_description: Period Circadian Regulator 2 — core clock gene in the mammalian suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN). PER2 protein is essential for maintaining circadian oscillations that regulate sleep-wake cycles, metabolism, and eating behavior patterns.

source.title: PER2 rs2304672 Polymorphism Moderates Circadian-Relevant Reward Circuitry Activity in Adolescents
source.finding: PER2 rs2304672 G allele moderates reward circuitry activity in the medial prefrontal cortex as a function of sleep timing, with later sleep midpoint associated with reduced reward response. G carriers also show longer sleep duration and greater probability of dietary treatment withdrawal.

GG.effect: Homozygous G carriers (rare, ~2%) show significantly altered circadian reward circuitry. Longer sleep duration (mean 8.2h vs 7.5h). Reduced medial prefrontal cortex reward response with later sleep timing. Greater probability of withdrawal from dietary obesity treatments. Altered chronotype with possible evening preference.
GG.recommendation: Tailor dietary interventions to account for lower adherence tendency — favor simpler, flexible dietary approaches over strict regimens. Leverage longer sleep duration as metabolic advantage. Avoid late-night eating. Behavioral support for diet compliance.

CG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate effects on sleep duration and reward circuitry modulation. Mild impact on dietary treatment adherence.
CG.recommendation: Standard dietary and sleep hygiene recommendations. Be aware of potential challenges with strict dietary programs.

GC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate effects on sleep duration and reward circuitry modulation. Mild impact on dietary treatment adherence.
GC.recommendation: Standard dietary and sleep hygiene recommendations. Be aware of potential challenges with strict dietary programs.

CC.effect: Wild-type with standard circadian period regulation. Normal sleep duration (~7.5h) and reward circuitry modulation. Standard dietary treatment adherence from this locus.
CC.recommendation: Standard dietary and sleep guidelines.

description: Period circadian regulator 2 and chronotype/circadian preference

source.title: Genome-wide association analyses of chronotype in 697,828 individuals provides insights into circadian rhythms
source.authors: Jones SE, Tyrrell J, Wood AR, et al.

TT.effect: Homozygous for the T allele (risk allele). Associated with strong evening/night chronotype preference, longer intrinsic circadian period (~24.3+ hours), difficulty with early morning schedules.
TT.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate chronotype with slightly delayed sleep preference.
TC.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate chronotype with slightly delayed sleep preference.
CT.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the C allele (reference allele). Associated with morning/early chronotype preference or standard sleep timing, typical intrinsic circadian period (~24 hours).
CC.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

source.title: A Common Variant Associated with Recurrent Myocardial Infarction

description: PITX2 rs2200733 is strongly associated with atrial fibrillation and increased risk of cardioembolic stroke. The variant on chromosome 4q25 is located upstream of the PITX2 gene.

CC.phenotype: Lower atrial fibrillation and stroke risk
CC.effect: Protective homozygote
CC.recommendation: Standard cardiovascular monitoring; maintain healthy lifestyle

CT.phenotype: Intermediate atrial fibrillation and stroke risk
CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier; modest increased risk
CT.recommendation: Regular cardiac monitoring; consider ECG screening; manage cardiovascular risk factors

TC.phenotype: Intermediate atrial fibrillation and stroke risk
TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier; modest increased risk
TC.recommendation: Regular cardiac monitoring; consider ECG screening; manage cardiovascular risk factors

TT.phenotype: High atrial fibrillation and stroke risk
TT.effect: Risk allele homozygote; ~1.4x increased risk per copy
TT.recommendation: Close cardiac monitoring; annual ECG; stroke risk assessment; consider anticoagulation evaluation if AF develops

gene_description: Patatin-like phospholipase domain-containing 3 — hepatic fat accumulation and NAFLD/NASH risk (I148M)

source.title: Genetic variation in PNPLA3 confers susceptibility to nonalcoholic fatty liver disease
source.finding: rs738409 (I148M) G allele strongly associates with hepatic steatosis and NAFLD across ancestries; effect strongest for GG.

CC.effect: No I148M variant. Lower PNPLA3-associated NAFLD genetic burden than G carriers (population and lifestyle still dominant).
CC.recommendation: Standard metabolic liver health: weight, alcohol, lipids, glucose; imaging if clinically indicated.

CG.effect: One copy of I148M (G). Meta-analyses report ~1.5–2x higher NAFLD odds vs CC with greater ALT/fibrosis signal than non-carriers.
CG.recommendation: Emphasize weight and cardiometabolic control; discuss NAFLD screening with clinician if risk factors.

GC.effect: One copy of I148M (G). Same interpretation as CG.
GC.recommendation: Emphasize weight and cardiometabolic control; discuss NAFLD screening with clinician if risk factors.

GG.effect: Homozygous I148M. Strongly associated with higher liver fat, NASH, and fibrosis progression in multiple cohorts.
GG.recommendation: High priority for metabolic risk reduction and hepatology follow-up when steatosis or elevated LFTs are suspected.

gene_description: Patatin-Like Phospholipase Domain Containing 3 — a membrane-bound enzyme involved in hepatic fat metabolism, triacylglycerol hydrolysis, and lipid remodeling. Variants disrupt adipose triglyceride lipase function, impairing hepatic fat mobilization and increasing NAFLD and fibrosis risk.

source.title: Genetic variation in PNPLA3 confers susceptibility to nonalcoholic fatty liver disease
source.finding: The I148M variant (rs738409-G) is strongly associated with increased hepatic fat levels (P = 5.9 × 10⁻¹⁰) and hepatic inflammation. Hepatic fat content is >2-fold higher in PNPLA3 rs738409[G] homozygotes vs. noncarriers. G allele most common in Hispanics (highest NAFLD risk group). Variant impairs hepatic fat mobilization and adipose triglyceride lipase activity.

GG.phenotype: PNPLA3 I148M/I148M homozygous — highest NAFLD/NASH/fibrosis risk
GG.effect: Homozygous for I148M variant. ~3.3-fold increased NAFLD risk, ~5-fold increased NASH and hepatic fibrosis risk. Markedly impaired hepatic lipid mobilization. Accumulation of triglycerides in hepatocytes. Increased inflammation and progression to cirrhosis. Severely reduced adipose triglyceride lipase activity.
GG.recommendation: Aggressive NAFLD management: weight loss (7-10% reduces fibrosis risk). Avoid alcohol completely. Monitor ALT/AST, FIB-4 index, and consider FibroScan annually. Consider pioglitazone or vitamin E if NASH diagnosed (biopsy-proven). Evaluate for hepatocellular carcinoma screening if cirrhosis develops. Semaglutide may provide benefit via weight loss and metabolic improvement. Avoid high-fat/high-sugar diet.

GC.phenotype: PNPLA3 I148M/M148 heterozygous — intermediate NAFLD risk
GC.effect: Heterozygous for I148M variant. ~1.7-fold increased NAFLD risk. Intermediate impairment of hepatic lipid metabolism. Increased hepatic fat content but not to the extent of homozygotes. Modest increase in inflammation and fibrosis progression risk.
GC.recommendation: Moderate lifestyle intervention: weight loss if overweight (5% reduction beneficial). Limit alcohol. Monitor liver function annually. Consider FibroScan if additional risk factors (metabolic syndrome, elevated transaminases). Mediterranean or low-fat diet. Regular exercise (150 min/week moderate intensity).

CG.phenotype: PNPLA3 I148M/M148 heterozygous — intermediate NAFLD risk
CG.effect: Heterozygous for I148M variant. ~1.7-fold increased NAFLD risk. Intermediate impairment of hepatic lipid metabolism. Increased hepatic fat content but not to the extent of homozygotes. Modest increase in inflammation and fibrosis progression risk.
CG.recommendation: Moderate lifestyle intervention: weight loss if overweight (5% reduction beneficial). Limit alcohol. Monitor liver function annually. Consider FibroScan if additional risk factors (metabolic syndrome, elevated transaminases). Mediterranean or low-fat diet. Regular exercise (150 min/week moderate intensity).

CC.phenotype: PNPLA3 M148/M148 wild-type — standard NAFLD risk
CC.effect: Homozygous for wild-type (M148) allele. Standard hepatic lipid metabolism. Normal adipose triglyceride lipase function. NAFLD risk driven primarily by metabolic factors (obesity, insulin resistance, diet) rather than genetic predisposition at this locus.
CC.recommendation: Standard preventive guidelines: maintain healthy weight, balanced diet, moderate alcohol, regular exercise. Monitor for metabolic syndrome. Liver function tests if other risk factors present.

gene_description: Peroxisome Proliferator-Activated Receptor Gamma — nuclear receptor and transcription factor highly expressed in adipose tissue, regulating adipogenesis, glucose homeostasis, lipid metabolism, and insulin sensitivity. Target of thiazolidinedione drugs (pioglitazone).

source.title: PPARG (Pro12Ala) genetic variant and risk of T2DM: a systematic review and meta-analysis
source.finding: PPARG Pro12Ala (rs1801282) meta-analysis of 73 studies (62,250 cases / 69,613 controls) confirms the Ala (G) allele is protective against T2DM. The Pro (C) allele increases diabetes risk, particularly in interaction with high-fat diets.

CC.effect: Homozygous Pro12 carriers have full PPARG transcriptional activity. Higher risk of type 2 diabetes, particularly with high-fat diet. Greater insulin resistance in the context of obesity. PPARG-mediated adipogenesis and lipid storage are more active, but insulin sensitization may be impaired under metabolic stress.
CC.recommendation: Limit saturated fat intake (<7% calories). Mediterranean or low-glycemic diet preferred. Regular physical activity to improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor fasting glucose and HbA1c. If diabetic, pioglitazone may provide particular benefit as it targets PPARG directly.

CG.effect: Heterozygous Pro12Ala carriers show 30-50% reduced PPARG ligand-induced activity. Intermediate diabetes risk. The Ala allele provides partial protection against T2DM development. Dietary fat-insulin resistance interaction is moderated.
CG.recommendation: Balanced diet with moderate fat intake. Standard diabetes prevention strategies. Regular exercise and weight management.

GC.effect: Heterozygous Pro12Ala carriers show 30-50% reduced PPARG ligand-induced activity. Intermediate diabetes risk. The Ala allele provides partial protection against T2DM development. Dietary fat-insulin resistance interaction is moderated.
GC.recommendation: Balanced diet with moderate fat intake. Standard diabetes prevention strategies. Regular exercise and weight management.

GG.effect: Homozygous Ala12 carriers have most reduced PPARG activity. Protective against type 2 diabetes. However, may be associated with slightly increased obesity risk and hypercholesterolemia in some populations. Lower insulin resistance overall.
GG.recommendation: Favorable diabetes genetics from this locus. Standard healthy lifestyle. Monitor cholesterol levels as GG may associate with higher total cholesterol in some studies.

gene_description: Protein Phosphatase Mg2+/Mn2+ Dependent 1K — mitochondrial phosphatase that activates BCKDH (branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase), the rate-limiting enzyme in branched-chain amino acid (BCAA) catabolism. Variants affect circulating BCAA levels and insulin secretion.

source.title: Effect of the interaction between diet composition and the PPM1K genetic variant on insulin resistance and β cell function markers during weight loss
source.finding: PPM1K rs1440581 T allele is associated with elevated serum valine and total BCAAs. Significant gene-diet interactions: T allele carriers show higher insulin reduction on high-fat diet but smaller glucose improvement during weight-loss. CC genotype associated with cardiovascular disease risk.

CC.effect: Homozygous C carriers show elevated serum valine and total BCAA levels. PPM1K phosphatase activity may be altered, affecting BCKDH-mediated BCAA catabolism. Associated with increased cardiovascular disease risk. Decreased PPM1K expression impairs glucose-stimulated insulin secretion in pancreatic beta cells.
CC.recommendation: Monitor cardiovascular risk markers. Moderate protein intake with balanced BCAA sources. Consider lower BCAA supplementation. Regular fasting glucose and insulin monitoring. Low-fat dietary pattern may be preferable.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate BCAA levels and mixed metabolic response to dietary interventions.
CT.recommendation: Balanced macronutrient intake. Standard metabolic monitoring.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate BCAA levels and mixed metabolic response to dietary interventions.
TC.recommendation: Balanced macronutrient intake. Standard metabolic monitoring.

TT.effect: T allele carriers show better glucose improvement during weight-loss interventions. Higher insulin reduction on high-fat diets (gene-diet interaction). Normal BCAA catabolism.
TT.recommendation: Standard dietary guidelines. Responds well to weight-loss dietary interventions.

description: PTPN22 rs2476601 (R620W, C1858T) variant affecting lymphocyte phosphatase function. The T allele increases T-cell activation, predisposing to autoimmune conditions including type 1 diabetes (~2.5x risk in TT homozygotes). Also associated with rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, and thyroiditis.

source.title: A functional variant of lymphoid tyrosine phosphatase is associated with type I diabetes
source.authors: Bottini N, Musso T, Nieto M, et al.

AA.phenotype: High genetic susceptibility to type 1 diabetes and other autoimmune conditions
AA.effect: High genetic susceptibility to type 1 diabetes and other autoimmune conditions
AA.recommendation: Family screening recommended if T1D diagnosis. Genetic counseling appropriate. Monitor for signs of T1D (thirst, polyuria, weight loss). Annual screening with HbA1c and fasting glucose in at-risk relatives. Consider also screening for other autoimmune conditions (thyroiditis, RA, SLE).

AG.phenotype: Moderately increased genetic risk for type 1 diabetes and autoimmune conditions
AG.effect: Moderately increased genetic risk for type 1 diabetes and autoimmune conditions
AG.recommendation: Standard T1D screening for symptomatic relatives. Counsel on early warning signs. Annual monitoring of metabolic markers if family history of T1D.

GA.phenotype: Moderately increased genetic risk for type 1 diabetes and autoimmune conditions
GA.effect: Moderately increased genetic risk for type 1 diabetes and autoimmune conditions
GA.recommendation: Standard T1D screening for symptomatic relatives. Counsel on early warning signs. Annual monitoring of metabolic markers if family history of T1D.

GG.phenotype: No increased PTPN22-related T1D genetic risk
GG.effect: No increased PTPN22-related T1D genetic risk
GG.recommendation: Standard population screening recommendations apply

gene_description: Ryanodine Receptor 1 — calcium release channel in skeletal muscle sarcoplasmic reticulum. Pathogenic variants cause malignant hyperthermia susceptibility, a potentially fatal pharmacogenomic reaction to volatile anesthetics and succinylcholine.

source.title: Functional analysis of RYR1 variants linked to malignant hyperthermia
source.finding: RYR1 pathogenic variants cause dysregulated calcium release from sarcoplasmic reticulum upon exposure to volatile anesthetics or succinylcholine, triggering malignant hyperthermia with mortality rate of 70% if untreated. Over 400 variants identified, 35 confirmed causative.

TT.effect: Homozygous for a pathogenic RYR1 variant. High susceptibility to malignant hyperthermia (MH) when exposed to triggering agents (volatile anesthetics, succinylcholine). MH presents as rapid temperature rise, muscle rigidity, metabolic acidosis, hyperkalemia, and rhabdomyolysis. Untreated mortality rate is approximately 70%. Even with dantrolene treatment, mortality remains 1-5%.
TT.recommendation: DO NOT USE volatile anesthetics (sevoflurane, desflurane, isoflurane, halothane) or succinylcholine. Use total intravenous anesthesia (TIVA) with propofol, non-depolarizing muscle relaxants, and opioids. Ensure dantrolene is immediately available. Use a dedicated anesthesia machine flushed of volatile agents. Medical alert bracelet recommended. Screen all first-degree relatives.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier. MH susceptibility is autosomal dominant, so even one pathogenic allele confers significant risk. Exposure to triggering agents can cause full MH crisis. Penetrance is variable — not all carriers develop MH on every exposure, but the risk is unpredictable and potentially fatal.
TC.recommendation: DO NOT USE volatile anesthetics or succinylcholine. Same precautions as homozygous: use TIVA with propofol, ensure dantrolene availability, use clean anesthesia machine. All first-degree relatives should be tested. Consider caffeine-halothane contracture test for definitive diagnosis if genetic testing is inconclusive.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier. MH susceptibility is autosomal dominant, so even one pathogenic allele confers significant risk. Exposure to triggering agents can cause full MH crisis. Penetrance is variable — not all carriers develop MH on every exposure, but the risk is unpredictable and potentially fatal.
CT.recommendation: DO NOT USE volatile anesthetics or succinylcholine. Same precautions as homozygous: use TIVA with propofol, ensure dantrolene availability, use clean anesthesia machine. All first-degree relatives should be tested. Consider caffeine-halothane contracture test for definitive diagnosis if genetic testing is inconclusive.

CC.phenotype: No Increased MH Risk at This Locus
CC.effect: Wild-type at this specific RYR1 position. However, over 400 RYR1 variants have been identified — absence of this specific variant does not rule out MH susceptibility from other RYR1 variants. A negative result for one SNP requires comprehensive RYR1 sequencing for complete risk assessment.
CC.recommendation: Standard anesthetic protocols if no other MH risk factors present. If family history suggests MH, pursue comprehensive RYR1 genetic testing or caffeine-halothane contracture test.

gene_description: Sodium channel neuronal type 1 alpha subunit (NaV1.1) — encodes the pore-forming alpha subunit of neuronal voltage-gated sodium channels. The rs3812718 splice-site polymorphism affects exon 5 splicing to include either the neonatal (5N) or adult (5A) isoform, modifying channel sensitivity to carbamazepine and phenytoin.

source.title: A common SCN1A splice-site polymorphism modifies the effect of carbamazepine on cortical excitability--a pharmacogenetic transcranial magnetic stimulation study
source.finding: The rs3812718 A/G polymorphism modulates the ratio of NaV1.1-5A to NaV1.1-5N isoforms. The 5N variant is more sensitive to phenytoin. AA genotype carriers showed higher maintenance doses and serum levels of carbamazepine (3-12 months) compared to GG genotype. Meta-analysis shows mixed results with some population-dependent associations between rs3812718 and carbamazepine responsiveness.

snps.note: IVS5N+5 G>A splice-site polymorphism; c.603-91G>A in genomic coordinates

AA.effect: Homozygous A-allele carriers produce predominantly the adult 5A isoform of NaV1.1. The 5A isoform is less sensitive to carbamazepine and phenytoin blockade. Clinical studies show AA genotype carriers require higher maintenance doses and achieve higher steady-state serum concentrations of carbamazepine (particularly 3-12 months post-initiation) compared to GG carriers. Some studies report increased carbamazepine resistance or non-retention with AA genotype.
AA.recommendation: Standard initial dosing is appropriate, but monitor clinical response closely. If seizure control is inadequate at standard doses, dose escalation may be needed. Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) is recommended to guide dosing. Target serum carbamazepine concentration is 4-12 mcg/mL; phenytoin target is 10-20 mcg/mL. Monitor for dose-related adverse effects with high-dose therapy.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers produce both 5A and 5N isoforms of NaV1.1. Intermediate sensitivity to sodium channel blockers compared to GG and AA homozygotes. Clinical response typically falls between the two homozygous groups, with moderate sensitivity to standard carbamazepine and phenytoin doses.
GA.recommendation: Standard dosing per prescribing guidelines is generally appropriate. Monitor seizure control closely during initial treatment phase. If seizure control is not achieved at standard doses, consider therapeutic drug monitoring to guide dose adjustments. Phenytoin dosing is nonlinear; monitor levels carefully with dose changes.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers produce both 5A and 5N isoforms of NaV1.1. Intermediate sensitivity to sodium channel blockers compared to GG and AA homozygotes. Clinical response typically falls between the two homozygous groups, with moderate sensitivity to standard carbamazepine and phenytoin doses.
AG.recommendation: Standard dosing per prescribing guidelines is generally appropriate. Monitor seizure control closely during initial treatment phase. If seizure control is not achieved at standard doses, consider therapeutic drug monitoring to guide dose adjustments. Phenytoin dosing is nonlinear; monitor levels carefully with dose changes.

GG.effect: Homozygous G-allele carriers produce predominantly the neonatal 5N isoform of NaV1.1. The 5N isoform is more sensitive to carbamazepine and phenytoin blockade, resulting in greater channel inhibition at standard doses. GG genotype carriers typically achieve adequate seizure control at lower maintenance doses and lower serum concentrations compared to AA carriers. Some studies report better carbamazepine responsiveness with GG genotype.
GG.recommendation: Standard initial dosing may be appropriate, but increased risk of dose-related adverse effects. Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) is strongly recommended to optimize dosing. Target therapeutic levels may be achieved at lower doses than in AA carriers. Start with lower doses and titrate cautiously based on clinical response and TDM results. Monitor for carbamazepine adverse effects including dizziness, ataxia, diplopia, hyponatremia, and rash.

gene_description: Sodium Voltage-Gated Channel Alpha Subunit 1 (NaV1.1) — pore-forming alpha subunit of neuronal voltage-gated sodium channels essential for action potential initiation and propagation in inhibitory neurons. The rs11890028 intronic SNP is the lead disease-risk variant from the 2014 ILAE GWAS, with the highest association signal on chromosome 2q24.3 for common epilepsy. The SNP affects NaV1.1 expression levels, modulating neuronal excitability and seizure threshold across epilepsy subtypes.

source.title: Genetic determinants of common epilepsies: a meta-analysis of genome-wide association studies

snps.note: Lead SNP at 2q24.3 locus in intronic region of SCN1A; associated with disease susceptibility across genetic generalized and focal epilepsies

AA.phenotype: Increased epilepsy risk, potentially broad phenotype
AA.effect: Homozygous A-allele carriers show elevated epilepsy susceptibility (approximately 1.35-1.50x relative risk compared to GG carriers). The A allele is associated with altered SCN1A expression, likely reducing NaV1.1 channel density in inhibitory neurons and reducing GABAergic inhibition. Increased seizure susceptibility across multiple epilepsy subtypes including generalized and focal epilepsies. AA carriers show lower seizure threshold and variable antiepileptic drug (AED) responsiveness.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show mild-to-moderate epilepsy risk elevation (approximately 1.15-1.25x relative risk compared to GG carriers). Intermediate SCN1A expression and NaV1.1 channel density. Modest reduction in seizure threshold primarily affecting generalized seizure susceptibility. Most heterozygotes, if affected, show favorable AED responsiveness.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show mild-to-moderate epilepsy risk elevation (approximately 1.15-1.25x relative risk compared to GG carriers). Intermediate SCN1A expression and NaV1.1 channel density. Modest reduction in seizure threshold primarily affecting generalized seizure susceptibility. Most heterozygotes, if affected, show favorable AED responsiveness.

GG.effect: Homozygous G-allele carriers serve as reference group. Standard SCN1A expression and NaV1.1 channel density in inhibitory neurons. Normal seizure threshold and standard epilepsy prevalence without specific SCN1A-mediated risk elevation.

gene_description: Sodium Voltage-Gated Channel Alpha Subunit 9 (Nav1.7) — peripheral sodium channel essential for pain signal transduction. Gain- and loss-of-function variants cause dramatically altered pain perception, from complete insensitivity to extreme pain disorders.

source.title: Pain perception is altered by a nucleotide polymorphism in SCN9A
source.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
source.finding: SCN9A rs6746030 A allele (R1150W) significantly associated with increased pain scores. GA and AA genotypes linked to increased Nav1.7 activity and lowered pain thresholds. Minor allele frequency ~10%.

AA.phenotype: Increased Pain Sensitivity — Enhanced Nav1.7 Activity
AA.effect: Homozygous for the R1150W variant (tryptophan at position 1150). Significantly increased Nav1.7 sodium channel activity leading to heightened pain perception across multiple pain modalities. Lower pain thresholds and increased pain scores. Under propofol anesthesia, significantly lower BIS values recorded, indicating altered anesthetic sensitivity.
AA.recommendation: Plan for higher analgesic requirements. Consider multimodal pain management strategies. Propofol dosing may need adjustment based on BIS monitoring. Local anesthetics targeting sodium channels (lidocaine, bupivacaine) may be particularly effective. Nav1.7-specific blockers in development may offer targeted benefit.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate Nav1.7 activity with moderately increased pain perception compared to GG genotype. Mild alteration in anesthetic response.
AG.recommendation: Be aware of potentially increased analgesic needs. Monitor pain scores and adjust management accordingly. Standard starting doses with readiness to titrate.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate Nav1.7 activity with moderately increased pain perception compared to GG genotype. Mild alteration in anesthetic response.
GA.recommendation: Be aware of potentially increased analgesic needs. Monitor pain scores and adjust management accordingly. Standard starting doses with readiness to titrate.

GG.effect: Homozygous for the reference allele (arginine at position 1150). Normal Nav1.7 sodium channel function and standard pain perception. Typical response to analgesics and anesthetics.
GG.recommendation: Standard pain management protocols. No pharmacogenomic adjustment needed.

description: SERPINA1 rs28929474 (E342K, PiZ variant) causing severe alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency. The Z allele produces a misfolded protein with <15% activity compared to normal, leading to progressive lung damage and ~8-10 fold increased COPD risk.

source.title: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency as a cause of COPD: an overview
source.authors: Stoller JK, Aboussouan LS
source.journal: American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine

TT.phenotype: Severe AAT deficiency with high risk of early panlobular emphysema and COPD
TT.effect: Severe AAT deficiency with high risk of early panlobular emphysema and COPD
TT.recommendation: Baseline and annual pulmonary function testing. Strongly avoid smoking; smoking accelerates disease by 10-15 years. Consider AAT augmentation therapy (IV infusions). Optimize COPD management with bronchodilators, corticosteroids. Genetic counseling for family members.

TC.phenotype: Intermediate AAT deficiency; modestly increased COPD risk, especially with smoking
TC.effect: Intermediate AAT deficiency; modestly increased COPD risk, especially with smoking
TC.recommendation: Smoking cessation strongly recommended. Avoid environmental occupational exposures. Monitor lung function. AAT augmentation generally not indicated unless concurrent lung disease develops.

CT.phenotype: Intermediate AAT deficiency; modestly increased COPD risk, especially with smoking
CT.effect: Intermediate AAT deficiency; modestly increased COPD risk, especially with smoking
CT.recommendation: Smoking cessation strongly recommended. Avoid environmental occupational exposures. Monitor lung function. AAT augmentation generally not indicated unless concurrent lung disease develops.

CC.phenotype: Normal AAT levels; no special COPD risk from AAT deficiency
CC.effect: Normal AAT levels; no special COPD risk from AAT deficiency
CC.recommendation: Standard COPD risk assessment based on other factors; smoking status remains key modifiable risk factor

gene_description: Sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) — binds testosterone and estradiol, regulating hormone bioavailability; the Pro185Leu variant reduces SHBG binding affinity and increases free hormone levels

source.title: Genetics of sex hormone-binding globulin and testosterone levels in fertile and infertile men
source.finding: rs6258 C>T (Pro185Leu) polymorphism reduces SHBG binding affinity; T allele associated with 22% higher free testosterone in heterozygotes despite similar total testosterone levels

TT.effect: Homozygous T carriers (Leu185Leu) have significantly reduced SHBG binding affinity. Substantially higher free testosterone and free estradiol despite normal total hormone levels. May result in elevated androgenic and estrogenic effects. Associated with altered androgen-mediated phenotypes and metabolic effects.
TT.recommendation: Monitor free testosterone/estradiol and clinical hormone effect symptoms. If on hormone replacement therapy, lower doses may be appropriate. Regular monitoring of testosterone-dependent pathways (prostate health, cardiovascular markers). Women may experience more pronounced estrogenic effects; consider monitoring bone health and cardiovascular risk.

CT.effect: Heterozygotes show reduced SHBG binding capacity compared to CC wild-type. Approximately 22% higher free testosterone levels while maintaining normal total testosterone. Intermediate increase in free hormone bioavailability.
CT.recommendation: Standard hormone replacement dosing generally appropriate. Monitor symptoms of androgenic/estrogenic excess (acne, hirsutism in women; aggression, gynecomastia risk in men). Periodic free testosterone measurement if dose adjustments needed.

TC.effect: Heterozygotes show reduced SHBG binding capacity compared to CC wild-type. Approximately 22% higher free testosterone levels while maintaining normal total testosterone. Intermediate increase in free hormone bioavailability.
TC.recommendation: Standard hormone replacement dosing generally appropriate. Monitor symptoms of androgenic/estrogenic excess (acne, hirsutism in women; aggression, gynecomastia risk in men). Periodic free testosterone measurement if dose adjustments needed.

CC.effect: Wild-type homozygotes (Pro185Pro) have normal SHBG binding affinity and hormone regulation. Normal free testosterone and estradiol bioavailability.
CC.recommendation: Standard hormone replacement therapy dosing per clinical guidelines. Monitor total and free testosterone/estradiol at appropriate intervals per standard endocrinology practice.

gene_description: Sirtuin 1 — NAD+-dependent histone deacetylase that acts as a master metabolic regulator. Controls transcription factors involved in glucose/lipid homeostasis, inflammation, stress response, and energy expenditure. Activated by caloric restriction and resveratrol.

source.title: The molecular genetics of sirtuins: association with human longevity and age-related diseases
source.finding: SIRT1 rs1467568 minor allele associated with lower BMI (0.2-0.4 kg/m² decrease per allele) and 13-18% decreased obesity risk (p=0.0009). Effect sustained over 6.4 year follow-up. In diabetic subgroups, SIRT1 variation relates to survival rate.

AA.effect: Homozygous major allele carriers have higher baseline BMI and increased obesity risk. Standard SIRT1 activity. May benefit more from caloric restriction and NAD+ precursor supplementation. In diabetic individuals, this genotype may relate to poorer metabolic outcomes.
AA.recommendation: Caloric restriction strategies and intermittent fasting may be particularly beneficial. Consider resveratrol-rich foods (grapes, berries, red wine in moderation). NAD+ precursor supplements (NMN, nicotinamide riboside) may support SIRT1 activity. Regular exercise (activates SIRT1).

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate BMI tendency. Moderate SIRT1 activity and metabolic regulation.
AG.recommendation: Standard healthy lifestyle with emphasis on regular physical activity and balanced caloric intake.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate BMI tendency. Moderate SIRT1 activity and metabolic regulation.
GA.recommendation: Standard healthy lifestyle with emphasis on regular physical activity and balanced caloric intake.

GG.effect: Homozygous minor allele carriers show lower BMI (0.4-0.8 kg/m² lower) and 13-18% reduced obesity risk. Potentially enhanced SIRT1 metabolic regulation. Favorable metabolic trajectory over time.
GG.recommendation: Favorable metabolic genetics from this locus. Maintain healthy lifestyle. Standard dietary guidelines.

source.title: Glutamate transporter gene SLC1A1 associated with obsessive-compulsive disorder

description: SLC1A1 rs301434 is associated with obsessive-compulsive disorder susceptibility, particularly in males and those with hoarding symptoms. The gene encodes the neuronal glutamate transporter EAAT3.

AA.effect: Reference homozygote
AA.recommendation: Standard mental health monitoring; baseline assessment only

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier; modest increased OCD risk, particularly in males
AG.recommendation: Psychiatric screening; monitor for obsessive or compulsive symptoms; early intervention if symptoms develop; family history assessment

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier; modest increased OCD risk, particularly in males
GA.recommendation: Psychiatric screening; monitor for obsessive or compulsive symptoms; early intervention if symptoms develop; family history assessment

GG.effect: Homozygous for risk allele; substantially increased glutamatergic dysfunction risk
GG.recommendation: Comprehensive psychiatric evaluation; OCD screening with Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale; early cognitive-behavioral therapy if symptoms present; medication options (SSRIs); augmentation with glutamatergic agents may be considered

gene_description: Solute Carrier Family 22 Member 1 — encodes Organic Cation Transporter 1 (OCT1), a hepatic influx transporter critical for metformin uptake into hepatocytes where it exerts its hypoglycemic effects

source.title: The influence of metformin transporter gene SLC22A1 and SLC47A1 variants on steady-state pharmacokinetics and glycemic response
source.finding: SLC22A1 rs628031 polymorphism affects metformin pharmacokinetics and glycemic response. The Met408Val variant (rs628031 A>G) reduces OCT1 mRNA expression in hepatocytes, resulting in decreased metformin hepatic uptake and accumulation in systemic circulation.

AA.effect: Wild-type genotype with full OCT1 expression and metformin transport capacity. Normal hepatic uptake of metformin allowing optimal glycemic response.
AA.recommendation: Standard metformin dosing per therapeutic guidelines. Expect robust hypoglycemic response.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate OCT1 expression and reduced metformin transport compared to wild-type. Moderately increased plasma metformin levels due to decreased hepatic uptake.
AG.recommendation: Standard metformin dosing, though some patients may require dose adjustment if GI side effects develop. Monitor glycemic response and HbA1c reduction.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate OCT1 expression and reduced metformin transport compared to wild-type. Moderately increased plasma metformin levels due to decreased hepatic uptake.
GA.recommendation: Standard metformin dosing, though some patients may require dose adjustment if GI side effects develop. Monitor glycemic response and HbA1c reduction.

GG.effect: Homozygous carriers have significantly reduced OCT1 mRNA expression and metformin transport capacity. Markedly elevated plasma metformin levels with decreased renal clearance. Increased risk of metformin-associated lactic acidosis (MALA) especially with renal impairment.
GG.recommendation: Caution with metformin use. Consider dose reduction or alternative antidiabetic agents. Monitor renal function closely. Avoid in patients with eGFR <45 mL/min/1.73m2. Regular monitoring of serum lactate and pH recommended.

gene_description: Solute Carrier Family 22 Member 2 — encodes Organic Cation Transporter 2 (OCT2), the primary renal secretory transporter responsible for metformin elimination from circulation

source.title: Interaction of rs316019 variants of SLC22A2 with metformin and other drugs: an in silico analysis
source.finding: The SLC22A2 rs316019 (270A>S) variant reduces OCT2 renal clearance of metformin, increasing systemic accumulation and risk of metformin-associated lactic acidosis (MALA) — a potentially life-threatening condition with 30-50% mortality rate.

AA.effect: Wild-type 270A form with normal OCT2 function and optimal renal metformin clearance. Rapid elimination from circulation with reduced accumulation risk.
AA.recommendation: Standard metformin dosing. Good renal elimination expected. Monitor renal function periodically, especially in elderly or those with declining eGFR.

AT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate renal OCT2 activity. Modest reduction in metformin renal clearance (approximately 31-44% lower) with some accumulation in plasma.
AT.recommendation: Standard metformin dosing typically appropriate, though careful renal function monitoring recommended. Be especially vigilant if eGFR declines. Consider dose reduction if baseline renal function marginal.

TA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate renal OCT2 activity. Modest reduction in metformin renal clearance (approximately 31-44% lower) with some accumulation in plasma.
TA.recommendation: Standard metformin dosing typically appropriate, though careful renal function monitoring recommended. Be especially vigilant if eGFR declines. Consider dose reduction if baseline renal function marginal.

TT.effect: Homozygous 270S form has severely reduced OCT2 function for metformin renal secretion. Marked accumulation in plasma (46-74% higher AUC) with significantly delayed elimination (CLrenal reduced 31-44%). High risk for metformin-associated lactic acidosis, especially if combined with renal impairment or acute illness.
TT.recommendation: Caution with metformin use. Careful dose adjustment or alternative agent consideration strongly advised. Mandatory regular renal function monitoring (eGFR, creatinine). Avoid if eGFR <45 mL/min/1.73m2. Periodic lactate and pH monitoring recommended. Absolute contraindication if eGFR <30 or severe renal disease.

gene_description: Solute Carrier Family 23 Member 1 — encodes SVCT1 (sodium-dependent vitamin C transporter 1), the primary active transporter for L-ascorbic acid absorption in intestine and renal reabsorption in kidneys.

source.title: Genetic variation at the SLC23A1 locus is associated with circulating concentrations of L-ascorbic acid (vitamin C): evidence from 5 independent studies with >15,000 participants
source.finding: SLC23A1 rs33972313 minor A allele is associated with -5.98 μmol/L reduction in plasma vitamin C per allele. Heterozygotes show 24% lower plasma vitamin C. The missense variant (Val→Met) impairs transporter function, reducing intestinal absorption and renal reabsorption of ascorbic acid.

AA.effect: Homozygous missense carriers (Met/Met) have markedly impaired SVCT1 transporter function. Plasma vitamin C levels reduced by 40-50% across dietary dose range. Very high risk of clinical vitamin C deficiency even with adequate dietary intake. Impaired intestinal absorption and renal reabsorption of ascorbate.
AA.recommendation: High-dose vitamin C supplementation recommended (500-1000mg daily in divided doses). Consume vitamin C-rich foods throughout the day (citrus, peppers, kiwi, broccoli). Monitor plasma vitamin C levels. Consider IV vitamin C if oral supplementation insufficient.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers (Val/Met) show ~24% lower plasma vitamin C concentrations (-5.98 μmol/L per allele). Partially impaired intestinal absorption and renal reabsorption. May not achieve optimal vitamin C status on standard dietary intake alone.
GA.recommendation: Increase vitamin C-rich foods in diet. Consider moderate supplementation (250-500mg daily). Ensure consistent daily vitamin C intake as body stores may be depleted faster. Avoid long periods without fresh fruits and vegetables.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers (Val/Met) show ~24% lower plasma vitamin C concentrations (-5.98 μmol/L per allele). Partially impaired intestinal absorption and renal reabsorption. May not achieve optimal vitamin C status on standard dietary intake alone.
AG.recommendation: Increase vitamin C-rich foods in diet. Consider moderate supplementation (250-500mg daily). Ensure consistent daily vitamin C intake as body stores may be depleted faster. Avoid long periods without fresh fruits and vegetables.

GG.effect: Wild-type SVCT1 transporter with full activity. Efficient intestinal absorption and renal reabsorption of ascorbic acid. Normal plasma vitamin C levels with adequate dietary intake.
GG.recommendation: Standard dietary intake of vitamin C (90mg/day men, 75mg/day women) from fruits and vegetables is sufficient.

gene_description: Solute carrier family 24 member 5 (SLC24A5) — cation exchanger protein controlling melanin synthesis and skin pigmentation

source.title: SLC24A5, a putative cation exchanger, affects pigmentation in zebrafish and humans
source.finding: rs1426654 A111T variant explains ~27% of skin pigmentation variation in South Asians. Ancestral allele (A) predominates in African and East Asian populations; derived allele (G/threonine) nearly fixed in Europeans and associated with lighter skin.

GG.effect: Homozygous for threonine at position 111. Strong light skin pigmentation phenotype. Nearly 100% frequency in European populations. Associated with reduced melanin production and increased risk of sunburn and UV damage.
GG.recommendation: High risk for sun damage and skin cancer. Daily broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen recommended. Avoid peak sun hours (10am-4pm). Regular skin cancer screening advised.

AG.effect: Heterozygous for mixed alanine/threonine alleles. Intermediate skin pigmentation—lighter than ancestral phenotype but darker than homozygous light-skin carriers. Common in admixed populations.
AG.recommendation: Moderate sun protection recommended. Use SPF 30+ sunscreen and protective clothing in sun exposure. Periodic skin checks appropriate.

GA.effect: Heterozygous for mixed alanine/threonine alleles. Intermediate skin pigmentation—lighter than ancestral phenotype but darker than homozygous light-skin carriers. Common in admixed populations.
GA.recommendation: Moderate sun protection recommended. Use SPF 30+ sunscreen and protective clothing in sun exposure. Periodic skin checks appropriate.

AA.effect: Homozygous for ancestral alanine at position 111. Strong melanin synthesis and dark skin pigmentation. Predominant in Sub-Saharan African, East Asian, and Indigenous American populations. Increased photoprotection against UV damage.
AA.recommendation: Standard sun protection sufficient (SPF 15+). Lower risk for skin cancer but still at risk during intense sun exposure. Vitamin D synthesis may be reduced in high-latitude regions with low sun exposure.

gene_description: Solute Carrier Family 2 Member 2 (GLUT2) — facilitative glucose transporter and glucose sensor in liver, pancreatic beta cells, intestine, kidney, and brain. Controls glucose sensing, insulin secretion, and sugar intake behavior.

source.title: Genetic variant in the glucose transporter type 2 is associated with higher intakes of sugars in two distinct populations
source.finding: SLC2A2 rs5400 (Thr110Ile) T allele carriers consume significantly more sugar per day (131g vs 115g). The variant affects GLUT2 glucose sensing, fasting glucose, HbA1c, and conversion from impaired glucose tolerance to type 2 diabetes.

TT.phenotype: Impaired glucose sensing / high sugar intake
TT.effect: Homozygous Ile110 carriers have altered GLUT2 glucose sensing function. Significantly higher habitual sugar consumption (~14% more). Elevated fasting glucose and HbA1c. Increased risk of conversion from impaired glucose tolerance to type 2 diabetes. Altered glycemic response to metformin.
TT.recommendation: Active sugar intake reduction — awareness that genetic predisposition drives higher sugar consumption. Use portion control and sugar alternatives. Regular fasting glucose and HbA1c monitoring. Avoid sugar-sweetened beverages. High-fiber diet to slow glucose absorption.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate sugar intake tendency and mildly elevated fasting glucose. Moderate GLUT2 function alteration.
CT.recommendation: Monitor sugar intake. Regular metabolic screening. Balanced diet with emphasis on complex carbohydrates over simple sugars.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate sugar intake tendency and mildly elevated fasting glucose. Moderate GLUT2 function alteration.
TC.recommendation: Monitor sugar intake. Regular metabolic screening. Balanced diet with emphasis on complex carbohydrates over simple sugars.

CC.effect: Wild-type with full GLUT2 glucose sensing and transport function. Normal sugar intake patterns and fasting glucose from this locus.
CC.recommendation: Standard dietary guidelines.

gene_description: Solute Carrier Family 6 Member 2 (NET) — encodes the norepinephrine transporter, the primary reuptake mechanism for norepinephrine and direct pharmacological target of atomoxetine, venlafaxine, and duloxetine.

source.title: A haplotype of the norepinephrine transporter (Net) gene Slc6a2 is associated with clinical response to atomoxetine in attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD)
source.finding: NET/SLC6A2 genomic region across exons 4-9 was significantly associated with atomoxetine treatment response in ADHD. Promoter variant rs28386840 T-allele carriers showed greater methylphenidate response in subsequent meta-analysis.

AA.effect: Homozygous for the A allele at the -3081 promoter position of NET. This variant affects SLC6A2 transcriptional activity and norepinephrine transporter expression. Associated with reduced response to both methylphenidate and atomoxetine in ADHD. Pooled data show T-allele carriers are significantly more likely to respond to stimulant treatment.
AA.recommendation: If atomoxetine or methylphenidate response is suboptimal, consider dose escalation or switch to alternative ADHD medications. Alpha-2 agonists (guanfacine, clonidine) may be considered as adjunct or alternative therapy.

AT.effect: Heterozygous carrier with one T allele. Intermediate NET promoter activity and norepinephrine transporter expression. Generally adequate response to atomoxetine and methylphenidate expected, though may not be as robust as TT homozygotes.
AT.recommendation: Standard dosing for atomoxetine or stimulants. Monitor clinical response and titrate based on symptom improvement.

TA.effect: Heterozygous carrier with one T allele. Intermediate NET promoter activity and norepinephrine transporter expression. Generally adequate response to atomoxetine and methylphenidate expected, though may not be as robust as TT homozygotes.
TA.recommendation: Standard dosing for atomoxetine or stimulants. Monitor clinical response and titrate based on symptom improvement.

TT.effect: Homozygous for the T allele. Associated with optimal norepinephrine transporter expression and best response to both atomoxetine and methylphenidate in ADHD. T-allele carriers showed significantly greater improvement in hyperactive-impulsive symptoms.
TT.recommendation: Standard dosing expected to be effective. Good clinical response anticipated with atomoxetine or stimulant medications.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the C allele at the -182 promoter position. This variant has potential to influence NET gene transcriptional activity, though direct association with methylphenidate response was not significant in meta-analysis. May contribute to ADHD susceptibility through altered noradrenergic tone.
CC.recommendation: Standard medication dosing. This variant alone does not strongly predict treatment response, but consider in context of overall pharmacogenomic profile.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate effect on NET promoter activity. No significant standalone association with treatment response reported.
CT.recommendation: Standard dosing per clinical guidelines.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate effect on NET promoter activity. No significant standalone association with treatment response reported.
TC.recommendation: Standard dosing per clinical guidelines.

TT.effect: Wild-type alleles at the -182 position. Standard norepinephrine transporter expression and typical ADHD medication response.
TT.recommendation: Standard dosing per clinical guidelines.

gene_description: Solute Carrier Family 6 Member 3 (DAT1) — encodes the dopamine transporter, the primary mechanism for dopamine reuptake from the synapse and main target of stimulant medications used in ADHD.

source.title: Response to methylphenidate in adults with ADHD is associated with a polymorphism in SLC6A3 (DAT1)
source.finding: The rs27072 T allele in DAT1 3'-UTR was associated with altered dopamine transporter expression and reduced methylphenidate response in ADHD adults (p=0.009), with a 2.1-fold effect on mRNA expression levels.

TT.effect: Homozygous for the T allele, associated with significantly altered dopamine transporter expression. Individuals may have reduced clinical response to methylphenidate due to changes in dopamine reuptake dynamics. Studies show the T allele affects DAT mRNA expression and translation efficiency, potentially leading to suboptimal stimulant efficacy.
TT.recommendation: Consider dose optimization or alternative stimulant formulations. If methylphenidate response is inadequate, evaluate amphetamine-based alternatives or non-stimulant options such as atomoxetine or guanfacine.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier of the T allele with one copy of the reference C allele. May show partially reduced methylphenidate efficacy compared to CC homozygotes. The effect on dopamine transporter expression is intermediate.
TC.recommendation: Standard stimulant dosing is typically appropriate. Monitor clinical response closely during titration and adjust dose based on symptom improvement and tolerability.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier of the T allele with one copy of the reference C allele. May show partially reduced methylphenidate efficacy compared to CC homozygotes. The effect on dopamine transporter expression is intermediate.
CT.recommendation: Standard stimulant dosing is typically appropriate. Monitor clinical response closely during titration and adjust dose based on symptom improvement and tolerability.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the reference C allele. Normal dopamine transporter expression and standard response to methylphenidate and other stimulant medications expected.
CC.recommendation: Standard stimulant dosing per clinical guidelines. No pharmacogenomic-based dose adjustment needed.

gene_description: Solute Carrier Family 6 Member 4 (5-HTT) — encodes the serotonin transporter, the primary reuptake mechanism for serotonin and direct pharmacological target of all SSRIs. 5-HTTLPR and rs25531 modulate transporter expression.

source.title: Serotonin Transporter Genetic Variation and Antidepressant Response and Tolerability: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
source.finding: Meta-analysis of 5-HTTLPR: L-allele carriers (LL/LS) were significantly more likely to respond to SSRI therapy compared to SS carriers in European populations. SS genotype associated with 50% less serotonin transporter expression.

GG.phenotype: Reduced SSRI Response — Low Serotonin Transporter Expression
GG.effect: Homozygous for the G allele (functionally equivalent to 5-HTTLPR LG, comparable to the S allele). Reduced SLC6A4 transcriptional activity resulting in approximately 50% less serotonin transporter expression. Associated with poorer SSRI response, longer time to remission, and higher rates of treatment-resistant depression. The low-expression genotype reduces the target density for SSRIs.
GG.recommendation: Consider starting with an SSRI but allow adequate trial duration (8-12 weeks). If response is inadequate, consider switching to an SNRI (venlafaxine, duloxetine), bupropion, or augmentation strategies earlier than usual. Cognitive behavioral therapy may provide additional benefit. Monitor closely for treatment-emergent anxiety in the first 2 weeks.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate serotonin transporter expression. The combination with the 5-HTTLPR long/short status further modulates the functional impact. Generally adequate SSRI response expected, though may not be optimal.
GA.recommendation: Standard SSRI dosing is appropriate. Monitor treatment response during the first 4-6 weeks. If response is partial, dose optimization or augmentation may be needed.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate serotonin transporter expression. The combination with the 5-HTTLPR long/short status further modulates the functional impact. Generally adequate SSRI response expected, though may not be optimal.
AG.recommendation: Standard SSRI dosing is appropriate. Monitor treatment response during the first 4-6 weeks. If response is partial, dose optimization or augmentation may be needed.

AA.phenotype: Favorable SSRI Response — High Serotonin Transporter Expression
AA.effect: Homozygous for the A allele (functionally equivalent to 5-HTTLPR LA). High SLC6A4 transcriptional activity with robust serotonin transporter expression. Associated with better SSRI treatment response, higher remission rates, and better tolerability in multiple meta-analyses.
AA.recommendation: SSRIs are likely to be effective first-line treatment. Standard dosing per clinical guidelines. Good likelihood of treatment response.

gene_description: Statin myopathy risk (simvastatin)

source.title: CPIC Guideline for SLCO1B1 and Simvastatin-Induced Myopathy
source.finding: SLCO1B1 521T>C: CC genotype has 17x increased simvastatin myopathy risk. Use alternative statin.

CC.effect: 17x increased risk of simvastatin myopathy. Severely impaired hepatic statin uptake.
CC.recommendation: Avoid simvastatin. Use pravastatin or rosuvastatin at lowest effective dose.

CT.effect: 4.5x increased risk of simvastatin myopathy.
CT.recommendation: Avoid simvastatin >20mg. Consider alternative statin.

TT.effect: Normal hepatic statin uptake. Standard myopathy risk.
TT.recommendation: Standard statin dosing.

gene_description: Synaptosomal-Associated Protein 25 — essential component of the SNARE complex mediating neurotransmitter vesicle fusion and release. Modulates dopaminergic and noradrenergic transmission relevant to ADHD and stimulant response.

source.title: Pharmacogenetics of methylphenidate in childhood attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder: long-term effects
source.finding: SNAP25 rs3746544 G allele carriers showed poorer methylphenidate response — only 33.3% of G/G genotype responded well vs 74.7% of T/T, with 2-3x higher risk of irritability and sleep problems during treatment.

GG.effect: Homozygous for the G allele (MnlI polymorphism 1065T>G). Only 33.3% of individuals with G/G genotype showed good clinical response to methylphenidate. G allele carriers were 2-3 times more likely to experience irritability and sleep problems during treatment. The variant affects SNAP25 expression and synaptic vesicle dynamics, impairing neurotransmitter release efficiency.
GG.recommendation: If methylphenidate is initiated, monitor closely for treatment-resistant symptoms and side effects (especially irritability and sleep disturbance). Consider amphetamine-based alternatives, atomoxetine, or guanfacine if response is inadequate. Behavioral interventions may have greater relative benefit.

GT.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Approximately 72.5% of T/G carriers showed good response to methylphenidate, significantly better than G/G but slightly less than T/T genotype. Moderate increase in risk of treatment-related irritability.
GT.recommendation: Standard methylphenidate dosing is reasonable. Monitor for irritability and sleep problems. Adjust formulation timing if sleep disturbance occurs.

TG.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Approximately 72.5% of T/G carriers showed good response to methylphenidate, significantly better than G/G but slightly less than T/T genotype. Moderate increase in risk of treatment-related irritability.
TG.recommendation: Standard methylphenidate dosing is reasonable. Monitor for irritability and sleep problems. Adjust formulation timing if sleep disturbance occurs.

TT.effect: Homozygous for the reference T allele. 74.7% of individuals with this genotype showed good clinical response to methylphenidate. Normal SNAP25 expression and synaptic vesicle function. Lower risk of treatment-emergent irritability and sleep problems.
TT.recommendation: Standard methylphenidate dosing per ADHD guidelines. Good likelihood of treatment response.

gene_description: Sortilin 1 — sorting receptor involved in hepatic lipoprotein metabolism and VLDL secretion. The 1p13.3 locus (CELSR2-PSRC1-SORT1) is one of the strongest GWAS signals for LDL cholesterol and coronary artery disease risk.

source.title: From noncoding variant to phenotype via SORT1 at the 1p13 cholesterol locus
source.finding: Variants at the 1p13.3 locus alter hepatic SORT1 expression, directly affecting VLDL secretion and LDL-C levels. The minor allele of rs629301 is associated with higher SORT1 expression, lower VLDL secretion, and reduced LDL-C and CAD risk.

GG.effect: Homozygous major allele carriers have lower hepatic SORT1 expression, leading to increased VLDL secretion and elevated LDL-C levels. Associated with increased risk of coronary artery disease, atherosclerosis, and arterial calcification. Equivalent to ~10-year additional cardiovascular risk in critical stenosis assessment.
GG.recommendation: Cardiovascular risk management: lipid-lowering diet (reduce saturated fat, increase soluble fiber, plant sterols). Consider statin therapy per clinical guidelines. Regular lipid panel monitoring. LDL-C target individualized by overall risk profile.

GT.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate SORT1 expression and LDL-C levels. Modest cardiovascular risk modification from this locus.
GT.recommendation: Heart-healthy diet. Monitor lipid levels annually. Standard cardiovascular risk assessment.

TG.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate SORT1 expression and LDL-C levels. Modest cardiovascular risk modification from this locus.
TG.recommendation: Heart-healthy diet. Monitor lipid levels annually. Standard cardiovascular risk assessment.

TT.effect: Homozygous minor allele carriers have higher hepatic SORT1 expression, resulting in reduced VLDL secretion, lower LDL-C, and reduced coronary artery disease risk. Naturally favorable lipid profile from this locus.
TT.recommendation: Standard dietary guidelines. Favorable genetic lipid profile but other risk factors still apply. Maintain healthy lifestyle.

gene_description: Transcription Factor 7 Like 2 — a transcription factor involved in incretin signaling and glucose homeostasis. The rs7903146 variant is the strongest genetic risk factor for type 2 diabetes identified to date.

source.title: TCF7L2 Genetic Variation Augments Incretin Resistance and Influences Response to a Sulfonylurea and Metformin: The Study to Understand the Genetics of the Acute Response to Metformin and Glipizide in Humans (SUGAR-MGH)
source.finding: TCF7L2 rs7903146 C>T variant affects metformin and sulfonylurea response through incretin signaling. T allele carriers show improved glycemic response to metformin with 6.7-16.8% lower fasting glucose and 25.9% lower fasting insulin after treatment.

CC.phenotype: Higher type 2 diabetes risk, variable drug response
CC.effect: Wild-type genotype associated with increased type 2 diabetes risk. Suboptimal metformin response with modest glycemic improvement. Moderate sulfonylurea response expected.
CC.recommendation: Standard metformin and sulfonylurea dosing. Monitor HbA1c response closely. May require combination therapy or early addition of second agent if inadequate control.

CT.phenotype: Intermediate diabetes risk, enhanced drug response
CT.effect: Heterozygous T allele carriers show intermediate type 2 diabetes genetic risk. Improved glycemic response to both metformin and sulfonylureas. Better fasting glucose and insulin control compared to CC carriers. Approximately 6.7% lower fasting glucose after 6 months metformin therapy.
CT.recommendation: Standard dosing appropriate. Expect robust glycemic response to metformin and sulfonylureas. Good candidates for monotherapy trials before combination therapy needed.

TC.phenotype: Intermediate diabetes risk, enhanced drug response
TC.effect: Heterozygous T allele carriers show intermediate type 2 diabetes genetic risk. Improved glycemic response to both metformin and sulfonylureas. Better fasting glucose and insulin control compared to CC carriers. Approximately 6.7% lower fasting glucose after 6 months metformin therapy.
TC.recommendation: Standard dosing appropriate. Expect robust glycemic response to metformin and sulfonylureas. Good candidates for monotherapy trials before combination therapy needed.

TT.phenotype: Reduced diabetes risk, optimal drug response
TT.effect: Homozygous T allele carriers (about 25-30% of populations) show the strongest protection against type 2 diabetes and most dramatic drug response improvements. Approximately 16.8% lower fasting glucose and 25.9% lower fasting insulin after metformin treatment. Enhanced incretin signaling contributes to superior glycemic response.
TT.recommendation: Excellent candidates for metformin monotherapy with expected robust HbA1c reduction. Strong sulfonylurea responders. Prioritize lifestyle modifications as metabolic capacity is preserved. Earlier remission of diabetes possible.

gene_description: Trichohyalin (TCHH) — structural protein in hair follicles involved in cross-linking of keratin filaments and determining hair fiber morphology

source.title: Common variants in the trichohyalin gene are associated with straight hair in Europeans
source.finding: rs11803731 T allele (leucine-to-methionine at position 790) accounts for ~6% of hair curliness variance. T allele carriers show 70% likelihood of straight hair vs 50% in non-carriers.

TT.effect: Homozygous for methionine at position 790. Strong association with straight hair phenotype. Approximately 70% of T allele carriers have straight hair. T allele affects cross-linking of keratin filaments in hair shaft, leading to reduced curliness.
TT.recommendation: No clinical action needed. Hair care recommendations tailored to straight hair products (volumizing shampoos if fine, smoothing products for frizz control).

AT.effect: Heterozygous for mixed methionine/leucine alleles. Intermediate hair texture phenotype—more likely straight but some wavy hair observed. Intermediate effects on keratin cross-linking.
AT.recommendation: No clinical action needed. Hair care routine depends on individual phenotype expression.

TA.effect: Heterozygous for mixed methionine/leucine alleles. Intermediate hair texture phenotype—more likely straight but some wavy hair observed. Intermediate effects on keratin cross-linking.
TA.recommendation: No clinical action needed. Hair care routine depends on individual phenotype expression.

AA.effect: Homozygous for ancestral leucine at position 790. Associated with curly or wavy hair texture. Represents the ancestral trait. Leucine maintains normal cross-linking of keratin filaments, permitting natural hair curl.
AA.recommendation: No clinical action needed. Hair care recommendations for curly hair (moisturizing products, curl-enhancing treatments).

gene_description: Transcobalamin II (TCN2) — encodes transcobalamin, the protein that binds 20-25% of circulating cobalamin (vitamin B12) and mediates cellular uptake and intracellular availability

source.title: Association of TCN2 rs1801198 c.776G>C polymorphism with markers of one-carbon metabolism and related diseases: a systematic review and meta-analysis
source.finding: rs1801198 G allele associated with 13-15% lower plasma holotranscobalamin (active B12) concentrations; Pro259Arg missense mutation reduces B12 intracellular delivery

GG.effect: Homozygous G carriers (Pro259Pro) have significantly lower plasma holotranscobalamin levels (13-15% reduction). Reduced cellular uptake and intracellular B12 availability despite adequate serum cobalamin. Associated with elevated homocysteine and methylmalonic acid in some populations. Risk of functional B12 deficiency even with normal serum B12 levels.
GG.recommendation: Monitor serum B12, holotranscobalamin, homocysteine, and methylmalonic acid. Maintain B12 intake at upper end of RDA (2.4 mcg daily). Consider B12 supplementation (1000 mcg weekly or 2000 mcg daily) or B12 injections to ensure adequate intracellular B12.
