Test holotranscobalamin if symptoms of B12 deficiency present (neuropathy, cognitive changes, anemia).

CG.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate holotranscobalamin levels. Modest reduction in B12 intracellular delivery compared to CC wild-type, but generally adequate with normal dietary intake.
CG.recommendation: Standard B12 RDA adequate (2.4 mcg daily for adults). Monitor for B12 deficiency symptoms if at risk (vegans, elderly, gastrointestinal disorders). Consider supplementation if dietary B12 intake limited or symptoms suggestive of deficiency develop.

GC.effect: Heterozygotes show intermediate holotranscobalamin levels. Modest reduction in B12 intracellular delivery compared to CC wild-type, but generally adequate with normal dietary intake.
GC.recommendation: Standard B12 RDA adequate (2.4 mcg daily for adults). Monitor for B12 deficiency symptoms if at risk (vegans, elderly, gastrointestinal disorders). Consider supplementation if dietary B12 intake limited or symptoms suggestive of deficiency develop.

CC.effect: Wild-type homozygotes (Arg259Arg) have normal holotranscobalamin levels and cellular B12 uptake. Adequate intracellular B12 availability with standard dietary intake.
CC.recommendation: Standard B12 intake adequate (2.4 mcg daily from animal products, fortified foods, or supplements). Dietary sources include meat, fish, poultry, eggs, and dairy products.

description: Telomerase reverse transcriptase and telomere length regulation

source.title: Common variants at 19p13 are associated with susceptibility to ovarian cancer
source.authors: Bojesen SE, Pooley KA, Johnatty SE, et al.

AA.effect: Homozygous for the A allele (risk allele). Associated with shorter telomere length, accelerated cellular aging phenotype. May confer higher risk for age-related diseases and shortened replicative lifespan.
AA.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

AC.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate telomere length with intermediate aging trajectory.

CA.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate telomere length with intermediate aging trajectory.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the C allele (reference allele). Associated with longer telomere length, slower cellular aging phenotype, potential protection against age-related cellular senescence.
CC.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

title: TERT rs2736100 Glioma Risk (Telomerase Reverse Transcriptase Promoter)
description: Common variant in the TERT promoter region associated with glioma susceptibility, distinct from telomere length effects. TERT encodes the catalytic subunit of telomerase, and TERT promoter mutations/variants drive reactivation of telomerase expression in cancer cells, enabling replicative immortality. High-grade gliomas (glioblastoma) and oligodendrogliomas frequently harbor TERT promoter mutations (70-95%), driving cancer cell proliferation and poor prognosis.

source.title: Genome-wide association study identifies multiple glioma loci

CC.effect: baseline_glioma_risk
CC.recommendation: standard_surveillance

CT.effect: 1.21x_increased_glioma_risk
CT.recommendation: enhanced_neuroimaging_surveillance

TC.effect: 1.21x_increased_glioma_risk
TC.recommendation: enhanced_neuroimaging_surveillance

TT.effect: 1.45x_increased_glioma_risk
TT.recommendation: aggressive_surveillance_aggressive_multimodal_treatment

gene_description: Transmembrane 6 superfamily member 2 — VLDL secretion, liver fat, and NAFLD fibrosis (E167K)

source.title: Exome-wide association study identifies a TM6SF2 variant that confers susceptibility to nonalcoholic fatty liver disease
source.finding: rs58542926 (E167K) T allele associates with higher liver fat, higher ALT, and lower LDL/TG; independent NAFLD signal from PNPLA3.

CC.effect: No E167K variant. Lower TM6SF2-associated hepatic steatosis genetic signal than T carriers.
CC.recommendation: Standard NAFLD prevention: diet, activity, metabolic control.

CT.effect: One copy of E167K (T). Associated with higher liver fat and fibrosis progression risk in biopsy cohorts, partly independent of BMI.
CT.recommendation: Reinforce weight and metabolic management; correlate with imaging/LFTs clinically.

TC.effect: One copy of E167K (T). Same interpretation as CT.
TC.recommendation: Reinforce weight and metabolic management; correlate with imaging/LFTs clinically.

TT.effect: Homozygous E167K. Greater association with advanced fibrosis/cirrhosis in NAFLD cohorts vs non-carriers.
TT.recommendation: Discuss hepatology/monitoring if NAFLD is suspected or liver enzymes are abnormal.

gene_description: Transmembrane Protein 18 — widely expressed in brain tissues, particularly in hypothalamic nuclei involved in energy homeostasis and appetite regulation. TMEM18 variants are consistently associated with obesity and metabolic dysfunction across populations, and influence response to weight management interventions.

source.title: The association between polymorphisms near TMEM18 and the risk of obesity: a meta-analysis
source.finding: Meta-analysis of 53,395 obesity cases and 123,972 controls across 27 studies found rs6548238 significantly associated with obesity (OR=1.25, 95% CI: 1.08-1.45). Stronger association in Europeans (OR=1.32) and Mexicans (OR=1.39). Minor allele carriers show greater metabolic response to metformin.

CC.effect: Homozygous C-allele carriers show significantly higher BMI, waist circumference, and waist-hip ratio. Increased fasting glucose, HbA1c, triglycerides, total cholesterol, and LDL-C. Higher type 2 diabetes risk. Reduced appetite suppression and higher energy intake. Enhanced metabolic benefits from weight loss interventions.
CC.recommendation: Intensive lifestyle modification with structured dietary and exercise programs. Metformin may provide additional metabolic benefit with 60% of C-allele carriers achieving >7% weight loss versus 21.7% of T-allele homozygotes. GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide, tirzepatide) recommended for enhanced weight loss. Close monitoring of metabolic parameters (glucose, lipids, inflammation markers). Consider combination pharmacotherapy.

CT.effect: Heterozygous C-allele carriers show intermediate BMI and metabolic parameters. Mildly elevated waist circumference and glucose levels. Intermediate response to metabolic interventions.
CT.recommendation: Structured lifestyle intervention with emphasis on diet and exercise. Standard weight loss pharmacotherapy (metformin or GLP-1 RA) with monitoring. Reassess metabolic parameters at 3-6 months.

TT.phenotype: Lower obesity risk, better metabolic health
TT.effect: Wild-type homozygous T-allele carriers show lower baseline BMI, better lipid profiles, and normal glucose metabolism. Standard appetite regulation and metabolic response expected.
TT.recommendation: Standard lifestyle recommendations. Standard weight loss approaches if needed. Regular monitoring of metabolic health markers.

TC.effect: Heterozygous C-allele carriers show intermediate BMI and metabolic parameters. Mildly elevated waist circumference and glucose levels. Intermediate response to metabolic interventions.
TC.recommendation: Structured lifestyle intervention with emphasis on diet and exercise. Standard weight loss pharmacotherapy (metformin or GLP-1 RA) with monitoring. Reassess metabolic parameters at 3-6 months.

gene_description: Thiopurine (azathioprine) dosing

source.title: CPIC Guideline for Thiopurine Dosing Based on TPMT and NUDT15
source.finding: TPMT intermediate: reduce thiopurine dose to 30-80%. Poor: consider alternative or 10% dose.

CC.effect: No TPMT activity. Life-threatening myelosuppression with standard thiopurine doses.
CC.recommendation: Reduce dose to 10% of standard or use alternative. Mandatory monitoring.

CT.effect: Reduced TPMT activity. Increased risk of myelosuppression.
CT.recommendation: Reduce thiopurine dose to 30-80% of standard. Monitor CBC weekly at initiation.

TT.effect: Normal TPMT activity.
TT.recommendation: Standard thiopurine dosing with routine monitoring.

TT.effect: TPMT*3B homozygous. No TPMT activity. Life-threatening myelosuppression with standard thiopurine doses.
TT.recommendation: Reduce dose to 10% of standard or use alternative. Mandatory monitoring.

CT.effect: TPMT*3B heterozygous. Reduced TPMT activity. Increased risk of myelosuppression.
CT.recommendation: Reduce thiopurine dose to 30-80% of standard. Monitor CBC weekly at initiation.

TC.effect: TPMT*3B heterozygous. Reduced TPMT activity. Increased risk of myelosuppression.
TC.recommendation: Reduce thiopurine dose to 30-80% of standard. Monitor CBC weekly at initiation.

CC.effect: No TPMT*3B variant. Normal TPMT activity.
CC.recommendation: Standard thiopurine dosing with routine monitoring.

GG.effect: TPMT*2 homozygous. No TPMT activity. Life-threatening myelosuppression with standard thiopurine doses.
GG.recommendation: Reduce dose to 10% of standard or use alternative. Mandatory monitoring.

CG.effect: TPMT*2 heterozygous. Reduced TPMT activity. Increased risk of myelosuppression.
CG.recommendation: Reduce thiopurine dose to 30-80% of standard. Monitor CBC weekly at initiation.

GC.effect: TPMT*2 heterozygous. Reduced TPMT activity. Increased risk of myelosuppression.
GC.recommendation: Reduce thiopurine dose to 30-80% of standard. Monitor CBC weekly at initiation.

CC.effect: No TPMT*2 variant. Normal TPMT activity.
CC.recommendation: Standard thiopurine dosing with routine monitoring.

gene_description: Thyroid Peroxidase (TPO) — essential enzyme catalyzing thyroid hormone synthesis through iodination of tyrosine residues in thyroglobulin. The rs11675434 polymorphism is located near TPO and is associated with increased serum TPO antibodies, higher TSH, and lower free T4. Risk variants are central to Hashimoto's thyroiditis pathogenesis and hypothyroidism susceptibility in approximately 20-30% of the population.

source.title: Genome-wide association study of hypothyroidism in a Chinese Han population

TT.phenotype: Increased hypothyroidism and Hashimoto's thyroiditis risk
TT.effect: Homozygous T-allele carriers show significantly elevated risk for hypothyroidism (approximately 1.4-1.6x relative risk compared to CC carriers). Associated with higher serum TPO antibody concentrations (2-3 fold increase), elevated TSH levels, and reduced free T4. Strong association with Hashimoto's thyroiditis, the most common cause of hypothyroidism. TT carriers show higher prevalence of anti-TPO antibodies and develop overt hypothyroidism at younger ages and higher rates.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show modestly elevated hypothyroidism risk (approximately 20-30% increased risk relative to CC carriers). Intermediate TPO antibody levels and thyroid function parameters. Some heterozygotes develop subclinical hypothyroidism (elevated TSH with normal free T4) before progression to overt disease.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show modestly elevated hypothyroidism risk (approximately 20-30% increased risk relative to CC carriers). Intermediate TPO antibody levels and thyroid function parameters. Some heterozygotes develop subclinical hypothyroidism (elevated TSH with normal free T4) before progression to overt disease.

CC.effect: Homozygous C-allele carriers serve as reference group. Normal TPO antibody levels and thyroid function. Standard hypothyroidism prevalence without specific TPO-mediated autoimmune risk elevation.

gene_description: Transient receptor potential melastatin 6 (TRPM6) — rate-limiting ion channel for active transcellular magnesium absorption in intestine and reabsorption in kidney

source.title: TRPM6 and TRPM7--Gatekeepers of human magnesium metabolism
source.finding: rs11144134 T allele (found in 92% of Americans) increases hypomagnesemia risk by ~20%. TRPM6 is critical for intestinal absorption and renal reabsorption; variants reduce bioavailable magnesium.

TT.effect: Homozygous for T allele. Decreased TRPM6 channel function and magnesium absorption efficiency. Approximately 20% increased risk of hypomagnesemia. Reduced intestinal absorption and renal magnesium conservation. Loss of magnesium in urine and feces increases.
TT.recommendation: Increase dietary magnesium intake (320 mg/day for women, 420 mg/day for men is standard; consider 400-500 mg/day). Avoid diuretics, alcohol, and foods/medications that impair Mg absorption. Consider magnesium supplementation (glycinate, citrate, or malate forms better absorbed). Monitor magnesium levels with healthcare provider.

CT.effect: Heterozygous for T/C alleles. Intermediate TRPM6 function and magnesium absorption capacity. Minimal increased hypomagnesemia risk compared to CC genotype. Generally adequate absorption with normal dietary intake.
CT.recommendation: Standard magnesium intake (320-420 mg/day) usually sufficient. Maintain adequate dietary sources. Avoid chronic alcohol and diuretic use.

TC.effect: Heterozygous for T/C alleles. Intermediate TRPM6 function and magnesium absorption capacity. Minimal increased hypomagnesemia risk compared to CC genotype. Generally adequate absorption with normal dietary intake.
TC.recommendation: Standard magnesium intake (320-420 mg/day) usually sufficient. Maintain adequate dietary sources. Avoid chronic alcohol and diuretic use.

CC.effect: Homozygous for C allele (wild-type). Normal TRPM6 channel function and magnesium absorption efficiency. Adequate active magnesium absorption in gut and renal reabsorption. No significant increased hypomagnesemia risk.
CC.recommendation: Standard dietary magnesium intake (320-420 mg/day) is adequate. Maintain diverse diet with magnesium-rich foods (nuts, seeds, spinach, whole grains, legumes).

gene_description: Transient Receptor Potential Cation Channel Subfamily M Member 8 — a cold-sensitive cation channel expressed in sensory neurons that detects cold temperatures and modulates pain signaling. Variants affect calcium influx, neuronal excitability, and migraine susceptibility through altered nociceptor sensitivity and trigeminal neuron function.

source.title: Genome-wide association of migraine implicates a common susceptibility variant on chromosome 8q22.1
source.finding: TRPM8 rs10166942-T variant associated with migraine susceptibility (p = 2.6 × 10⁻¹³) in genome-wide association meta-analysis of 6,000 migraine cases and 9,500 controls. T allele increases migraine risk through altered cold-sensing and pain modulation pathways. Linkage disequilibrium analysis suggests TRPM8 as causal locus in 8q22.1 region.

TT.phenotype: TRPM8 homozygous risk — elevated migraine susceptibility
TT.effect: Homozygous for T risk allele. ~1.3-fold increased migraine risk per GWAS meta-analysis. Altered TRPM8 channel function and calcium signaling in trigeminal sensory neurons. Enhanced nociceptor sensitivity and cold-pain perception. Increased migraine threshold activation and more frequent migraine attacks. Impaired normal pain modulation through cold-sensing pathways.
TT.recommendation: Consider migraine preventive therapy: topiramate (blocks sodium channels and GABA function), propranolol, or amitriptyline. CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab) particularly effective for TRPM8-linked migraine. Acute treatment: triptans (sumatriptan, rizatriptan) during attacks. Identify and avoid triggers (cold exposure, menstrual cycle, stress, certain foods). Maintain regular sleep and meal schedules. Biofeedback and relaxation techniques. Consider genetic counseling if strong family history.

TC.effect: Heterozygous for T risk allele. Modest increase in migraine susceptibility from this locus. Intermediate TRPM8 channel activity and nociceptor sensitivity. Increased likelihood of migraines but lower penetrance than homozygotes. Environmental and other genetic factors play larger role in phenotype expression.
TC.recommendation: Lifestyle measures: trigger identification and avoidance, stress management, regular sleep, adequate hydration, exercise. Acute migraine treatment as needed (triptans). If frequent migraines (>4 per month), consider preventive medication after discussion with neurologist. Monitor for patterns to identify specific triggers.

CT.effect: Heterozygous for T risk allele. Modest increase in migraine susceptibility from this locus. Intermediate TRPM8 channel activity and nociceptor sensitivity. Increased likelihood of migraines but lower penetrance than homozygotes. Environmental and other genetic factors play larger role in phenotype expression.
CT.recommendation: Lifestyle measures: trigger identification and avoidance, stress management, regular sleep, adequate hydration, exercise. Acute migraine treatment as needed (triptans). If frequent migraines (>4 per month), consider preventive medication after discussion with neurologist. Monitor for patterns to identify specific triggers.

CC.phenotype: TRPM8 homozygous wild-type — standard migraine risk
CC.effect: Homozygous for C reference allele. Normal TRPM8 channel function. Standard migraine risk from this locus. Migraine susceptibility determined primarily by environmental factors, other genetic loci, and neural predisposition. Normal cold-pain sensing and nociceptor function.
CC.recommendation: Standard migraine management if applicable: trigger avoidance, stress management, regular sleep. Acute treatment with triptans if migraines occur. No specific pharmacogenomic guidance from this variant.

gene_description: Transient Receptor Potential Cation Channel Subfamily V Member 1 — pain and temperature sensing ion channel activated by vanilloids and heat; Ile585Val variant (rs8065080) modulates pain perception and heat sensitivity

source.title: The Ile585Val TRPV1 variant is involved in risk of painful knee osteoarthritis
source.finding: TRPV1 rs8065080 (Ile585Val) variant affects thermal pain sensitivity and nociception. The GG genotype (Val-Val, encoded by T at rs8065080) is associated with reduced pain sensitivity, while AA and AG genotypes show increased thermal pain perception and altered response to pain therapeutics.

TT.phenotype: Altered pain threshold — reduced thermal pain sensitivity
TT.effect: Homozygous for the Val585 allele (TT genotype). Associated with lower thermal pain sensitivity and reduced capsaicin-induced pain perception. May have diminished awareness of painful stimuli, which could be protective in acute pain situations but may delay detection of serious underlying pathology (myocardial infarction, appendicitis). Modified response to nociceptive drugs.
TT.recommendation: Patient education about potential delayed pain recognition — encourage medical evaluation for non-pain symptoms of serious conditions (fatigue, nausea, dyspnea). Standard analgesic dosing, though efficacy may vary. Monitor for medication overuse due to altered pain feedback. Consider physical therapy and non-pharmacological approaches to manage chronic pain. Regular screening for asymptomatic conditions.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers (Ile/Val) show intermediate pain perception. Normal to slightly reduced thermal pain threshold. Standard nociceptive response expected.
TC.recommendation: Standard analgesic management and dosing. Routine pain assessment and monitoring.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers (Ile/Val) show intermediate pain perception. Normal to slightly reduced thermal pain threshold. Standard nociceptive response expected.
CT.recommendation: Standard analgesic management and dosing. Routine pain assessment and monitoring.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the Ile585 allele (CC genotype). Normal TRPV1 function with typical thermal pain perception and capsaicin sensitivity. Standard nociceptive response to pain stimuli and analgesics.
CC.recommendation: Standard pain management and analgesic therapy per clinical guidelines. Routine pain assessment.

source.title: TSHR Gene (rs179247) Polymorphism and Susceptibility to Autoimmune Thyroid Disease: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis

description: TSHR rs179247 is a functional intronic polymorphism associated with increased risk of Graves disease and autoimmune thyroiditis. The A allele confers greater risk than the G allele.

GG.effect: Reference homozygote; protective genotype
GG.recommendation: Standard thyroid health maintenance; routine screening only if symptoms develop

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier; modest increased risk
GA.recommendation: Baseline TSH screening; monitor for thyroid symptoms (fatigue, tremor, heat intolerance); consider periodic TSH monitoring

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier; modest increased risk
AG.recommendation: Baseline TSH screening; monitor for thyroid symptoms (fatigue, tremor, heat intolerance); consider periodic TSH monitoring

AA.phenotype: Elevated Graves disease and autoimmune thyroid disease risk
AA.effect: Risk allele homozygote; substantially increased risk for autoimmune thyroiditis
AA.recommendation: Baseline TSH and thyroid antibodies (TPO, thyroglobulin); annual thyroid function screening; symptom monitoring; ophthalmology evaluation if eye symptoms develop; specialist endocrinology if disease manifests

source.title: Truncations of Titin Causing Dilated Cardiomyopathy

description: TTN (Titin) truncating variants are the most common genetic cause of heritable dilated cardiomyopathy, accounting for up to 25% of familial cases. TTN is a critical sarcomeric protein essential for myocardial function.

GG.effect: Reference homozygote
GG.recommendation: Standard cardiac health maintenance; family screening based on history

GA.effect: Carrier for TTN variation; risk profile depends on specific variant type
GA.recommendation: Baseline echocardiogram; periodic cardiac monitoring; genetic counseling; family screening

AG.effect: Carrier for TTN variation; risk profile depends on specific variant type
AG.recommendation: Baseline echocardiogram; periodic cardiac monitoring; genetic counseling; family screening

AA.effect: Homozygous or compound heterozygous for pathogenic TTN variants
AA.recommendation: Comprehensive cardiac evaluation including echocardiogram and genetic testing; specialist cardiology care; family member screening; device therapy consideration

source.title: Variant of TYR and Autoimmunity Susceptibility Loci in Generalized Vitiligo

description: TYR rs1126809 (R402Q) encodes a thermolabile tyrosinase with reduced catalytic activity at body temperature. The variant is associated with vitiligo risk through altered melanocyte-HLA antigen presentation.

GG.effect: Reference homozygote for normal tyrosinase
GG.recommendation: Standard dermatologic health; sun protection as baseline

GA.effect: Heterozygous carrier for R402Q variant
GA.recommendation: Baseline dermatologic evaluation; monitor for depigmented patches; sun protection to affected areas; consider early treatment if vitiligo develops

AG.effect: Heterozygous carrier for R402Q variant
AG.recommendation: Baseline dermatologic evaluation; monitor for depigmented patches; sun protection to affected areas; consider early treatment if vitiligo develops

AA.effect: Homozygous for R402Q thermolabile tyrosinase; reduced enzyme activity at 37°C
AA.recommendation: Regular dermatologic monitoring; early intervention if depigmented patches develop; topical corticosteroids or calcineurin inhibitors for treatment; phototherapy consideration; cosmetic camouflage for psychological support

gene_description: UDP-glucuronosyltransferase Family 1 Member A1 — catalyzes the glucuronidation and elimination of bilirubin and many drugs. The rs8175347 TA repeat polymorphism in the TATA box promoter regulates enzyme expression and activity.

source.title: Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium (CPIC) Guideline for UGT1A1 and Irinotecan Prescribing
source.finding: UGT1A1*28 (TA7 repeats from rs8175347) reduces enzyme activity to approximately 30% of normal, increasing risk of severe irinotecan toxicity (neutropenia, diarrhea) and bilirubin elevation with atazanavir.

TA6/TA6.effect: Wild-type genotype with normal UGT1A1 expression and glucuronidation capacity. Normal bilirubin metabolism and drug elimination. Normal bilirubin levels (typically <1.2 mg/dL).
TA6/TA6.recommendation: Standard irinotecan dosing per protocol. Standard atazanavir dosing appropriate. No dose adjustments needed. Routine monitoring of liver function and bilirubin.

TA6/TA7.effect: Heterozygous carriers with intermediate UGT1A1 expression (approximately 70% of normal activity). Slightly elevated bilirubin levels (often 1.2-3.0 mg/dL without clinical jaundice). Moderate risk for irinotecan dose-limiting toxicity (severe diarrhea, neutropenia).
TA6/TA7.recommendation: Irinotecan: Use standard dosing but with close toxicity monitoring. Consider 10-15% dose reduction if dose-limiting toxicity observed. Atazanavir: Standard dosing, monitor for hyperbilirubinemia and jaundice. If bilirubin elevates, may require alternative protease inhibitor.

TA7/TA7.effect: Homozygous TA7 carriers (UGT1A1*28/*28) with significantly reduced enzyme activity (approximately 30% of normal). Chronic unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (typically 2-5 mg/dL) without hemolysis or hepatic dysfunction. High risk for severe irinotecan toxicity and atazanavir-related hyperbilirubinemia with jaundice.
TA7/TA7.recommendation: Irinotecan: FDA recommends 25-30% dose reduction from standard protocol starting dose. Monitor CBC and diarrhea closely during first cycle. Atazanavir: Use with caution or consider alternative protease inhibitor. If used, baseline and regular bilirubin monitoring mandatory. Patient counseling on expected benign mild jaundice. Avoid concurrent use with irinotecan.

gene_description: UDP-glucuronosyltransferase 1-4 (UGT1A4) — Phase II enzyme responsible for glucuronidation of lamotrigine and other xenobiotics. The UGT1A4*3 variant (Leu48Val) has increased enzymatic activity in vitro but shows conflicting clinical effects on lamotrigine metabolism and seizure control.

source.title: Frequencies of UGT1A4*2 (P24T) and *3 (L48V) and their effects on serum concentrations of lamotrigine
source.journal: European Journal of Drug Metabolism and Pharmacokinetics
source.finding: UGT1A4*3 carriers (G allele at rs2011425) show reduced lamotrigine serum concentrations and reduced therapeutic efficacy in some studies but no clinical consequence in others. Meta-analysis of 413 patients showed carriers of G allele (TG+GG genotypes) had significantly reduced lamotrigine concentrations vs TT wild-type. However, some recent studies (2023) show no relevant clinical consequence for lamotrigine troughs in adult epilepsy patients.

snps.note: c.142T>G SNP resulting in Leu48Val (L48V) amino acid change in UGT1A4*3 variant

GG.effect: Homozygous carriers of the Val48 variant may have enhanced glucuronidation capacity for lamotrigine in vitro. Clinical studies show mixed results: some show significantly lower lamotrigine serum concentrations and higher dose requirements, while others (particularly 2023 data) show no clinically relevant differences. G-allele carriers showed reduced lamotrigine serum concentrations in meta-analysis of 3 studies (n=413), with lower therapeutic efficacy reported vs wild-type TT.
GG.recommendation: Lamotrigine therapy can be used with standard dosing, but therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) is recommended to ensure adequate serum concentrations (target 4-20 mcg/mL). If GG genotype is identified and lamotrigine levels are subtherapeutic despite standard dosing, consider modest dose increase. Monitor seizure control closely during titration.

TG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate glucuronidation activity. Some studies report modestly reduced lamotrigine serum concentrations compared to TT wild-type (approximately 15-25% lower), while recent large studies show no clinically relevant consequence. The effect appears population and context-dependent.
TG.recommendation: Standard lamotrigine dosing is generally appropriate. Therapeutic drug monitoring may be considered if seizure control is suboptimal or if patient is on concomitant enzyme-inducing or inhibiting medications. Adjust dose based on clinical response and TDM levels if available.

GT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate glucuronidation activity. Some studies report modestly reduced lamotrigine serum concentrations compared to TT wild-type (approximately 15-25% lower), while recent large studies show no clinically relevant consequence. The effect appears population and context-dependent.
GT.recommendation: Standard lamotrigine dosing is generally appropriate. Therapeutic drug monitoring may be considered if seizure control is suboptimal or if patient is on concomitant enzyme-inducing or inhibiting medications. Adjust dose based on clinical response and TDM levels if available.

TT.effect: Wild-type UGT1A4 with normal glucuronidation capacity. Standard lamotrigine serum concentrations and therapeutic efficacy expected with standard dosing regimens.
TT.recommendation: Standard lamotrigine dosing per prescribing guidelines is appropriate. No UGT1A4-related dose adjustments needed. Monitor seizure control and adjust dose based on clinical response.

gene_description: UDP-glucuronosyltransferase Family 1 Member A9 — the primary hepatic enzyme responsible for glucuronidation and elimination of mycophenolic acid (MPA), the active metabolite of mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) used in organ transplant recipients

source.title: The main role of UGT1A9 in the hepatic metabolism of mycophenolic acid and the effects of naturally occurring variants
source.finding: UGT1A9 polymorphisms (rs72551330, rs2741049) significantly affect mycophenolic acid pharmacokinetics in transplant patients. Genetic variation contributes to inter-individual variability in MPA exposure and clinical outcomes.

CC.effect: Wild-type genotype with normal mycophenolic acid glucuronidation and elimination. Expected MPA area under curve (AUC) and half-life values consistent with clinical pharmacokinetic targets.
CC.recommendation: Standard mycophenolate mofetil dosing of 1000 mg twice daily. Monitor MPA trough levels (therapeutic range typically 1-3.5 mg/h/mL). Standard immunosuppressive efficacy expected.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carriers show moderately reduced UGT1A9 expression and MPA glucuronidation capacity. Elevated MPA exposure (increased AUC approximately 15-25%) with moderately prolonged elimination half-life. Increased risk of dose-dependent mycophenolate toxicity (GI side effects, hematologic effects).
CT.recommendation: Standard mycophenolate dosing with therapeutic drug monitoring. Measure MPA trough levels at steady state (days 7-14). If levels exceed target (>3.5 mg/h/mL), consider dose reduction by 250-500 mg. Monitor complete blood count for myelosuppression.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carriers show moderately reduced UGT1A9 expression and MPA glucuronidation capacity. Elevated MPA exposure (increased AUC approximately 15-25%) with moderately prolonged elimination half-life. Increased risk of dose-dependent mycophenolate toxicity (GI side effects, hematologic effects).
TC.recommendation: Standard mycophenolate dosing with therapeutic drug monitoring. Measure MPA trough levels at steady state (days 7-14). If levels exceed target (>3.5 mg/h/mL), consider dose reduction by 250-500 mg. Monitor complete blood count for myelosuppression.

TT.effect: Homozygous carriers with significantly impaired mycophenolic acid glucuronidation (30-50% reduced activity). Markedly elevated MPA plasma exposure with extended half-life. High risk for mycophenolate toxicity including severe GI side effects (diarrhea, nausea, vomiting), myelosuppression (anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia), and increased infection risk.
TT.recommendation: Consider mycophenolate dose reduction (750 mg or 500 mg twice daily initial dose). Mandatory therapeutic drug monitoring with target MPA trough 1.5-3.0 mg/h/mL (lower end of range). Frequent CBC monitoring for bone marrow suppression. Prophylactic antimicrobial coverage consideration. Switch to alternative immunosuppressant if MPA toxicity develops despite dose adjustment.

GG.effect: Wild-type genotype with normal mycophenolic acid metabolism. Expected MPA pharmacokinetic profile with standard mycophenolate dosing.
GG.recommendation: Standard mycophenolate mofetil dosing. Therapeutic drug monitoring with target MPA trough 1-3.5 mg/h/mL.

GA.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate UGT1A9 function with modest increase in MPA exposure. Slightly elevated trough levels may occur.
GA.recommendation: Standard mycophenolate dosing with therapeutic drug monitoring. Adjust dose if MPA levels exceed 3.5 mg/h/mL.

AG.effect: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate UGT1A9 function with modest increase in MPA exposure. Slightly elevated trough levels may occur.
AG.recommendation: Standard mycophenolate dosing with therapeutic drug monitoring. Adjust dose if MPA levels exceed 3.5 mg/h/mL.

AA.effect: Homozygous carriers with reduced UGT1A9-mediated MPA glucuronidation. Significantly elevated mycophenolic acid exposure with increased toxicity risk.
AA.recommendation: Consider mycophenolate dose reduction. Mandatory therapeutic drug monitoring targeting lower MPA levels (1.5-3.0 mg/h/mL). Close monitoring for mycophenolate toxicity.

gene_description: Vitamin D receptor function

source.title: Vitamin D receptor gene polymorphisms and cancer risk
source.finding: VDR FokI (rs2228570) T allele associated with altered vitamin D signaling and cancer risk.

TT.effect: Lower vitamin D receptor activity. Reduced calcium absorption and immune modulation. Associated with increased cancer risk.
TT.recommendation: Ensure adequate vitamin D levels (target 40-60 ng/mL). Higher supplementation doses may be needed. Regular level monitoring.

CT.effect: Moderately reduced vitamin D receptor activity.
CT.recommendation: Maintain vitamin D levels above 30 ng/mL. Consider supplementation during winter months.

CC.effect: Normal vitamin D receptor activity and signaling.
CC.recommendation: Standard vitamin D intake sufficient.

AA.effect: Associated with lower bone mineral density and altered vitamin D metabolism.
AA.recommendation: Monitor bone density. Ensure vitamin D and calcium sufficiency.

AG.effect: Intermediate VDR expression variant.
AG.recommendation: Standard monitoring.

GG.effect: Normal VDR expression.
GG.recommendation: Standard diet and supplementation.

AA.effect: Associated with altered vitamin D signaling efficiency.
AA.recommendation: Monitor vitamin D levels. May need higher supplementation.

CC.effect: Normal vitamin D signaling.
CC.recommendation: Standard diet.

title: VHL rs1642742 Renal Cell Carcinoma Risk (Von Hippel-Lindau Tumor Suppressor)
description: Tag SNP near the VHL gene locus associated with sporadic renal cell carcinoma risk. VHL encodes a tumor suppressor protein that regulates hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF) degradation. Loss of VHL function leads to accumulation of HIF, driving angiogenesis and tumor growth in renal epithelial cells.

source.title: Renal cell carcinoma association study with multiple susceptibility loci

CC.effect: baseline_renal_cancer_risk
CC.recommendation: standard_surveillance

CT.effect: 1.15x_increased_renal_cancer_risk
CT.recommendation: enhanced_imaging_surveillance

TC.effect: 1.15x_increased_renal_cancer_risk
TC.recommendation: enhanced_imaging_surveillance

TT.effect: 1.35x_increased_renal_cancer_risk
TT.recommendation: aggressive_surveillance_tyrosine_kinase_inhibitor_awareness

gene_description: Warfarin dose sensitivity

source.title: CPIC Guideline for Pharmacogenetics-Guided Warfarin Dosing
source.finding: VKORC1 -1639G>A: AA genotype requires ~50% lower warfarin dose.

AA.effect: Requires ~50% lower warfarin dose. High bleeding risk at standard doses.
AA.recommendation: Start warfarin at significantly reduced dose. Use pharmacogenomic dosing algorithm.

AG.effect: Requires ~25% lower warfarin dose.
AG.recommendation: Reduce initial warfarin dose. Monitor INR closely.

GG.effect: Standard warfarin sensitivity.
GG.recommendation: Standard warfarin dosing with routine INR monitoring.

description: WWC1 protein expression and episodic memory performance

source.title: Common Kibra Alleles Are Associated with Human Memory Performance
source.authors: Papassotiropoulos A, Stephan DA, Huentelman MJ, et al.

CC.effect: Homozygous for the C allele (risk allele). Associated with lower episodic memory performance, reduced hippocampal activation during memory retrieval tasks.
CC.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

CT.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate memory performance with moderate hippocampal activation.
CT.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

TC.effect: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate memory performance with moderate hippocampal activation.
TC.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.

TT.effect: Homozygous for the T allele (reference/favorable allele). Associated with enhanced episodic memory performance, higher hippocampal activation during memory retrieval tasks.
TT.recommendation: Consult clinical guidelines.
